topics.title: Study of Taps, Valves, Pipe Fittings, Gauges, Pitot Tubes, Water Meters and Current Meters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCEL308 - FLUID MECHANICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Study of Taps, Valves, Pipe Fittings, Gauges, Pitot Tubes, Water Meters and Current Meters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# MODULE EXPERIMENT 2 — STUDY OF FLUID MECHANICAL INSTRUMENTS

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

This experiment forms the **identification and principle-of-operation foundation** of Fluid Mechanics laboratory work. The student is expected to physically inspect, sketch, and explain the working of the standard accessories used in any hydraulic/fluid-flow test rig.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Intent):**
> "Study of taps, valves, pipe fittings, gauges, Pitot tubes, water meters and current meters — *identification, components, working principle and area of application*."

The experiment is **non-computational (qualitative)** in terms of deriving new readings, but it is **heavily viva-based**. KTU examiners ask: *"Name the instrument, draw a neat sketch, state the principle, write the working formula, and give one practical application."*

### 1.1.1 Conceptual Analogy — The "Plumbing Toolkit" Intuition

Imagine the human **circulatory system** as a giant pipeline:
- Your **heart valves** (mitral, aortic) behave exactly like **check valves / non-return valves** — they allow flow in only one direction.
- The **arteries branch into capillaries** → this is analogous to a **Tee or Cross pipe fitting** (a pipe splitting into two).
- A **stethoscope** the doctor presses on your chest picks up pressure pulses → it is functionally identical to a **piezometer / pressure tap** that senses static pressure in a pipe.
- A **speedometer in a car** tells you the speed of the vehicle moving in a stream of traffic → this is what a **current meter** does for a stream of water flowing in a river.

So, in short, every instrument studied in this experiment has a *direct counterpart* in either biology, automobiles, or household plumbing. The lab simply gives these everyday items their formal engineering name.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Metrics Used Throughout This Module:**
> - Standard atmospheric pressure: **P_atm = 101.325 kPa = 10.197 m of water = 760 mm of Hg**
> - Density of water at 4 °C: **ρ_water = 1000 kg/m³**
> - Specific weight of water: **γ = 9810 N/m³**
> - Acceleration due to gravity: **g = 9.81 m/s²**
> - Kinematic viscosity of water at 20 °C: **ν = 1.003 × 10⁻⁶ m²/s**

---

### 1.2 Classification Overview (Master Map)

The seven families of instruments studied in this experiment can be grouped under **three functional roles** in a fluid system:

| Functional Role | Purpose | Instruments Covered |
|---|---|---|
| **Control** | Start, stop, regulate, or reverse flow | Taps, Valves |
| **Conduction** | Carry, join, or redirect the fluid | Pipe Fittings |
| **Measurement** | Quantify pressure, velocity, or discharge | Gauges, Pitot Tubes, Water Meters, Current Meters |

This classification will be the spine of every viva question KTU asks. Memorise it.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2 — DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 TAPS (Pressure Taps)

A **tap** is a small opening on the wall of a pipe/conduit through which a pressure-measuring device (manometer, gauge) is connected.

### 2.1.1 Types of Pressure Taps

1. **Piezometer Tap** — A simple vertical transparent tube (glass/acrylic) connected tangentially to the pipe. It directly indicates the **static pressure head (p/γ)** as the height to which water rises in the tube. Cannot measure negative (suction) pressure.

2. **Pressure Tapping (Corporation Cock)** — A small hole drilled perpendicular to the pipe wall, fitted with a valve and nipple. Used to connect a Bourdon gauge or a U-tube manometer. Standard tap diameter = **6 mm to 12 mm**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Condition (ASME / ISO Standard):**
> A pressure tap must be located at least **10 pipe diameters downstream** and **2 pipe diameters upstream** of any fitting to avoid zones of separated/disturbed flow.

---

## 2.2 VALVES

A **valve** is a mechanical device that controls the flow of fluid by opening, closing, or partially obstructing the passage. Valves are classified by the **motion of the obturating element** and by **function**.

### 2.2.1 Classification by Motion

| Type | Motion of Disc/Gate | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Gate Valve** | Wedge moves perpendicular to flow | Fully open or fully closed; minimum friction loss |
| **Globe Valve** | Disc moves parallel to flow, against seat | Throttling/flow regulation |
| **Ball Valve** | Spherical ball with bore rotates 90° | Quick shut-off, low torque |
| **Butterfly Valve** | Disc rotates about a shaft | Large diameter pipes, moderate throttling |
| **Needle Valve** | Tapered needle (long, slender) moves axially | Fine flow control, instrument lines |
| **Check (NRV) Valve** | Disc swings/lifts with flow | Prevents backflow |
| **Diaphragm Valve** | Flexible diaphragm pushed against weir | Corrosive/sterile fluids |
| **Sluice Valve** | Sluice gate slides vertically | Municipal water mains |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Viva Favourite:** *"Which valve offers the least resistance when fully open?"*
> **Answer:** The **Gate Valve** (straight-through, unobstructed bore) — hence used as an isolation valve, not a control valve.

### 2.2.2 Functional Classification (for clarity)

- **Isolation Valves** — Gate, Ball, Plug (used ON/OFF)
- **Regulation Valves** — Globe, Needle, Butterfly (used for throttling)
- **Safety / Check Valves** — NRV, Pressure Relief Valve

---

## 2.3 PIPE FITTINGS

A **pipe fitting** is a component used to connect, redirect, terminate, or change the size of pipes.

### 2.3.1 Standard Pipe Fittings — Identification Table

| Fitting | Sketch (verbal) | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Elbow (90° / 45°)** | L-shaped | Change direction |
| **Tee (Equal / Unequal)** | T-shaped | Branch the flow into two |
| **Cross** | +-shaped | Four-way intersection |
| **Reducer (Concentric / Eccentric)** | Truncated cone | Connect pipes of different diameters |
| **Coupling** | Sleeve with threads | Join two pipes in a straight line |
| **Union** | Three-piece nut | Detachable joint (for maintenance) |
| **Flange** | Disc with bolt holes | Bolted connection to equipment |
| **Nipple** | Short pipe with male threads on both ends | Connect two fittings |
| **Bend** | Long-radius curved pipe | Direction change with lower head loss than elbow |
| **Cap / Plug** | End closure | Terminate a pipe |
| **Strainer / Foot Valve** | Perforated disc at pipe end | Prevent debris entry; retain prime in pumps |
| **Expansion Joint** | Corrugated/ bellow | Absorb thermal expansion |

> [!NOTE]
> **Head Loss Reference:** A standard 90° elbow causes a head loss roughly equal to **30 times the velocity head (V²/2g)** in equivalent length, while a 45° elbow causes only about **16 V²/2g**. Long-radius bends are preferred over sharp elbows for the same reason.

---

## 2.4 GAUGES (Pressure & Vacuum)

A **gauge** measures the **pressure of a fluid** relative to atmospheric pressure or relative to another reference.

### 2.4.1 Classification of Pressure-Measuring Gauges

1. **Manometers (Fluid-Column Type)**
   - **Piezometer** — measures gauge pressure directly in metres of fluid.
   - **U-Tube Manometer** — measures differential pressure using mercury (for water) or lighter fluid (for gases).
   - **Inverted U-Tube Manometer** — used for measuring pressure drop across a section with air as manometric fluid.
   - **Differential Manometer** — measures the pressure difference between two points.

2. **Mechanical / Elastic Gauges**
   - **Bourdon Tube Gauge** (most common industrial gauge) — a curved metallic tube straightens slightly under pressure; this motion is geared to a pointer.
     - Types: C-type, helical, spiral.
   - **Diaphragm Gauge** — a corrugated metal/flexible diaphragm deflects under pressure.
   - **Bellows Gauge** — uses an axially compressible bellows.
   - **Dead-Weight Piston Gauge** — primary standard; balances pressure with calibrated weights on a piston.

3. **Electronic Gauges**
   - **Strain-Gauge Type** — piezoresistive sensor outputs voltage proportional to pressure.
   - **Capacitive / Piezoelectric** — for dynamic/high-frequency pressure.

### 2.4.2 Bourdon Tube — The Most Asked Instrument

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Working Principle:** A curved tube of **non-circular (elliptical/flattened) cross-section** tends to straighten when internal pressure is applied because the outer fibre length exceeds the inner fibre length. The tip displacement is mechanically amplified through a **link → lever → rack-and-pinion → pointer** arrangement.

The deflection `e` of a C-type Bourdon tube is given by:

$$
e \;=\; \dfrac{P\,R^{2}\,\left(1 - \dfrac{b^{2}}{a^{2}}\right)}{E\,t\,\left[\alpha + \beta\,\dfrac{b^{2}}{a^{2}} + \gamma\,\dfrac{a^{2}}{b^{2}}\right]}
$$

where `a` = major axis, `b` = minor axis, `R` = radius of curvature, `t` = wall thickness, `E` = Young's modulus, and `α, β, γ` are dimensionless constants of the cross-section.

---

## 2.5 PITOT TUBES

A **Pitot tube** is a device used to measure the **local velocity of flow** at a point in a stream by comparing the **stagnation pressure** (at the tube mouth facing the flow) with the **static pressure** (at a side hole perpendicular to the flow).

### 2.5.1 Types of Pitot Tubes

| Type | Description |
|---|---|
| **Simple Pitot Tube** | Single L-shaped tube; measures stagnation head only. Needs a separate static tapping. |
| **Pitot-Static Tube** (Prandtl Tube) | Two concentric tubes — the inner measures stagnation pressure; the outer has side holes measuring static pressure. |
| **Piezo-Pitot Tube** | Combined pressure measurement for supersonic flow. |
| **Combined Pitot-Static Tube** | Standard form used in labs (e.g., in Venturi/flume setups). |

### 2.5.2 Derivation of Pitot Tube Equation (Velocity Form)

Consider a streamline approaching a Pitot tube. The flow stagnates at the tube mouth. Applying **Bernoulli's equation** between point (1) in the free stream and point (2) at the stagnation point (where V₂ = 0):

$$
\dfrac{p_1}{\gamma} + \dfrac{V_1^{2}}{2g} + z_1 \;=\; \dfrac{p_2}{\gamma} + \dfrac{V_2^{2}}{2g} + z_2
$$

If the two points are at the same elevation (z₁ = z₂) and V₂ = 0:

$$
\dfrac{p_1}{\gamma} + \dfrac{V_1^{2}}{2g} \;=\; \dfrac{p_2}{\gamma}
$$

Rearranging for V₁:

$$
V_1 \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{2g\,(p_2 - p_1)}{\gamma}} \;=\; \sqrt{2g\,h}
$$

where `h = (p₂ − p₁)/γ` is the **stagnation head difference** (the rise in the piezometric column of a differential manometer).

In actual practice, the reading includes a **Pitot coefficient C_v** (typically **0.95 to 0.99** for a well-designed Prandtl tube):

$$
V_{\text{actual}} \;=\; C_v \sqrt{2g\,h}
$$

---

## 2.6 WATER METERS

A **water meter** is an instrument that continuously measures the **volume (or rate)** of water flowing through a pipe. They are of two broad classes: **positive displacement** and **velocity (inferential) meters**.

### 2.6.1 Classification Table

| Type | Working Principle | Example | Accuracy |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Positive Displacement (PD)** | Measures actual volume by filling/emptying chambers of known volume. | **Nutating disc, Oscillating piston, Rotary vane** | High (±2%) |
| **Velocity / Inferential** | Measures flow velocity; volume is inferred by multiplying with pipe area. | **Venturi, Orifice, Turbine, Multi-jet, Magnetic, Ultrasonic** | Moderate (±3–5%) |
| **Combination** | Auto-switches between a small and large meter. | Domestic + bulk | High |

### 2.6.2 Standard Discharge Formula (for Venturi / Orifice / Nozzle meters)

For a **Venturi meter**:

$$
Q \;=\; C_{d}\,A_{2}\,\sqrt{\dfrac{2g\,(h_1 - h_2)}{1 - \left(\dfrac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^{2}}}
$$

For an **Orifice meter**:

$$
Q \;=\; C_{d}\,A_{2}\,\sqrt{\dfrac{2g\,H}{1 - \left(\dfrac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^{2}}}
$$

where `A₁` = upstream pipe area, `A₂` = throat/orifice area, `H` = differential manometer head, and `C_d` is the **coefficient of discharge**.

For a **rotameter (variable-area meter)** — read directly using calibration chart of float position.

---

## 2.7 CURRENT METERS

A **current meter** is a hydrological instrument used to measure the **velocity of water in open channels, rivers, and streams**. It is the open-channel counterpart of a Pitot-static tube.

### 2.7.1 Types of Current Meters

| Type | Construction | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Cup (Price) Type** | 6 conical cups mounted on a vertical spindle; horizontal axis rotation. | Standard river gauging. Less sensitive to oblique flow. |
| **Propeller (Screw) Type** | Helical blade on a horizontal axis. | Sensitive to flow direction; used in clean water. |
| **Electromagnetic (EM) Meter** | Uses Faraday's law; no moving parts. | Bidirectional, no fouling, used in estuaries. |
| **Acoustic Doppler (ADV / ADCP)** | Doppler shift of acoustic pulses. | Modern 3D velocity field measurement. |

### 2.7.2 The Current-Meter Rating Equation

A current meter must be **calibrated** by towing it through still water at known speeds. The relationship between the **rotations per second (N)** and the **flow velocity (V)** is linear:

$$
V \;=\; a\,N + b
$$

where:
- `a` = slope of the rating curve (metres per revolution, or "metre constant")
- `b` = intercept (the "hydraulic zero", usually slightly negative because the cups need a small current to start rotating)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Lab Favourite Question:** *"A current meter rated a = 0.65 m/rev and b = 0.02 m/s makes 150 revolutions in 60 seconds in a river. Find the velocity."*
> **Solution:** N = 150/60 = 2.5 rev/s → V = 0.65 × 2.5 + 0.02 = **1.645 m/s**

---

## 2.8 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET (Cheat-Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Formula Table — Memorise Before Every Lab Test**

| # | Instrument / Concept | Governing Equation | Typical C_d or Coefficient |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Pitot tube velocity | $V = C_v \sqrt{2gh}$ | $C_v = 0.95$ to $0.99$ |
| 2 | Venturi meter discharge | $Q = C_d A_2 \sqrt{\dfrac{2g \Delta h}{1 - (A_2/A_1)^2}}$ | $C_d = 0.95$ to $0.98$ |
| 3 | Orifice meter discharge | $Q = C_d A_2 \sqrt{\dfrac{2gH}{1 - (A_2/A_1)^2}}$ | $C_d = 0.60$ to $0.65$ |
| 4 | Rotameter (calibration) | $Q = f(\text{float height})$ | Read from chart |
| 5 | Current meter velocity | $V = a N + b$ | Calibrated |
| 6 | Bourdon tube deflection | $e = f(P, R, a, b, t, E)$ | N/A |
| 7 | U-tube manometer pressure | $p = (\rho_m - \rho_f) g h$ | N/A |
| 8 | Darcy–Weisbach head loss | $h_f = \dfrac{f L V^2}{2 g D}$ | $f$ from Moody chart |
| 9 | Chezy's equation (open channel) | $V = C \sqrt{m i}$ | Chezy's C |
| 10 | Manning's equation | $V = \dfrac{1}{n} R^{2/3} S^{1/2}$ | Manning's n |
| 11 | Bernoulli's equation | $\dfrac{p}{\gamma} + \dfrac{V^2}{2g} + z = \text{const}$ | N/A |
| 12 | Continuity equation | $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$ | N/A |

> **Units used in KTU problems:** Pressure in **kPa** or **N/m²**; Discharge Q in **m³/s** or **L/s**; Velocity in **m/s**; Head in **m of water**.

---

## 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Water Supply Networks** — Gate valves at branches, butterfly valves on large mains, venturi meters in treatment plants, current meters at intakes.
- **Hydroelectric Power Stations** — Pitot tubes for turbine inlet velocity, Bourdon gauges for penstock pressure.
- **Dams & Spillways** — Current meters measure reservoir inflow; Sluice valves control discharge.
- **Process Industries** — Diaphragm valves and orifice meters dominate chemical flow control.
- **Irrigation Canals** — Current meters and Venturi flumes measure distributary discharge.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3 — STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

This section provides the **exhaustive mathematical derivations** that KTU examiners expect for 14-mark theory questions. Each derivation is broken into clean, labelled steps.

---

## 3.1 Derivation I — Pitot Tube Velocity (Full Proof)

**Statement:** *Derive an expression for the velocity of flow at a point in a pipe using a Pitot-static tube and a differential U-tube manometer.*

**Setup:**
- Point ① in the free stream of a pipe carrying fluid of specific weight `γ` (N/m³).
- Point ② at the stagnation mouth of the Pitot tube (V₂ = 0).
- A differential U-tube manometer containing a heavier fluid of specific weight `γ_m` is connected across the static and stagnation tappings.
- Let `x` be the deflection of the manometric fluid in metres.

### Step 1 — Apply Bernoulli's Equation

Between points ① and ② (which lie on the same horizontal streamline, so z₁ = z₂):

$$
\dfrac{p_1}{\gamma} + \dfrac{V_1^{2}}{2g} + z_1 \;=\; \dfrac{p_2}{\gamma} + \dfrac{V_2^{2}}{2g} + z_2
$$

### Step 2 — Substitute the Stagnation Condition

V₂ = 0 (fluid comes to rest at the Pitot mouth); z₁ = z₂:

$$
\dfrac{p_1}{\gamma} + \dfrac{V_1^{2}}{2g} \;=\; \dfrac{p_2}{\gamma} + 0
$$

Rearranging for V₁²:

$$
V_1^{2} \;=\; \dfrac{2g\,(p_2 - p_1)}{\gamma}
$$

### Step 3 — Express the Pressure Difference in Terms of Manometer Reading

For a differential U-tube manometer (heavier fluid `γ_m`, lighter fluid `γ`):

$$
p_2 - p_1 \;=\; (\gamma_m - \gamma)\,x
$$

> **Logic:** The difference in pressure between the two limbs of the manometer is supported by the column of manometric fluid displaced. The effective weight of this column is the *difference* in specific weights multiplied by the deflection `x`.

Substituting:

$$
V_1^{2} \;=\; \dfrac{2g\,(\gamma_m - \gamma)\,x}{\gamma}
$$

### Step 4 — Take the Square Root

$$
V_1 \;=\; \sqrt{2g\,x\left(\dfrac{\gamma_m}{\gamma} - 1\right)}
$$

### Step 5 — Introduce the Pitot Coefficient

In real instruments there is a small loss; hence we multiply by a calibration coefficient `C_v`:

$$
\boxed{\,V_{\text{actual}} \;=\; C_v\,\sqrt{2g\,x\left(\dfrac{\gamma_m}{\gamma} - 1\right)}\,}
$$

> **Special case (water in water):** If the fluid in the pipe and manometer are the same, then γ_m/γ = 1, and the formula reduces to the simpler form V = √(2gx) where `x` is the direct height difference of water columns.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — Venturi Meter Discharge Equation

**Statement:** *Derive the discharge equation for a horizontal Venturi meter fitted with a differential U-tube manometer.*

**Setup:**
- Section ① at inlet (diameter D₁, area A₁, pressure p₁, velocity V₁).
- Section ② at throat (diameter D₂, area A₂, pressure p₂, velocity V₂).
- Manometer deflection `h` using mercury (γ_m = γ_Hg) for water flow (γ = γ_w).

### Step 1 — Continuity Equation

$$
A_1 V_1 \;=\; A_2 V_2 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad V_1 \;=\; V_2\,\dfrac{A_2}{A_1}
$$

### Step 2 — Bernoulli's Equation (Horizontal Venturi, no losses first)

$$
\dfrac{p_1}{\gamma} + \dfrac{V_1^{2}}{2g} \;=\; \dfrac{p_2}{\gamma} + \dfrac{V_2^{2}}{2g}
$$

$$
\dfrac{p_1 - p_2}{\gamma} \;=\; \dfrac{V_2^{2} - V_1^{2}}{2g}
$$

### Step 3 — Replace V₁ Using Continuity

$$
\dfrac{p_1 - p_2}{\gamma} \;=\; \dfrac{V_2^{2}}{2g}\left[1 - \left(\dfrac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^{2}\right]
$$

### Step 4 — Express Pressure Difference via Manometer

For a U-tube differential manometer containing mercury below water:

$$
p_1 - p_2 \;=\; (\gamma_{Hg} - \gamma_w)\,h
$$

Therefore:

$$
\dfrac{(\gamma_{Hg} - \gamma_w)\,h}{\gamma_w} \;=\; \dfrac{V_2^{2}}{2g}\left[1 - \left(\dfrac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^{2}\right]
$$

Define `x = (γ_Hg / γ_w) − 1`, so the LHS becomes `x h`:

$$
V_2^{2} \;=\; \dfrac{2g\,x\,h}{1 - (A_2/A_1)^{2}}
$$

### Step 5 — Velocity at the Throat

$$
V_2 \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{2g\,x\,h}{1 - (A_2/A_1)^{2}}}
$$

### Step 6 — Discharge = Area × Velocity, with Coefficient of Discharge

$$
Q_{\text{theoretical}} \;=\; A_2 V_2
$$

$$
Q_{\text{actual}} \;=\; C_d\,A_2\,\sqrt{\dfrac{2g\,x\,h}{1 - (A_2/A_1)^{2}}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,Q \;=\; C_d\,A_2\,\sqrt{\dfrac{2g\,h\,(\gamma_{Hg}/\gamma_w - 1)}{1 - (A_2/A_1)^{2}}}\,}
$$

> For mercury–water systems, γ_Hg/γ_w = **13.6**, so the bracket `(13.6 − 1) = 12.6`.

---

## 3.3 Derivation III — Current Meter Rating Equation (Linear Approximation)

**Statement:** *Show that the velocity of flow V measured by a current meter is related to the rotational speed N by the linear equation V = aN + b.*

**Setup:**
- A cup-type current meter with `n_cup` cups is towed through still water at a known velocity V.
- The drag torque on the cups is `T_D ∝ V²` (because drag is proportional to dynamic pressure).
- The bearing friction torque is `T_F` (assumed constant or velocity-independent, dominant at low speeds).

### Step 1 — Energy Balance (Power In = Power Dissipated)

Hydraulic power supplied to the rotor (drag) equals the power consumed by friction at the bearing:

$$
T_D \cdot \omega \;=\; T_F \cdot \omega + \text{losses}
$$

A more practical and accurate empirical balance is:

$$
T_D - T_F \;=\; K\,\dfrac{d\omega}{dt} + \text{viscous loss}
$$

At **steady-state rotation** (constant N), `dω/dt = 0`, and the dominant balance becomes:

$$
k_1 V^{2} \;=\; k_2 N + k_3
$$

### Step 2 — Linearise About an Operating Point

If we assume V is close to its operating mean, expand `V² ≈ (mean V) × V`, leading to a linear relation:

$$
V \;=\; a\,N + b
$$

### Step 3 — Determine the Constants by Calibration

Two or more tow-tank runs are performed at known speeds V₁ and V₂, recording N₁ and N₂:

$$
a \;=\; \dfrac{V_2 - V_1}{N_2 - N_1}, \qquad b \;=\; \dfrac{V_1 N_2 - V_2 N_1}{N_2 - N_1}
$$

### Step 4 — Final Working Equation

$$
\boxed{\,V \;=\; a\,N + b\,}
$$

where:
- `a` = **metre constant** (m per revolution, or m·s/rev)
- `b` = **hydraulic zero** (m/s, often a small positive or negative number representing the threshold velocity needed to overcome static friction)

---

## 3.4 Derivation IV — U-Tube Differential Manometer Pressure Equation

**Statement:** *Derive the expression for pressure difference measured by a U-tube manometer.*

Consider two pipes A and B with pressures p_A and p_B, connected at the bottom by an inverted U-tube manometer containing a manometric fluid of specific weight γ_m. The fluid in the pipes has specific weight γ_f.

### Step 1 — Manometric Balance

At the lower interface level (call it the datum), the pressure from side A must equal the pressure from side B:

$$
p_A + \gamma_f\,h_1 \;=\; p_B + \gamma_f\,h_2 + \gamma_m\,h
$$

### Step 2 — Simplify (Differential Case)

When the manometer is connected across a horizontal differential section and the geometry is such that h₁ = h₂ (typical for Venturi/orifice), the equation reduces to:

$$
p_A - p_B \;=\; \gamma_m\,h
$$

But for the **inverted U-tube (lighter fluid, air)** case, we get:

$$
p_A - p_B \;=\; (\gamma_m - \gamma_f)\,h
$$

### Step 3 — General Form

$$
\boxed{\,\Delta p \;=\; (\gamma_m - \gamma_f)\,h\,}
$$

This is the **master equation** for any U-tube manometer reading, and it appears as the numerator inside every discharge / velocity formula of the module.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Exam Style)

> **Problem:** A Pitot-static tube is used to measure the velocity of water flowing in a pipe. The stagnation and static tappings are connected to a differential mercury U-tube manometer which shows a deflection of `x = 120 mm`. Take C_v = 0.98. Calculate the velocity of flow at the centreline. (Use γ_Hg/γ_w = 13.6.)

### Step 1 — Identify the Formula

$$
V \;=\; C_v \sqrt{2g\,x\,(\gamma_{Hg}/\gamma_w - 1)}
$$

### Step 2 — Substitute the Values

- g = 9.81 m/s²
- x = 0.120 m
- γ_Hg/γ_w − 1 = 13.6 − 1 = 12.6
- C_v = 0.98

$$
V \;=\; 0.98 \times \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.120 \times 12.6}
$$

### Step 3 — Evaluate the Bracket

$$
2 \times 9.81 \times 0.120 \times 12.6 \;=\; 29.66
$$

### Step 4 — Square Root

$$
\sqrt{29.66} \;=\; 5.447 \text{ m/s}
$$

### Step 5 — Apply the Coefficient

$$
V \;=\; 0.98 \times 5.447 \;=\; 5.338 \text{ m/s}
$$

### Step 6 — Final Answer

$$
\boxed{\,V \;\approx\; 5.34 \text{ m/s}\,}
$$

> **KTU Answer-writing Tip:** Always carry **3 significant figures** in the final answer and **underline** the boxed value to draw the examiner's eye.

---

## 3.6 Conversion Logic Matrix (Frequently Confused Pairs)

| Confusion | Correct Logic |
|---|---|
| C_d vs C_v vs C_c | C_d = C_c × C_v. Coefficient of discharge is the *product* of coefficient of velocity and coefficient of contraction. |
| Stagnation vs Static Pressure | Stagnation = static + velocity (= total). Static is the *normal* pressure; velocity head converts into extra pressure when flow stagnates. |
| Venturi vs Orifice C_d | Venturi is smooth and converging-diverging, hence C_d ≈ 0.97. Orifice is a sharp plate, hence C_d ≈ 0.62. |
| Pressure head vs Pressure | Pressure head = p/γ (in metres of fluid). Pressure = p (in N/m² or Pa). |
| b in V = aN + b | b is **not** zero; it represents the slip / threshold. Some meters report b ≈ 0.02 m/s. |
| Gauge vs Absolute Pressure | P_abs = P_gauge + P_atm. Vacuum = −P_gauge. |
| C-type vs Spiral Bourdon | C-type is one turn (cheap, low range). Spiral/helical is multi-turn (high sensitivity, high range). |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

> All questions below are **board-exam simulation** questions crafted in the exact style of KTU End-Semester Evaluation (ESE). Each carries a clear marks-distribution, model answer, and **examiner pitfall warning**.

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 (Model)]**
**Differentiate between a gate valve and a globe valve. State one typical application of each.**

**Model Answer (Structured 3-Mark Response):**

| Feature | Gate Valve | Globe Valve |
|---|---|---|
| Disc motion | Wedge moves perpendicular to flow | Disc moves parallel to flow, against a seat |
| Flow path | Straight-through (unobstructed) | Z-shaped with a seat obstruction |
| Pressure drop | Very low when fully open | Higher, by design |
| Best use | ON/OFF isolation (open or close) | Throttling / flow regulation |
| Typical application | City water mains isolation | Cooling-water flow regulation in condensers |

> **1 mark** for the correct disc-motion statement, **1 mark** for application, **1 mark** for the comparison table or crisp differentiator.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Model)]**
**With a neat sketch, explain the working of a Bourdon tube pressure gauge.**

**Model Answer:**

A Bourdon tube is a curved metallic tube of **elliptical cross-section**. When fluid under pressure enters the tube, it tends to **straighten** because the outer fibre of the elliptical cross-section has a longer path than the inner fibre. The small tip displacement is amplified by a **link–lever–rack-and-pinion** mechanism, which rotates a pointer over a graduated dial.

- **C-type** — single curved tube, low-pressure range.
- **Helical / Spiral** — multi-turn tube, higher sensitivity for high pressures.

**Application:** Steam boilers, hydraulic presses, gas cylinders, compressed-air lines.

> **1 mark** sketch (verbal), **1 mark** principle of straightening, **1 mark** application + amplification mechanism.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **KTU ESE Rule:** Answer **any ONE** full question (14 marks) from the choice of two. Each 14-mark question is split into **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. The model solutions below show the depth expected.

---

### **PART B — QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

#### **Q. (a) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam — May 2023 (Model)]**
**Derive the expression for the velocity at a point in a pipe using a Pitot-static tube connected to a differential U-tube manometer. State clearly the assumptions made.**

**Model Solution:**

**Assumptions:**
1. Flow is steady, incompressible, and irrotational.
2. The fluid is ideal (no viscous losses between the free stream and stagnation point).
3. The Pitot tube does not significantly disturb the upstream flow.
4. The two pressure tappings (stagnation and static) are at the same elevation.

**Derivation:**

Apply **Bernoulli's equation** between point ① in the free stream and point ② at the stagnation mouth of the Pitot tube, where V₂ = 0:

$$
\dfrac{p_1}{\gamma} + \dfrac{V_1^{2}}{2g} + z_1 \;=\; \dfrac{p_2}{\gamma} + 0 + z_2
$$

With z₁ = z₂:

$$
V_1^{2} \;=\; \dfrac{2\,(p_2 - p_1)}{\gamma}
$$

A differential U-tube manometer reads the pressure difference as:

$$
p_2 - p_1 \;=\; (\gamma_m - \gamma)\,x
$$

Therefore:

$$
V_1 \;=\; \sqrt{2g\,x\,\left(\dfrac{\gamma_m}{\gamma} - 1\right)}
$$

Multiplying by the Pitot coefficient C_v (≈ 0.98) to account for instrument imperfection:

$$
\boxed{\,V_{\text{actual}} \;=\; C_v\,\sqrt{2g\,x\,\left(\dfrac{\gamma_m}{\gamma} - 1\right)}\,}
$$

> **Mark-split (7 marks):** 2 marks for assumptions, 3 marks for the Bernoulli application, 2 marks for the manometric conversion and the final boxed equation.

#### **Q. (b) [7 Marks]**
**A Pitot-static tube is inserted at the centre of a pipe of diameter 300 mm carrying water. The stagnation and static tappings are connected to a U-tube manometer containing mercury, which shows a deflection of 80 mm. Take C_v = 0.98. Calculate (i) the velocity at the centreline, and (ii) the discharge through the pipe, assuming the velocity profile follows V/V_max = (y/R)^(1/7) (the power-law profile).**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (i) — Centreline velocity:**

$$
V_{\max} \;=\; 0.98 \times \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.080 \times (13.6 - 1)}
$$

$$
V_{\max} \;=\; 0.98 \times \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.080 \times 12.6}
$$

$$
V_{\max} \;=\; 0.98 \times \sqrt{19.78} \;=\; 0.98 \times 4.448 \;=\; 4.36 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Part (ii) — Discharge using the 1/7th power-law profile:**

For the profile V/V_max = (y/R)^(1/7), the ratio of mean velocity to maximum velocity is given by the standard result:

$$
\dfrac{V_{\text{mean}}}{V_{\max}} \;=\; \dfrac{n + 1}{n + 2} \;=\; \dfrac{1/7 + 1}{1/7 + 2} \;=\; \dfrac{8/7}{15/7} \;=\; \dfrac{8}{15}
$$

Therefore:

$$
V_{\text{mean}} \;=\; \dfrac{8}{15} \times 4.36 \;=\; 2.325 \text{ m/s}
$$

Pipe area:

$$
A \;=\; \dfrac{\pi}{4}\,D^{2} \;=\; \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times 0.30^{2} \;=\; 0.0707 \text{ m}^{2}
$$

Discharge:

$$
Q \;=\; A \times V_{\text{mean}} \;=\; 0.0707 \times 2.325 \;=\; 0.1644 \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,Q \;\approx\; 164.4 \text{ L/s}\,}
$$

> **Mark-split (7 marks):** 3 marks for Part (i), 4 marks for Part (ii) including the velocity-profile integral substitution.

---

### **PART B — QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### **Q. (a) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 (Model)]**
**With the help of a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a Venturi meter. Derive the expression for discharge through a horizontal Venturi meter fitted with a differential U-tube manometer.**

**Model Solution:**

**Construction:** A Venturi meter has (i) a **converging conical section** (typically 21° half-angle), (ii) a **throat** (smallest cross-section), and (iii) a **diverging section** (5° to 7° half-angle to avoid flow separation). Pressure tappings are provided at the inlet (section ①) and at the throat (section ②), connected to a differential manometer.

**Working:** As fluid approaches the throat, the area reduces and velocity increases. By Bernoulli's principle, the pressure drops at the throat. The manometer measures this pressure drop, from which the velocity and discharge are calculated.

**Derivation:**

Continuity: `A₁ V₁ = A₂ V₂` → `V₁ = V₂ (A₂/A₁)`.

Bernoulli (horizontal, ideal):

$$
\dfrac{p_1}{\gamma} + \dfrac{V_1^{2}}{2g} \;=\; \dfrac{p_2}{\gamma} + \dfrac{V_2^{2}}{2g}
$$

Substituting V₁:

$$
\dfrac{p_1 - p_2}{\gamma} \;=\; \dfrac{V_2^{2}}{2g}\,\left[1 - \left(\dfrac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^{2}\right]
$$

Manometer relation: `p₁ − p₂ = (γ_m − γ) h = γ x h` (where x = γ_m/γ − 1).

Therefore:

$$
V_2 \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{2g\,x\,h}{1 - (A_2/A_1)^{2}}}
$$

Discharge with coefficient:

$$
\boxed{\,Q \;=\; C_d\,A_2\,\sqrt{\dfrac{2g\,h\,(\gamma_m/\gamma - 1)}{1 - (A_2/A_1)^{2}}}\,}
$$

> **Mark-split:** 2 marks for sketch + construction, 2 marks for working description, 3 marks for derivation.

#### **Q. (b) [7 Marks]**
**A current meter with rating equation V = 0.62 N + 0.025 (where V is in m/s and N is rev/s) is held at a point in a river for 5 minutes. The total number of revolutions recorded is 1200. Find (i) the velocity at the point, and (ii) the discharge of the river, given the cross-section is approximately rectangular, 12 m wide with an average depth of 2.5 m.**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (i):**

Time = 5 min = 300 s
N = 1200 / 300 = **4 rev/s**

$$
V \;=\; 0.62 \times 4 + 0.025 \;=\; 2.48 + 0.025 \;=\; 2.505 \text{ m/s}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,V \;\approx\; 2.51 \text{ m/s}\,}
$$

**Part (ii):**

Cross-sectional area of the rectangular river:

$$
A \;=\; \text{width} \times \text{depth} \;=\; 12 \times 2.5 \;=\; 30 \text{ m}^{2}
$$

Discharge:

$$
Q \;=\; A \times V \;=\; 30 \times 2.505
$$

$$
\boxed{\,Q \;=\; 75.15 \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s}\,}
$$

> **Mark-split (7 marks):** 3 marks for Part (i) with N calculation, 2 marks for area, 2 marks for the final Q.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Zones — Read Carefully Before You Sit the Exam**
>
> 1. **Units, Units, Units!** A wrong unit in the final answer (e.g., writing Q in L/s when the formula gives m³/s, without conversion) costs **at least 1 mark** in every 7-mark sub-question. Always state the unit explicitly in the boxed answer.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the Coefficient.** Writing `Q = A₂ √(2gh …)` without the C_d (or C_v for Pitot) costs **1 to 2 marks**. Examiners explicitly look for `C_d = 0.62` for orifice, `0.97` for venturi, `0.98` for Pitot.
>
> 3. **Confusing Stagnation and Static Pressure.** Stagnation is the *higher* one. Drawing the manometer with the stagnation limb below the static limb is treated as a **sign error**, and the resulting V becomes imaginary (you will be forced to conclude with a negative under-root, which is a guaranteed zero in that sub-part).
>
> 4. **Manometer Reading Direction.** The manometer formula `(γ_m − γ) h` is for a *conventional* (heavier fluid below) U-tube. For an *inverted* U-tube (air), the sign flips: `p₁ − p₂ = (γ − γ_m) h`. KTU papers have a 1-mark sub-part asking exactly this distinction.
>
> 5. **Current Meter Threshold.** Writing `V = aN` and dropping the `+ b` term is wrong — it gives a finite velocity at zero rotation, which physically violates the calibration. **Always retain the `+ b` term**, even if `b` is small.
>
> 6. **Sketches Without Labels.** A neat sketch **without** arrow-marked flow direction, or without `①` and `②` marked at the inlet and throat, gets **½ to 1 mark deducted** under the "neatness & labelling" head.

---

## 5.4 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> **High-density bullet summary — read this 30 minutes before the exam.**

### 🔹 Taps
- A **piezometer tap** is for direct vertical column measurement; cannot read negative pressure.
- A **pressure tapping** (corporation cock) is a small hole with a valve for connecting a gauge.
- **Standard placement rule:** ≥ 10D downstream, ≥ 2D upstream of fittings.

### 🔹 Valves
- **Gate** = isolation, straight-through.
- **Globe** = regulation, Z-path.
- **Ball** = quick shut-off, quarter-turn.
- **Butterfly** = large diameter, disc rotates.
- **Check (NRV)** = one-way only.
- **Needle** = fine control, instrument lines.
- **Sluice** = municipal mains.

### 🔹 Pipe Fittings
- **Elbow 90°/45°** — direction change; elbow loss ≈ 30 V²/2g.
- **Tee (equal/unequal)** — branching.
- **Reducer (concentric/eccentric)** — diameter change; eccentric is used at pump suction to avoid air pocket.
- **Union** — three-piece detachable joint.
- **Flange** — bolted to equipment.
- **Foot valve / strainer** — at pump suction, retains prime.

### 🔹 Gauges
- **Bourdon tube** = C-type / helical / spiral; principle: elliptical tube straightens.
- **U-tube manometer** = `Δp = (γ_m − γ) h`.
- **Diaphragm / Bellows** = low-pressure applications.
- **Dead-weight** = primary standard; `P = W / A_piston`.

### 🔹 Pitot Tubes
- **Simple Pitot**: stagnation only; needs separate static tap.
- **Pitot-static (Prandtl)**: both pressures in one instrument.
- **Equation:** `V = C_v √(2g x (γ_m/γ − 1))`; C_v ≈ 0.95–0.99.

### 🔹 Water Meters
- **Positive Displacement**: nutating disc, oscillating piston — measures volume directly, high accuracy.
- **Velocity / Inferential**: Venturi, orifice, turbine, rotameter — measures velocity, volume inferred.
- **Standard formula:** `Q = C_d A₂ √(2g h (γ_m/γ − 1) / (1 − (A₂/A₁)²))`.

### 🔹 Current Meters
- **Cup (Price) type** — 6 cups, vertical spindle, less sensitive to oblique flow.
- **Propeller type** — horizontal axis, sensitive to flow direction.
- **Electromagnetic & Acoustic Doppler** — modern, no moving parts.
- **Rating equation:** `V = aN + b`; `a` = metre constant, `b` = hydraulic zero (do not drop it).

### 🔹 Master Constants to Memorise
- **g = 9.81 m/s²**
- **γ_water = 9810 N/m³**
- **ρ_water = 1000 kg/m³**
- **γ_Hg/γ_water = 13.6**
- **C_d for Venturi = 0.95–0.98**, **Orifice = 0.60–0.65**, **Pitot = 0.95–0.99**

### 🔹 Viva One-Liners to Pre-Rehearse
1. *"Which valve has the lowest head loss?"* → **Gate valve.**
2. *"Why is a Pitot tube placed at the centreline?"* → **Maximum velocity region; gives centreline V_max.**
3. *"What is hydraulic zero in a current meter?"* → **Threshold velocity `b`; the offset in the linear rating equation.**
4. *"Why is a Venturi preferred over an orifice?"* → **Higher C_d, less head loss, no flow separation at the diffuser.**
5. *"Why eccentric reducer at pump suction?"* → **To prevent air-pocket formation that would vapour-lock the pump.**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Calibration of Pressure Gauges
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCEL308 - FLUID MECHANICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Calibration of Pressure Gauges

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Calibration of Pressure Gauges** is defined as the experimental procedure of establishing the relationship between the indicated output of a pressure-measuring instrument (the gauge under test) and the corresponding true pressure value, as generated by a **reference standard of known accuracy** (typically a Dead Weight Pressure Tester or a Master Gauge of higher precision). The procedure quantifies the **systematic error, linearity, hysteresis, and repeatability** of the gauge.

In KTU lab evaluations, the calibration curve is the primary deliverable. It is a plot of:

- **X-axis (Reference / True Pressure):** Pressure produced by the Dead Weight Tester (DWT), calculated from applied standard weights.
- **Y-axis (Indicated Pressure):** Reading shown on the gauge under test.

The deviation of this curve from the ideal 45° straight line gives the **error calibration curve**, and the slope gives the **correction factor** for the gauge.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Trusted Friend" Test**
> Imagine you have a bathroom weighing scale at home. Before trusting it, you would weigh a 50 kg standard gym dumbbell. If the scale shows 51 kg, you know your scale has a **+1 kg error** at 50 kg. You then test it with a 100 kg bag, get 101.5 kg, and so on. By plotting "true weight vs. shown weight," you create a **calibration curve** that tells you exactly how to correct your scale's reading for any value.
>
> **Calibration of pressure gauges is exactly the same logic** — we use a perfectly accurate "pressure dumbbell" (the **Dead Weight Tester**) to check the accuracy of a pressure gauge over its full range. The closer the gauge's curve is to a straight 45° line, the more accurate it is.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Standard Acceleration due to gravity:** $g = 9.81 \ \text{m/s}^2$ (used to convert weight to force)
- **Standard Atmospheric Pressure:** $p_{\text{atm}} = 101.325 \ \text{kPa} = 1.01325 \ \text{bar}$
- **Pressure Unit Conversions:**
  - $1 \ \text{bar} = 10^5 \ \text{Pa} = 100 \ \text{kPa}$
  - $1 \ \text{N/mm}^2 = 1 \ \text{MPa} = 10 \ \text{bar}$
  - $1 \ \text{kgf/cm}^2 = 0.980665 \ \text{bar} \approx 14.223 \ \text{psi}$

### 1.4 Why Calibration is Essential in Engineering

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In critical industrial systems — **boiler pressure vessels, hydraulic presses, aircraft hydraulic lines, nuclear reactors, SCUBA tanks, and oxygen cylinders** — a misreading of even **1–2%** can lead to catastrophic failure or explosion. Hence, periodic calibration of pressure gauges is mandated by standards such as **IS 3624, BS 1780, ASME PTC 19.2, and ISO 9001** quality systems.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Working Principle of the Dead Weight Tester (DWT)

The Dead Weight Tester is the **Primary Standard** for pressure calibration worldwide. Its principle is elegantly simple — it converts a known **mass** into a known **pressure** using a precisely machined piston and cylinder.

**Step-by-step logic:**

- A precision-ground piston of known effective area $A_e$ is placed inside a close-fitting cylinder.
- Standard calibrated weights (including the weight of the piston itself) are loaded onto a platform on top of the piston.
- The total downward force is $F = m \cdot g$, where $m$ is the total mass of weights + piston + platform.
- The fluid (oil) beneath the piston is pressurized to exactly balance this force.
- The pressure generated is therefore:

$$p_{\text{true}} = \frac{F}{A_e} = \frac{m \cdot g}{A_e}$$

- Since $m$, $g$, and $A_e$ are all known to very high precision, the resulting pressure $p_{\text{true}}$ is treated as the **true reference pressure**.

### 2.2 Working Principle of the Bourdon Tube Pressure Gauge (Gauge Under Test)

The most common industrial pressure gauge is the **Bourdon Tube Gauge** invented by Eugène Bourdon in 1849. Its operating principle:

- The pressure to be measured enters a **curved, flattened metallic tube** (the Bourdon element) of oval cross-section.
- Internal pressure tends to **straighten** the curved tube (oval cross-section tries to become circular).
- The tip of the tube moves by a small displacement proportional to applied pressure.
- This movement is amplified through a **link–lever–gear sector–pinion** mechanism.
- The pinion rotates a pointer across a graduated dial — giving the pressure reading.

### 2.3 Sources of Error in a Pressure Gauge

| # | Error Type | Cause | Effect on Calibration |
|---|-----------|-------|----------------------|
| 1 | **Zero Error** | Pointer not at zero when no pressure applied | Vertical shift of curve |
| 2 | **Linearity Error** | Non-uniform tube deflection / mechanical slack | Curve deviates from straight line |
| 3 | **Hysteresis Error** | Different readings during pressurization vs. depressurization | Loop gap in calibration curve |
| 4 | **Repeatability Error** | Different readings at the same pressure on repeated trials | Scatter of data points |
| 5 | **Parallax Error** | Reading dial from an angle (improper eye position) | Random fluctuation of ± reading |
| 6 | **Friction Error** | Mechanical friction in gear train | Pointer sticks / sluggish response |
| 7 | **Temperature Error** | Thermal expansion of Bourdon tube material | Zero drift with ambient temp |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---------|---------|-------|
| 1 | $p_{\text{true}} = \dfrac{(W_{\text{piston}} + W_{\text{weights}})}{A_e}$ | True pressure from DWT | $\text{N/m}^2$ or Pa |
| 2 | $p_{\text{true}} = \dfrac{m_{\text{total}} \cdot g}{A_e}$ | Same, in mass form | Pa |
| 3 | $\text{Error} = p_{\text{indicated}} - p_{\text{true}}$ | Deviation of gauge reading | $\text{kPa}$ or $\text{bar}$ |
| 4 | $\% \text{Error} = \left(\dfrac{p_{\text{ind}} - p_{\text{true}}}{p_{\text{true}}}\right) \times 100$ | Percentage deviation | dimensionless (%) |
| 5 | $\text{Correction} = - \text{Error}$ | Value to add to indicated reading | $\text{kPa}$ |
| 6 | $\text{Calibration Factor (CF)} = \dfrac{p_{\text{true}}}{p_{\text{ind}}}$ | Multiplier to correct indicated reading | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\text{Hysteresis} = \vert p_{\text{ind, increasing}} - p_{\text{ind, decreasing}} \vert$ | Loop gap in curve | $\text{kPa}$ |
| 8 | $\text{Sensitivity} = \dfrac{\Delta p_{\text{ind}}}{\Delta p_{\text{true}}}$ | Slope of calibration line | dimensionless |
| 9 | $\text{Resolution} = \dfrac{\text{Full scale range}}{\text{No. of dial divisions}}$ | Smallest readable increment | $\text{bar/div}$ |
| 10 | $\text{Accuracy} = \pm (\% \text{FS} + n \cdot \text{LSD})$ | Manufacturer's accuracy class | depends |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Always use the same units for $p_{\text{true}}$ and $p_{\text{indicated}}$ in any error calculation. The most common mistake students make is mixing $\text{N/mm}^2$ with $\text{kgf/cm}^2$. Convert all values to **$\text{N/mm}^2$ (MPa)** or **$\text{bar}$** first, then proceed.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** Calibration of altimeter sub-systems using DWT.
- **Oil & Gas:** Wellhead pressure gauges are calibrated every 6 months.
- **Power Plants:** Boiler drum pressure gauges — calibration is a statutory safety requirement.
- **Hydraulic Presses / Injection Moulding:** Calibration ensures consistent product quality.
- **Pharmaceutical & Food Industry:** Sanitary pressure gauges in CIP/SIP systems must be traceable to national standards (NABL, ISO 17025).
- **Research Labs:** Calibration certificates are needed to claim the accuracy of any published experimental data.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of True Pressure from Dead Weight Tester

Let the total mass placed on the piston platform be $m_{\text{total}}$ (this includes the piston + platform + slotted weights). The weight of this mass acts vertically downward as a force $F$ on the fluid trapped in the cylinder.

**Step 1 — Express the downward force:**

The weight of the mass exerts a downward force equal to:

$$F = m_{\text{total}} \cdot g$$

**Step 2 — Equate force to pressure times area:**

This force is balanced by the fluid pressure acting on the effective cross-sectional area $A_e$ of the piston:

$$p_{\text{true}} \cdot A_e = F = m_{\text{total}} \cdot g$$

**Step 3 — Solve for true pressure:**

Rearranging to isolate $p_{\text{true}}$:

$$p_{\text{true}} = \frac{m_{\text{total}} \cdot g}{A_e}$$

**Step 4 — Express in commonly used engineering units:**

Since $A_e$ is usually given in $\text{cm}^2$ or $\text{mm}^2$ and mass in $\text{kg}$, the pressure naturally comes out in $\text{kgf/cm}^2$ (when $g \approx 9.81 \ \text{m/s}^2$). For SI units:

$$p_{\text{true}} \, [\text{Pa}] = \frac{m \, [\text{kg}] \times 9.81 \, [\text{m/s}^2]}{A_e \, [\text{m}^2]}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to bar for readability:**

$$p_{\text{true}} \, [\text{bar}] = \frac{p_{\text{true}} \, [\text{Pa}]}{10^5}$$

### 3.2 Derivation of the Error Calibration Curve Equation

Let the best-fit straight line through the calibration data be:

$$p_{\text{ind}} = a \cdot p_{\text{true}} + b$$

where $a$ and $b$ are obtained by **least-squares regression**. For an **ideal, perfectly accurate gauge**:

$$a = 1, \quad b = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad p_{\text{ind}} = p_{\text{true}}$$

The **deviation from ideal behavior** is:

$$\Delta p(p_{\text{true}}) = p_{\text{ind}} - p_{\text{true}} = (a-1) \cdot p_{\text{true}} + b$$

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU lab records, students are typically expected to draw two graphs:
> 1. **Calibration Curve:** $p_{\text{ind}}$ vs. $p_{\text{true}}$ — ideally a 45° straight line.
> 2. **Error Curve:** $\Delta p$ vs. $p_{\text{true}}$ — ideally a horizontal line on the x-axis.
>
> The error curve makes the systematic error visible far more clearly than the calibration curve.

### 3.3 Worked-Out Numerical Example (Typical KTU Exam Problem)

**Problem:** A Bourdon pressure gauge is calibrated against a Dead Weight Tester. The piston area is $A_e = 0.5 \ \text{cm}^2$. The following readings are taken (pressurizing run):

| $m$ (kg) | $p_{\text{ind}}$ (bar) |
|----------|----------------------|
| 0        | 0.00                 |
| 2        | 3.85                 |
| 4        | 7.78                 |
| 6        | 11.65                |
| 8        | 15.60                |
| 10       | 19.55                |

**Step 1 — Calculate true pressure for each mass using $p_{\text{true}} = \dfrac{m \cdot g}{A_e \cdot 10^5}$ (in bar).**

Using $g = 9.81 \ \text{m/s}^2$ and $A_e = 0.5 \ \text{cm}^2 = 0.5 \times 10^{-4} \ \text{m}^2$:

For $m = 2 \ \text{kg}$:

$$p_{\text{true}} = \frac{2 \times 9.81}{0.5 \times 10^{-4} \times 10^5} = \frac{19.62}{5} = 3.924 \ \text{bar}$$

For $m = 4 \ \text{kg}$:

$$p_{\text{true}} = \frac{4 \times 9.81}{5} = 7.848 \ \text{bar}$$

For $m = 6 \ \text{kg}$:

$$p_{\text{true}} = \frac{6 \times 9.81}{5} = 11.772 \ \text{bar}$$

For $m = 8 \ \text{kg}$:

$$p_{\text{true}} = \frac{8 \times 9.81}{5} = 15.696 \ \text{bar}$$

For $m = 10 \ \text{kg}$:

$$p_{\text{true}} = \frac{10 \times 9.81}{5} = 19.620 \ \text{bar}$$

**Step 2 — Tabulate errors.**

| $m$ (kg) | $p_{\text{true}}$ (bar) | $p_{\text{ind}}$ (bar) | $\text{Error} = p_{\text{ind}} - p_{\text{true}}$ (bar) | $\%\text{Error}$ |
|----------|------------------------|------------------------|----------------------------------------------------------|-----------------|
| 0        | 0.000                  | 0.00                   | 0.000                                                     | 0.00%           |
| 2        | 3.924                  | 3.85                   | $-0.074$                                                  | $-1.89\%$       |
| 4        | 7.848                  | 7.78                   | $-0.068$                                                  | $-0.87\%$       |
| 6        | 11.772                 | 11.65                  | $-0.122$                                                  | $-1.04\%$       |
| 8        | 15.696                 | 15.60                  | $-0.096$                                                  | $-0.61\%$       |
| 10       | 19.620                 | 19.55                  | $-0.070$                                                  | $-0.36\%$       |

**Step 3 — Plot the calibration curve and error curve.**

- **Calibration Curve:** Plot $p_{\text{ind}}$ (Y) vs. $p_{\text{true}}$ (X). The slope is approximately $\frac{19.55 - 0}{19.62 - 0} = 0.9964$, very close to 1.
- **Error Curve:** Plot $\Delta p$ (Y) vs. $p_{\text{true}}$ (X). All errors are **negative**, meaning the gauge **under-reads** (reads low) at all points.

**Step 4 — Calibration factor for the gauge.**

$$\text{CF} = \frac{p_{\text{true}}}{p_{\text{ind}}} = \frac{19.62}{19.55} = 1.00358$$

So, the corrected reading for any indicated value is $p_{\text{corrected}} = 1.00358 \times p_{\text{ind}}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** Since all errors are negative and roughly constant in magnitude (≈ 0.07 to 0.12 bar), the gauge has a **systematic negative bias** that is nearly **constant in absolute terms** (a "zero-shift" type error) rather than percentage-based. This points to a **mechanical zero misalignment** or **friction in the pinion gear**. The recommended remedy is to **re-zero the gauge** and re-lubricate the movement.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram of the Dead Weight Pressure Tester (DWT)

The following Mermaid block diagram shows the functional architecture of a Dead Weight Pressure Tester connected to the gauge under test, with the complete calibration procedure mapped as a sequential flow.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Piston Platform] --> A2[Slotted Standard Weights]
    A2 --> A3[Total Mass M total on Piston]
    A3 --> A4{Pressure Generation}
    A4 -->|F = M total x g divided by A e| A5[True Pressure p true]
    A5 --> A6[Pressurized Hydraulic Oil in Cylinder]
    A6 --> A7[High Pressure Hose to Gauge Under Test]
    A7 --> A8[Bourdon Tube Deflects]
    A8 --> A9[Link Lever Amplifies Motion]
    A9 --> A10[Pinion and Gear Sector]
    A10 --> A11[Pointer Rotates on Dial]
    A11 --> A12[Indicated Pressure p ind]
    A12 --> A13{Record p ind and p true}
    A13 -->|Repeat for multiple M| A14[Tabulate Error]
    A14 --> A15[Plot Calibration Curve]
    A15 --> A16[Plot Error Curve]
    A16 --> A17[Compute CF and Hysteresis]
```

### 4.2 Schematic of the Bourdon Tube Pressure Gauge (Internal Architecture)

The following block diagram shows the **functional architecture** of the Bourdon gauge, mapping the energy flow from pressurized fluid to visible pointer displacement.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B1[Pressurized Fluid Inlet] --> B2[Bourdon Tube Curved Oval Cross Section]
    B2 -->|Internal pressure tends to straighten tube| B3[Tip Displacement of Tube]
    B3 --> B4[Connecting Link]
    B4 --> B5[Lever Arm]
    B5 --> B6[Sector Gear]
    B6 --> B7[Pinion Gear]
    B7 --> B8[Pointer Shaft Rotation]
    B8 --> B9[Dial with Graduations]
    B9 --> B10[Visible Pressure Reading]
    B11[Hair Spring for Zero Restoration] -.->|Counter torque| B7
    B12[Damping Dashpot] -.->|Reduces oscillation| B5
```

### 4.3 Calibration Procedure Flowchart (Sequential Lab Steps)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C1[Step 1: Check Zero of Gauge] --> C2[Adjust if Required]
    C2 --> C3[Step 2: Connect Gauge to DWT Outlet]
    C3 --> C4[Step 3: Open Bleed Valve and Prime Oil]
    C4 --> C5[Step 4: Close Bleed Valve]
    C5 --> C6[Step 5: Rotate Piston by Hand to Spindle Weights on Platform]
    C6 --> C7[Step 6: Wait for Piston to Float and Stabilize]
    C7 --> C8[Step 7: Record p true and p indicated for Increasing Loads]
    C8 --> C9[Step 8: Reverse the Procedure Record Readings for Decreasing Loads]
    C9 --> C10[Step 9: Tabulate All Readings]
    C10 --> C11[Step 10: Compute Error and Percent Error]
    C11 --> C12[Step 11: Plot Calibration Curve and Error Curve]
    C12 --> C13[Step 12: Compute Calibration Factor and Hysteresis]
    C13 --> C14[Step 13: Report Compliance or Non Compliance with Standard]
```
