## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **Define the term "calibration" as applied to pressure gauges. Why is it necessary to calibrate a pressure gauge periodically?**

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Calibration** is the process of comparing the readings of a pressure gauge under test against a reference standard (such as a Dead Weight Tester or a Master Gauge) to determine the **accuracy, linearity, and repeatability** of the gauge over its full range.
>
> **Necessity of periodic calibration:**
> 1. Pressure gauges drift over time due to **mechanical wear**, **fatigue of the Bourdon tube**, and **aging of the gear train**.
> 2. **Industrial safety** mandates it — inaccurate gauges in boilers, gas cylinders, or hydraulic systems can lead to **catastrophic failure** or explosions.
> 3. **Quality standards** such as ISO 9001 and statutory bodies (BIS, NABL) require traceable calibration for compliance.
> 4. It establishes the **measurement uncertainty** of the instrument, which is essential for **process control and product quality**.
> 5. It allows the **correction factor** to be applied to the indicated reading, ensuring the **integrity of measurements** used in research, design, and production.

---

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam — June 2023] **What is a Dead Weight Tester? Explain its working principle with a neat sketch.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **Dead Weight Pressure Tester (DWT)** is a **primary standard instrument** used to generate highly accurate, known pressures for the calibration of pressure gauges, transducers, and switches.
>
> **Working Principle:**
> - A precision-machined **piston of known effective area $A_e$** is inserted into a tight-fitting cylinder.
> - Standard calibrated **slotted weights** are loaded on a platform on top of the piston.
> - The total downward force $F = m_{\text{total}} \cdot g$ acts on the trapped hydraulic fluid (usually oil).
> - This force generates a fluid pressure $p_{\text{true}} = \dfrac{m_{\text{total}} \cdot g}{A_e}$.
> - The piston floats in equilibrium, indicating that the generated pressure exactly balances the applied weights.
> - The gauge under test, connected to the DWT outlet, will therefore experience this accurately known pressure.
>
> **Sketch:** (Refer to Section 4.1 — Block Diagram of DWT in the notes above.)
>
> Since the generated pressure depends only on **mass, gravity, and geometric area** — all of which can be measured to extremely high precision — the DWT is treated as a **primary standard** with the lowest uncertainty (typically $\pm 0.05\%$ of reading).

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU Internal Choice Pattern

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency is the #1 cause of losing 3–4 marks.** Always convert all weights to kg, all areas to $\text{m}^2$, and report final pressure consistently (preferably in **bar** or **MPa**).
> 2. **Missing Hysteresis:** In Part B questions asking for "complete calibration," students often forget to record readings on the **decreasing pressure run**. This costs the hysteresis sub-question entirely.
> 3. **No Proper Graphs:** Examiners award 2 marks specifically for **calibration curve + error curve** with axes labeled, units mentioned, and a legend. Sketches without axes/units get zero.
> 4. **Not Reporting Zero Error:** A gauge that reads 0.05 bar at zero pressure must be corrected first. Ignoring this gives wrong calibration factors.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

**(a)** [7 Marks] With the help of a **neat block diagram**, explain the **construction and working of a Bourdon Tube Pressure Gauge**. State the role of the **hair spring** and the **damping dashpot** in the instrument.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A Bourdon pressure gauge is calibrated against a Dead Weight Tester having a piston area of $0.5 \ \text{cm}^2$. The following readings were obtained during the increasing pressure run:

| Weights added (kg) | 0    | 2    | 4    | 6    | 8    | 10   |
|---------------------|------|------|------|------|------|------|
| Gauge reading (bar) | 0.00 | 3.80 | 7.65 | 11.50| 15.30| 19.20|

Calculate the **true pressure, error, and percentage error** for each loading condition. Plot the **calibration curve** and the **error curve**, and determine the **calibration factor** of the gauge.

---

##### **Model Solution for Q-A (a):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Construction and Working of a Bourdon Tube Pressure Gauge:**
>
> A Bourdon tube gauge consists of the following main parts (Refer to Section 4.2 Block Diagram):
>
> 1. **Bourdon Tube:** A curved, flattened metal tube (usually phosphor bronze, beryllium copper, or stainless steel) of **elliptical cross-section**, bent into a C-shape or spiral. One end is **fixed** to the gauge inlet; the other end is **free** and sealed.
>
> 2. **Operating Mechanism:** A **link–lever** arrangement connects the free end of the Bourdon tube to a **sector gear**, which meshes with a **pinion gear** mounted on the pointer spindle.
>
> 3. **Pointer and Dial:** A pointer attached to the pinion shaft moves over a circular graduated dial to indicate pressure.
>
> 4. **Hair Spring:** A small spiral spring (typically beryllium copper) attached to the pinion shaft. It provides a small **counter-torque** so that:
>    - The pointer returns to zero immediately when pressure is removed.
>    - Gear backlash (play) is taken up, eliminating zero shift.
>
> 5. **Damping Dashpot:** A small piston-cylinder filled with viscous oil, connected to the lever. It **damps out rapid oscillations** of the pointer caused by pulsating pressures, allowing steady, readable indications.
>
> **Working:**
> - The pressurized fluid enters the Bourdon tube and tries to straighten it (since the elliptical cross-section tends to become circular under internal pressure).
> - The free tip of the tube deflects outward by a small distance.
> - This deflection is amplified by the link and lever, rotated through the gear train, and displayed as a pressure reading on the dial.
> - When the pressure is removed, the tube's elasticity restores it to its original curved shape, and the hair spring pulls the pointer back to zero.

---

##### **Model Solution for Q-A (b):**

**Step 1 — Calculate true pressure using $p_{\text{true}} = \dfrac{m \cdot g}{A_e \cdot 10^5}$ in bar.**

With $A_e = 0.5 \ \text{cm}^2 = 5 \times 10^{-5} \ \text{m}^2$ and $g = 9.81 \ \text{m/s}^2$:

For $m = 2 \ \text{kg}$:

$$p_{\text{true}} = \frac{2 \times 9.81}{5 \times 10^{-5} \times 10^5} = \frac{19.62}{5} = 3.924 \ \text{bar}$$

For $m = 4 \ \text{kg}$:

$$p_{\text{true}} = \frac{4 \times 9.81}{5} = 7.848 \ \text{bar}$$

For $m = 6 \ \text{kg}$:

$$p_{\text{true}} = \frac{6 \times 9.81}{5} = 11.772 \ \text{bar}$$

For $m = 8 \ \text{kg}$:

$$p_{\text{true}} = \frac{8 \times 9.81}{5} = 15.696 \ \text{bar}$$

For $m = 10 \ \text{kg}$:

$$p_{\text{true}} = \frac{10 \times 9.81}{5} = 19.620 \ \text{bar}$$

**Step 2 — Tabulate Error and Percentage Error.**

| Weights (kg) | $p_{\text{true}}$ (bar) | $p_{\text{ind}}$ (bar) | $\Delta p = p_{\text{ind}} - p_{\text{true}}$ (bar) | $\%\text{Error}$ |
|--------------|--------------------------|--------------------------|------------------------------------------------------|------------------|
| 0            | 0.000                    | 0.00                     | 0.000                                                 | 0.00%            |
| 2            | 3.924                    | 3.80                     | $-0.124$                                              | $-3.16\%$        |
| 4            | 7.848                    | 7.65                     | $-0.198$                                              | $-2.52\%$        |
| 6            | 11.772                   | 11.50                    | $-0.272$                                              | $-2.31\%$        |
| 8            | 15.696                   | 15.30                    | $-0.396$                                              | $-2.52\%$        |
| 10           | 19.620                   | 19.20                    | $-0.420$                                              | $-2.14\%$        |

**Step 3 — Plot the Calibration Curve.**
- X-axis: $p_{\text{true}}$ (bar) from 0 to 20.
- Y-axis: $p_{\text{ind}}$ (bar) from 0 to 20.
- Plot the six points and join with a smooth curve.
- The curve lies below the ideal 45° reference line, showing that the gauge under-reads throughout.

**Step 4 — Plot the Error Curve.**
- X-axis: $p_{\text{true}}$ (bar) from 0 to 20.
- Y-axis: $\Delta p$ (bar), from $-0.5$ to $0$.
- All points lie below the x-axis, indicating a **negative systematic error** that grows in magnitude with pressure.

**Step 5 — Determine the Calibration Factor.**

Using the maximum-scale readings:

$$\text{CF} = \frac{p_{\text{true, max}}}{p_{\text{ind, max}}} = \frac{19.620}{19.20} = 1.0219$$

**Result:** The corrected pressure for any indicated reading is $p_{\text{corrected}} = 1.0219 \times p_{\text{ind}}$.

**Conclusion:** The gauge is **not within standard accuracy** (typical industrial grade is $\pm 1\%$ of full scale) and should be **recalibrated, re-zeroed, or replaced**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — June 2022] — *Internal Choice Alternative*

**(a)** [7 Marks] Describe with a **neat block diagram** the **construction and working of a Dead Weight Pressure Tester**. List the **advantages and limitations** of the DWT.

**(b)** [7 Marks] During calibration of a pressure gauge using a DWT with piston area $0.8 \ \text{cm}^2$, the following readings were obtained:

| Load (kg) | Gauge reading — Increasing (bar) | Gauge reading — Decreasing (bar) |
|-----------|----------------------------------|----------------------------------|
| 0         | 0.00                             | 0.00                             |
| 4         | 4.85                             | 4.90                             |
| 8         | 9.70                             | 9.80                             |
| 12        | 14.55                            | 14.70                            |
| 16        | 19.40                            | 19.60                            |
| 20        | 24.30                            | 24.55                            |

Determine: (i) the **true pressure** for each load, (ii) the **hysteresis error** at each load, (iii) the **average percentage error**, and (iv) the **calibration factor**.

---

##### **Model Solution for Q-B (a):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Dead Weight Pressure Tester (DWT) — Construction and Working:**
>
> **Main Parts (Refer to Section 4.1 Block Diagram):**
> 1. **Piston and Cylinder Assembly:** A precision-ground piston fits closely into a hardened steel cylinder. The clearance is so tight (a few microns) that fluid leakage is negligible but the piston can still float freely.
> 2. **Weight Carrier Platform:** A tray on top of the piston to stack slotted weights.
> 3. **Standard Slotted Weights:** Highly accurate, NABL-traceable weights in denominations (typically 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1, 2, 5 kg) that can be stacked on the platform.
> 4. **Hydraulic Oil Reservoir:** Connected through a hand pump (screw plunger) to generate pressure and a bleed valve to release it.
> 5. **Outlet Connection:** A high-pressure hose connects the DWT to the gauge under test.
> 6. **Rotating Spindle:** A handle to gently rotate the piston during measurement — this breaks static friction and ensures the piston floats freely.
>
> **Working Principle:**
> - The hand pump forces oil into the cylinder, pushing the piston up against gravity.
> - Standard weights placed on the platform exert a downward force $F = m \cdot g$.
> - When the piston floats freely (rotated manually to eliminate friction), the fluid pressure balances the weight:
>
> $$p_{\text{true}} = \frac{m \cdot g}{A_e}$$
>
> - By adding or removing weights and pumping oil accordingly, any desired pressure can be accurately generated.
>
> **Advantages:**
> 1. Highest accuracy available (primary standard, $\pm 0.05\%$ of reading).
> 2. Pressure is directly traceable to mass, length, and time — the SI base quantities.
> 3. Wide range (from 0.1 bar to 1000+ bar) using different piston-cylinder sets.
> 4. Long-term stability — no electronic components to drift.
> 5. Suitable for calibrating gauges, transducers, and pressure switches.
>
> **Limitations:**
> 1. Slow and labour-intensive — cannot be automated easily.
> 2. Sensitive to local gravity — must apply local $g$ correction.
> 3. Temperature variations cause oil viscosity and dimensional changes.
> 4. Requires very clean oil and a vibration-free horizontal surface.
> 5. Piston-cylinder wear over time degrades the effective area.

---

##### **Model Solution for Q-B (b):**

**Step 1 — Compute True Pressure using $A_e = 0.8 \ \text{cm}^2 = 8 \times 10^{-5} \ \text{m}^2$.**

For $m = 4 \ \text{kg}$:

$$p_{\text{true}} = \frac{4 \times 9.81}{8 \times 10^{-5} \times 10^5} = \frac{39.24}{8} = 4.905 \ \text{bar}$$

For $m = 8 \ \text{kg}$:

$$p_{\text{true}} = \frac{8 \times 9.81}{8} = 9.810 \ \text{bar}$$

For $m = 12 \ \text{kg}$:

$$p_{\text{true}} = \frac{12 \times 9.81}{8} = 14.715 \ \text{bar}$$

For $m = 16 \ \text{kg}$:

$$p_{\text{true}} = \frac{16 \times 9.81}{8} = 19.620 \ \text{bar}$$

For $m = 20 \ \text{kg}$:

$$p_{\text{true}} = \frac{20 \times 9.81}{8} = 24.525 \ \text{bar}$$

**Step 2 — Hysteresis Error at Each Load = $\vert p_{\text{inc}} - p_{\text{dec}} \vert$.**

| $m$ (kg) | $p_{\text{true}}$ (bar) | $p_{\text{inc}}$ (bar) | $p_{\text{dec}}$ (bar) | Hysteresis (bar) |
|----------|--------------------------|-------------------------|-------------------------|------------------|
| 0        | 0.000                    | 0.00                    | 0.00                    | 0.000            |
| 4        | 4.905                    | 4.85                    | 4.90                    | 0.050            |
| 8        | 9.810                    | 9.70                    | 9.80                    | 0.100            |
| 12       | 14.715                   | 14.55                   | 14.70                   | 0.150            |
| 16       | 19.620                   | 19.40                   | 19.60                   | 0.200            |
| 20       | 24.525                   | 24.30                   | 24.55                   | 0.250            |

**Step 3 — Average Percentage Error (using the increasing run).**

$$\%\text{Error at 4 kg} = \left(\frac{4.85 - 4.905}{4.905}\right) \times 100 = -1.12\%$$

$$\%\text{Error at 8 kg} = \left(\frac{9.70 - 9.810}{9.810}\right) \times 100 = -1.12\%$$

$$\%\text{Error at 12 kg} = \left(\frac{14.55 - 14.715}{14.715}\right) \times 100 = -1.12\%$$

$$\%\text{Error at 16 kg} = \left(\frac{19.40 - 19.620}{19.620}\right) \times 100 = -1.12\%$$

$$\%\text{Error at 20 kg} = \left(\frac{24.30 - 24.525}{24.525}\right) \times 100 = -0.92\%$$

$$\text{Average }\%\text{Error} = \frac{-1.12 - 1.12 - 1.12 - 1.12 - 0.92}{5} = -1.08\%$$

**Step 4 — Calibration Factor (using average values).**

$$\text{CF} = \frac{p_{\text{true, avg}}}{p_{\text{ind, avg}}} = \frac{(4.905+9.810+14.715+19.620+24.525)/5}{(4.85+9.70+14.55+19.40+24.30)/5}$$

$$= \frac{14.715}{14.56} = 1.01065$$

**Step 5 — Result Interpretation.**

- The gauge has a **systematic negative bias** of about **$-1.08\%$**, which is just outside the typical $\pm 1\%$ industrial accuracy class.
- The **hysteresis grows linearly with pressure** (0.05 bar per 4 kg step), pointing to **mechanical friction in the gear train** or **elastic after-effect in the Bourdon tube**.
- The recommended actions are: **(i)** lubricate the movement, **(ii)** re-zero the gauge, and **(iii)** if the error persists, **send the gauge to a NABL-accredited calibration lab** for adjustment.

---

### 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Calibration of Pressure Gauges**

**Core Definitions:**
- ☐ Calibration = comparison of gauge reading against a known reference standard.
- ☐ Dead Weight Tester = primary standard that generates true pressure from standard weights.
- ☐ Bourdon Tube = the curved elliptical-cross-section sensing element of an industrial pressure gauge.

**Critical Formulas to Memorize:**
- ☐ $p_{\text{true}} = \dfrac{m \cdot g}{A_e}$ (heart of the DWT equation)
- ☐ $\text{Error} = p_{\text{ind}} - p_{\text{true}}$
- ☐ $\%\text{Error} = \left(\dfrac{\text{Error}}{p_{\text{true}}}\right) \times 100$
- ☐ $\text{Calibration Factor} = \dfrac{p_{\text{true}}}{p_{\text{ind}}}$
- ☐ $\text{Hysteresis} = \vert p_{\text{inc}} - p_{\text{dec}} \vert$

**Experimental Procedure (must-do in this order):**
- ☐ Check zero of gauge → record zero error.
- ☐ Connect gauge to DWT, prime the oil, close bleed valve.
- ☐ Add weights incrementally (5–6 steps from 0 to full scale).
- ☐ Wait for piston to float freely (rotate spindle gently).
- ☐ Record $p_{\text{ind}}$ for **both** increasing and decreasing runs.
- ☐ Tabulate $m$, $p_{\text{true}}$, $p_{\text{ind}}$, error, %error, hysteresis.
- ☐ Plot calibration curve ($p_{\text{ind}}$ vs. $p_{\text{true}}$).
- ☐ Plot error curve ($\Delta p$ vs. $p_{\text{true}}$).
- ☐ Compute calibration factor and maximum hysteresis.
- ☐ State whether the gauge is fit for use or needs servicing.

**Sources of Error in Experiment:**
- ☐ Zero mis-setting of the gauge.
- ☐ Parallax while reading the dial.
- ☐ Friction in DWT piston / gauge movement.
- ☐ Air bubbles in oil line.
- ☐ Local gravity variation (use $g = 9.81 \ \text{m/s}^2$ for Kerala).
- ☐ Worn or non-concentric piston-cylinder.

**Engineering Relevance:**
- ☐ Safety in boilers, gas cylinders, hydraulic systems.
- ☐ Quality compliance (ISO 9001, NABL).
- ☐ Product consistency in process industries.
- ☐ Research data integrity.

**Exam Strategy Tips:**
- ☐ Always carry out **unit conversion** in the first step of every numerical problem.
- ☐ Mention the **value of $g$ used** explicitly in your solution.
- ☐ Draw **both** curves with **labeled axes and units**.
- ☐ Always compute and report **hysteresis** if both increasing and decreasing runs are given.
- ☐ State a **conclusion** — whether the gauge is accurate, accurate-with-correction, or rejected.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of Metacentric Height and Radius of Gyration of Floating Bodies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCEL308 - FLUID MECHANICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of Metacentric Height and Radius of Gyration of Floating Bodies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE EXPERIMENT 2 — DETERMINATION OF METACENTRIC HEIGHT AND RADIUS OF GYRATION OF FLOATING BODIES

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> The **metacentric height (GM)** of a floating body is the vertical distance between the **Centre of Gravity (G)** of the body and the **Metacentre (M)**. The **metacentre (M)** is the point of intersection of the line of action of the buoyant force (vertical line through the new centre of buoyancy B′) with the original vertical line passing through the centroid of the body (line through the initial centre of buoyancy B and centre of gravity G), when the body is given a small angular displacement.

The **radius of gyration (k)** of the waterline area of a floating body is defined as the distance from the axis of rotation (about which the body tilts) at which the entire area of the waterline plane may be assumed to be concentrated to give the same **second moment of area (I)** as the actual distributed area.

Mathematically:

$$k = \sqrt{\frac{I}{A}}$$

where:
- **I** = Second moment of area of the waterline plane about the longitudinal axis of rotation (m⁴)
- **A** = Area of the waterline plane (m²)
- **k** = Radius of gyration of the waterline area (m)

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The Floating Tilt Test:**
> Imagine placing a rectangular wooden block (a "pontoon") in a water trough. It floats happily because the upward push of water (buoyancy) exactly balances the downward weight. Now, place a small movable weight (called a **jockey weight**) near one edge of the block. The block tips slightly. Water gets pushed away from the heavy side and accumulates on the lighter side. The new centre of buoyancy shifts toward the heavier side because more volume is submerged there. The line of this new buoyant force, when extended upward, cuts the central vertical axis of the body at a point M — the **Metacentre**.
>
> The distance between the centre of gravity G (where the body's own weight acts) and this Metacentre M is the **metacentric height (GM)**. A small GM means the body is "tippy" and unstable (like a narrow racing canoe). A large GM means the body is stable and self-righting (like a wide flat barge). A negative GM means the body will capsize (like a tall, top-heavy buoy).

The relationship tying together metacentric height, radius of gyration, and the body geometry is the **classic stability equation of floating bodies**:

$$GM = \frac{I}{V_{sub}} - BG = \frac{k^{2} \cdot A}{V_{sub}} - BG$$

This is the **single most important equation** in the KTU Fluid Mechanics Lab for this experiment.

| Symbol | Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **GM** | Metacentric height | m |
| **I** | Second moment of waterline area about tilt axis | m⁴ |
| **A** | Waterline plane area | m² |
| **k²** | Square of radius of gyration = I/A | m² |
| **V_sub** | Submerged (displaced) volume of the body | m³ |
| **B** | Centre of buoyancy (centroid of submerged volume) | — |
| **G** | Centre of gravity of the floating body | — |
| **M** | Metacentre | — |
| **BG** | Distance between B and G (positive if G is above B) | m |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physical Setup of the Experiment

In the KTU lab, the apparatus consists of:

1. A **rectangular pontoon** (holow metal or wooden box) of known dimensions — length L, breadth B, height H — which floats in a **rectangular water trough**.
2. A **movable jockey weight (w)** that can be slid along the length of the pontoon.
3. A **pointer or vertical index** attached to the pontoon that moves over a **graduated arc / scale** to measure the angle of tilt θ.
4. A **graduated scale on the trough** to measure the vertical immersion depth (h) of the pontoon.
5. The pontoon has a **central plumb line** (or vertical hairline) to confirm the body returns to the vertical.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

1. **Step 1 — Initial Setup:** The empty pontoon is floated in the trough. It floats upright. Note the depth of immersion h. The waterline plane is a rectangle of dimensions L × B, so its area is **A = L × B**.
2. **Step 2 — Place Jockey Weight:** The jockey weight **w** is placed at the centre of the pontoon. The body must remain perfectly level (no tilt). The total downward weight is now **W + w**, where W is the weight of the pontoon. The submerged depth increases, but the body is still symmetrical.
3. **Step 3 — Shift the Jockey Weight:** The jockey weight is moved by a known distance **d** along the length of the pontoon (toward one edge). The body tilts by a small angle **θ**. This tilt is measured using the graduated arc.
4. **Step 4 — Measure Tilt:** The angle θ is read from the graduated arc (or by measuring the rise/fall of one corner using a vernier).
5. **Step 5 — Repeat:** The experiment is repeated for different values of d (e.g., 2 cm, 4 cm, 6 cm, 8 cm, 10 cm on each side), and corresponding θ values are noted.
6. **Step 6 — Compute GM:** For each reading, GM is calculated. The mean GM is reported.

## 2.3 Derivation of the Experimental Formula

Let us derive the formula that connects **w, d, W, θ, and GM**.

Consider the pontoon in its tilted position. The body is in rotational equilibrium under the action of:
- The total weight **W + w** acting vertically downward through the combined centre of gravity G.
- The buoyant force **W + w** acting vertically upward through the new centre of buoyancy B′.

For equilibrium, the moment of the applied disturbing couple (caused by shifting w through distance d) about the centre of buoyancy B must equal the restoring couple due to the metacentric height GM.

**Disturbing couple** = w × d (the jockey weight is displaced from the central vertical by distance d, and only this offset contributes to the tilting moment since the body weight W acts at the centre).

**Restoring couple** = (W + w) × GM × sin θ ≈ (W + w) × GM × tan θ (for small angles, sin θ ≈ tan θ).

Equating the two couples for equilibrium:

$$w \times d = (W + w) \times GM \times \tan\theta$$

Rearranging to obtain the **KTU working formula for GM**:

$$GM = \frac{w \times d}{(W + w) \times \tan\theta}$$

In most KTU lab manuals, the **jockey weight w is much smaller than W** (typically w ≈ 5–10 % of W), so it is customary to use the **approximate form**:

$$\boxed{\;GM \approx \frac{w \times d}{W \times \tan\theta}\;}$$

## 2.4 Computation of the Radius of Gyration

Once GM, the submerged volume V_sub, and the distance BG are known, the radius of gyration is found from the **stability equation**:

$$GM = \frac{k^{2} \cdot A}{V_{sub}} - BG$$

Rearranging for k²:

$$k^{2} = \frac{(GM + BG) \times V_{sub}}{A}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\;k = \sqrt{\frac{(GM + BG) \times V_{sub}}{A}}\;}$$

For a rectangular pontoon of length L and breadth B (in the waterline plane):

- **A = L × B** (waterline area)
- **I = L × B³ / 12** (second moment of area about the longitudinal axis through the centroid, when tilting about the longitudinal axis)
- **k²_calculated = I / A = B² / 12** (theoretical value, depends only on breadth)

The **experimental value of k** from the floating test should be compared with the **theoretical value B/√12**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why two different k values?**
> In the experimental approach, we compute k from the stability equation using **measured GM** and **measured immersion depth**. In the theoretical approach, k is purely a function of the **waterline geometry** (breadth). The closeness of these two values is the **validity check** the examiner expects you to mention in the viva.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Used For |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $A = L \times B$ | Waterline area of pontoon | Computing k² |
| 2 | $I = \dfrac{L \times B^{3}}{12}$ | Second moment of waterline area (tilt about long axis) | Theoretical k |
| 3 | $k^{2} = \dfrac{I}{A} = \dfrac{B^{2}}{12}$ | Theoretical k² | Validity check |
| 4 | $V_{sub} = A \times h = L \times B \times h$ | Submerged volume | Stability equation |
| 5 | $BG = \overline{y} - \dfrac{h}{2}$ | Distance between B and G | Stability equation |
| 6 | $GM = \dfrac{w \times d}{(W + w) \times \tan\theta}$ | Working formula (exact) | Direct computation |
| 7 | $GM \approx \dfrac{w \times d}{W \times \tan\theta}$ | Working formula (approximate, w ≪ W) | Lab computation |
| 8 | $k = \sqrt{\dfrac{(GM + BG) \times V_{sub}}{A}}$ | Experimental radius of gyration | Final result |
| 9 | $\theta = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{\text{rise or fall}}{\text{half-length}}\right)$ | Tilt angle | Measuring θ |
| 10 | $\rho_{water} = 1000 \; \text{kg/m}^{3}$ | Density of fresh water | Computing W if needed |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Ship Design:** Naval architects compute GM to ensure ships are stable but not too stiff (a very high GM makes a ship roll uncomfortably).
- **Submarine Design:** Ballast tanks are used to deliberately shift G and thus control GM during submersion.
- **Floating Docks & Pontoon Bridges:** GM must exceed a minimum to ensure they don't capsize under uneven loading.
- **Stability of Buoys & Offshore Platforms:** Radius of gyration of the waterline area directly determines roll stability in waves.
- **Hydraulic Models:** Scaled ship models are tested for GM before full-scale construction.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Full Derivation of the Working Formula (GM = wd / (W tanθ))

### Step 1 — Free-Body Diagram of the Tilted Pontoon

When the jockey weight **w** is displaced by a distance **d** from the central vertical axis of the pontoon, the pontoon tilts by a small angle **θ** about a longitudinal axis passing through the centre of buoyancy B (approximately). The forces acting are:

- The total weight **W + w** acting vertically downward through the new combined centre of gravity G.
- The buoyant force **F_B = (W + w)** acting vertically upward through the **new** centre of buoyancy B′.

### Step 2 — Take Moments About the Centre of Buoyancy B (in the un-tilted position)

Taking moments about the original centre of buoyancy B (which is a fixed point for small angular displacements), and recognizing that the buoyant force has no moment about B itself:

The **disturbing moment** (tending to tilt the body further) is caused solely by the offset of the jockey weight w from the body's central vertical axis:

$$M_{disturbing} = w \times d$$

The **restoring moment** (tending to right the body) is produced by the upward buoyant force acting at the new centre B′, which is now displaced horizontally by a distance **GM × sin θ** from the original vertical through B (this is the very definition of the metacentre M being GM above G, and B′ being aligned with M):

$$M_{restoring} = (W + w) \times GM \times \sin\theta$$

### Step 3 — Apply Rotational Equilibrium

For the body to be in equilibrium at angle θ:

$$M_{disturbing} = M_{restoring}$$

$$w \times d = (W + w) \times GM \times \sin\theta$$

### Step 4 — Small-Angle Approximation

For small angles of tilt (θ < 10°, which is the case in the lab):

$$\sin\theta \approx \tan\theta \approx \theta \text{ (in radians)}$$

So:

$$w \times d = (W + w) \times GM \times \tan\theta$$

### Step 5 — Solve for GM

Rearranging:

$$GM = \frac{w \times d}{(W + w) \times \tan\theta}$$

**Exact form.** When w ≪ W:

$$GM \approx \frac{w \times d}{W \times \tan\theta}$$

**Approximate form used in KTU labs.** This completes the derivation.

## 3.2 Full Derivation of the Stability Equation (GM = I/V - BG)

This is a **classical result from ship hydrostatics** that the KTU examiner expects you to know.

### Step 1 — Geometry of Tilt

When the floating body tilts by a small angle θ about a longitudinal axis OO′ through the centroid of the waterline area, the centre of buoyancy moves from B to B′. The wedge-shaped volume W1 (emerged on one side) and W2 (immersed on the other side) are equal in magnitude because the weight of the body is unchanged, so the displaced volume remains the same.

Let:
- **OO′** = axis of tilt (longitudinal axis through the centroid of the waterline area).
- **x** = horizontal distance of an elementary strip dA from the axis OO′.
- **dV** = elementary volume submerged due to the tilt = x · θ · dA (this is a first-order approximation valid for small θ).

### Step 2 — Horizontal Shift of Centre of Buoyancy

The horizontal shift BB′ (let's call it **BB̄** to avoid confusion with body length) is obtained by taking the first moment of the wedge volumes about the axis OO′ and dividing by the total submerged volume V_sub:

$$BB\bar{} = \frac{\int x \, dV}{V_{sub}} = \frac{\int x \cdot (x\theta \, dA)}{V_{sub}} = \frac{\theta \int x^{2} \, dA}{V_{sub}}$$

But $\int x^{2} \, dA = I$, the **second moment of area** of the waterline plane about the axis of tilt.

Therefore:

$$BB\bar{} = \frac{I \cdot \theta}{V_{sub}}$$

### Step 3 — Identify the Metacentre

The metacentre M lies on the line of action of the new buoyant force (through B′) and on the original vertical through B. By definition of GM:

$$\tan\theta = \frac{BB\bar{}}{GM}$$

For small angles, tan θ ≈ θ:

$$\theta = \frac{BB\bar{}}{GM} = \frac{I \cdot \theta}{V_{sub} \cdot GM}$$

### Step 4 — Solve for GM

Cross-multiplying:

$$GM \cdot \theta \cdot V_{sub} = I \cdot \theta$$

$$\boxed{\;GM = \frac{I}{V_{sub}}\;}$$

This gives the **metacentric height measured from the centre of buoyancy B**. If we wish to measure it from the centre of gravity G, we must account for the distance BG:

$$GM_{from\,G} = \frac{I}{V_{sub}} - BG = \frac{k^{2} \cdot A}{V_{sub}} - BG$$

This is the **complete stability equation**.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board-Style)

> **Problem:** A rectangular pontoon of length **L = 0.60 m**, breadth **B = 0.30 m**, weight **W = 18.0 N** floats in water. A jockey weight of **w = 2.0 N** is moved by **d = 0.08 m** from the centre along the length, causing the pontoon to tilt by an angle **θ = 5°**. Find: (a) the metacentric height GM, (b) the radius of gyration k of the waterline plane, given that the depth of immersion h = 0.05 m. Take g = 9.81 m/s².

### Part (a): Metacentric Height

The **exact** working formula:

$$GM = \frac{w \times d}{(W + w) \times \tan\theta}$$

Calculate tan(5°):

$$\tan(5°) \approx 0.0875$$

Substitute:

$$GM = \frac{2.0 \times 0.08}{(18.0 + 2.0) \times 0.0875}$$

$$GM = \frac{0.16}{20.0 \times 0.0875} = \frac{0.16}{1.750}$$

$$\boxed{GM = 0.0914 \text{ m} \approx 9.14 \text{ cm}}$$

### Part (b): Radius of Gyration

First, compute the waterline area:

$$A = L \times B = 0.60 \times 0.30 = 0.18 \; \text{m}^{2}$$

Submerged volume:

$$V_{sub} = A \times h = 0.18 \times 0.05 = 0.009 \; \text{m}^{3}$$

Distance BG (the body is a uniform hollow box, so its CG is at the geometric centre. The centre of buoyancy is at the centroid of the submerged depth, which is at h/2 from the bottom. For a uniform pontoon, G is at H/2 from the bottom.):

$$BG = \frac{H}{2} - \frac{h}{2}$$

(For this example, we'll assume BG = 0.02 m after geometric analysis of the pontoon.)

Now apply the stability equation to find k:

$$GM = \frac{k^{2} \cdot A}{V_{sub}} - BG$$

$$0.0914 = \frac{k^{2} \times 0.18}{0.009} - 0.02$$

$$0.0914 + 0.02 = 20 \, k^{2}$$

$$0.1114 = 20 \, k^{2}$$

$$k^{2} = 0.00557 \; \text{m}^{2}$$

$$\boxed{k = 0.0746 \; \text{m} \approx 7.46 \; \text{cm}}$$

### Validity Check — Theoretical Radius of Gyration

$$k_{theoretical} = \frac{B}{\sqrt{12}} = \frac{0.30}{3.464} = 0.0866 \; \text{m}$$

The experimental value (7.46 cm) and theoretical value (8.66 cm) should be in reasonable agreement. Any deviation is attributed to experimental error (friction, meniscus, tilt measurement inaccuracy).

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Sources of Error & Their Effects

| Source of Error | Effect on GM | Effect on k | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Reading θ on a curved arc | Under/over-estimation | Propagated error | Use vernier scale; take multiple readings |
| Surface tension at pontoon edges | Slight extra meniscus pull | Small positive bias in W | Wet pontoon sides; use sharp edges |
| Pontoon not returning to level | Inconsistent zero | Systematic error | Re-level trough before each reading |
| Jockey weight not at exact centre | Asymmetric initial tilt | Skewed tan θ | Use plumb line to verify centre |
| Parallax while reading scale | Random error | Random error | Eye level perpendicular to scale |
| Temperature variation of water | Changes ρ slightly | Changes V_sub | Use water at room temperature |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Experimental Setup

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA[Water Trough Filled with Water] --> nodeB[Rectangular Pontoon Floats Upright]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Immersion Depth h is measured using side scale]
    nodeB --> nodeD[Plumb Line Confirms Verticality]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Jockey Weight w placed at Centre]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Pontoon Remains Level Total Weight W + w]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Jockey Weight Shifted by Distance d to one side]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Pontoon Tilts by Angle theta]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Angle theta read from Graduated Arc]
    nodeH --> nodeJ[GM Computed using wd over W tan theta]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[k Computed from Stability Equation]
    nodeK --> nodeL[Compare with Theoretical k equals B over root twelve]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Force and Moment Analysis of Tilted Pontoon

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Total Weight W plus w] -->|Acts at G downward| nodeB[Original Vertical through B]
    nodeC[Buoyant Force F sub B equals W plus w] -->|Acts at B prime upward| nodeD[New Vertical Tilted Line]
    nodeD -->|Intersects Original Vertical at| nodeM[Metacentre M]
    nodeA -->|GM is distance from G to| nodeM
    nodeE[Jockey Weight w Offset by d] -->|Creates Disturbing Moment w times d| nodeF[Rotational Equilibrium]
    nodeM -->|Creates Restoring Moment W plus w times GM times sin theta| nodeF
    nodeF -->|Equation w times d equals W plus w times GM times tan theta| nodeG[Solve for GM]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Position of B, G, and M in Stable and Unstable Bodies

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeS[Start Floating Body Analysis] --> nodeC1{Centre of Gravity G Position}
    nodeC1 -->|G is Below M| nodeS1[STABLE Body GM positive Self Righting]
    nodeC1 -->|G coincides with M| nodeS2[NEUTRALLY STABLE Body GM equals zero]
    nodeC1 -->|G is Above M| nodeS3[UNSTABLE Body GM negative Capsizes]
    nodeS1 --> nodeE1[Example Wide Barge Large Breadth Large k Positive GM]
    nodeS2 --> nodeE2[Example Sphere of Uniform Density Marginal Stability]
    nodeS3 --> nodeE3[Example Tall Narrow Buoy Top Heavy Negative GM]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — Logical Flow of Computations

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    node1[Measure L and B of Pontoon] --> node2[Compute Waterline Area A equals L times B]
    node1 --> node3[Compute Theoretical k equals B over root twelve]
    node4[Weigh Pontoon to get W] --> node5[Place Jockey Weight w at Centre]
    node5 --> node6[Measure Immersion Depth h]
    node6 --> node7[Compute V sub equals A times h]
    node7 --> node8[Compute BG equals half H minus half h]
    node9[Shift Jockey by d and Read theta] --> node10[Compute tan theta]
    node10 --> node11[Compute GM equals w times d over W plus w times tan theta]
    node11 --> node12[Compute k experimental equals square root of GM plus BG times V sub over A]
    node3 --> node13[Compare Experimental and Theoretical k]
    node12 --> node13
    node13 --> node14[Report Mean GM and Mean k with Validity Check]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Define metacentric height. What is its physical significance in determining the stability of a floating body?**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Metacentric height (GM)** is defined as the distance between the **centre of gravity (G)** of a floating body and the **metacentre (M)**, where the metacentre is the point at which the line of action of the new buoyant force (after a small angular displacement) intersects the original vertical line through the centre of gravity and the initial centre of buoyancy.

**Physical significance:**
- If **GM > 0** (i.e., M is above G): The body is **stable** and will return to its upright position after a small tilt. The restoring couple is proportional to GM.
- If **GM = 0**: The body is in **neutral equilibrium**; it remains in the tilted position.
- If **GM < 0** (i.e., M is below G): The body is **unstable** and will capsize.

A higher GM indicates greater initial stability but a shorter rolling period (stiffer ship), while a smaller GM gives a longer, more comfortable roll but risks instability. **(3 Marks)**

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**What is meant by the radius of gyration of the waterline area of a floating body? Write its formula.**

**Model Answer:**

The **radius of gyration (k)** of the waterline area of a floating body is defined as the distance from the axis of rotation (about which the body tilts) at which the **entire waterline area** may be assumed to be concentrated such that its **second moment of area** about the axis equals that of the actual distributed area.

**Formula:**

$$k = \sqrt{\frac{I}{A}}$$

where:
- **I** = second moment of area of the waterline plane about the axis of rotation
- **A** = area of the waterline plane

For a rectangular pontoon of length L and breadth B tilting about its longitudinal axis:

$$k = \frac{B}{\sqrt{12}}$$

**Significance:** The radius of gyration determines the body's **initial transverse stability**; a larger k (i.e., a wider waterline plane) produces a larger metacentric radius BM and hence a larger GM. **(3 Marks)**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2]`

**(a)** Derive the relationship between the metacentric height (GM), the second moment of area of the waterline plane (I), the submerged volume (V), and the distance BG. Clearly state the assumptions used. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** In an experiment to determine the metacentric height of a rectangular pontoon, the following data were obtained:

| S.No | Jockey weight shift d (m) | Angle of tilt θ (degrees) |
|------|---------------------------|---------------------------|
| 1 | 0.02 | 1.5 |
| 2 | 0.04 | 3.0 |
| 3 | 0.06 | 4.5 |
| 4 | 0.08 | 6.0 |
| 5 | 0.10 | 7.5 |

Weight of pontoon **W = 25.0 N**, jockey weight **w = 1.5 N**, length **L = 0.50 m**, breadth **B = 0.25 m**, immersion depth **h = 0.04 m**.

Calculate the **mean metacentric height GM** and the **radius of gyration k** of the waterline plane. Take g = 9.81 m/s². **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks):**

Assumption: small angular displacement θ, pontoon treated as a rigid body, fluid is at rest.

Consider a floating body that tilts by a small angle θ about a longitudinal axis OO′ passing through the centroid of the waterline area. The centre of buoyancy shifts from B to B′. The wedge of volume emerged on one side equals the wedge immersed on the other side (so the displaced volume V remains constant).

For an elementary strip of area dA at a distance x from the axis OO′, the volume dV submerged due to tilt is:

$$dV = x \cdot \theta \cdot dA$$

The horizontal shift of the centre of buoyancy is the first moment of these wedge volumes divided by V:

$$BB\bar{} = \frac{\int x \cdot dV}{V} = \frac{\theta \int x^{2} \, dA}{V} = \frac{I \cdot \theta}{V}$$

By definition of the metacentre M, the line BB′ extended meets the original vertical through G at M, and:

$$\tan\theta = \frac{BB\bar{}}{GM}$$

For small angles, $\tan\theta \approx \theta$:

$$\theta = \frac{I \cdot \theta}{V \cdot GM}$$

Therefore, the metacentric radius BM:

$$BM = \frac{I}{V_{sub}}$$

The metacentric height **measured from G** is:

$$\boxed{\;GM = \frac{I}{V_{sub}} - BG = \frac{k^{2} A}{V_{sub}} - BG\;}$$

This is the **classical stability equation of a floating body**.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute tan θ for each reading:**

| Reading | d (m) | θ (°) | tan θ | GM = (1.5 × d) / [(25 + 1.5) × tan θ] (m) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.02 | 1.5 | 0.02619 | (0.030) / (26.5 × 0.02619) = 0.0432 |
| 2 | 0.04 | 3.0 | 0.05241 | (0.060) / (26.5 × 0.05241) = 0.0432 |
| 3 | 0.06 | 4.5 | 0.07870 | (0.090) / (26.5 × 0.07870) = 0.0431 |
| 4 | 0.08 | 6.0 | 0.10510 | (0.120) / (26.5 × 0.10510) = 0.0431 |
| 5 | 0.10 | 7.5 | 0.13165 | (0.150) / (26.5 × 0.13165) = 0.0430 |

**Mean GM ≈ 0.0431 m = 4.31 cm**

**Step 2 — Compute the radius of gyration:**

Waterline area:

$$A = L \times B = 0.50 \times 0.25 = 0.125 \; \text{m}^{2}$$

Submerged volume:

$$V_{sub} = A \times h = 0.125 \times 0.04 = 0.0050 \; \text{m}^{3}$$

For a uniform hollow pontoon, assume the centre of gravity G is at the geometric centre, so:

$$BG = \frac{H}{2} - \frac{h}{2}$$

(For standard KTU pontoons, $H = 0.08$ m, giving $BG = 0.04 - 0.02 = 0.02$ m.)

Apply the stability equation:

$$GM = \frac{k^{2} \cdot A}{V_{sub}} - BG$$

$$0.0431 = \frac{k^{2} \times 0.125}{0.0050} - 0.02$$

$$0.0431 + 0.02 = 25 \, k^{2}$$

$$k^{2} = \frac{0.0631}{25} = 0.002524 \; \text{m}^{2}$$

$$\boxed{k = 0.0502 \; \text{m} = 5.02 \; \text{cm}}$$

**Step 3 — Validity Check (Theoretical k):**

$$k_{theory} = \frac{B}{\sqrt{12}} = \frac{0.25}{3.464} = 0.0722 \; \text{m} = 7.22 \; \text{cm}$$

The experimental value (5.02 cm) is reasonably close to the theoretical value (7.22 cm), with the deviation attributable to the simplification w ≪ W not being exact, meniscus effects, and tilt measurement tolerance. The order of magnitude is consistent, validating the experiment.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2]`

**(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the experimental procedure to determine the metacentric height of a rectangular floating body in the lab. List the apparatus required and the observations to be recorded. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A ship of weight **5000 tonnes** floats in seawater (specific gravity = 1.025). Its waterline area is **1200 m²** and the second moment of this area about the longitudinal axis is **6 × 10⁵ m⁴**. The centre of gravity is **0.4 m** above the centre of buoyancy. Determine: (i) the metacentric radius BM, (ii) the metacentric height GM, and (iii) the radius of gyration of the waterline area. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Experimental Procedure (7 Marks):**

**Apparatus Required:**
1. Rectangular pontoon (hollow metal box) of known dimensions L, B, H.
2. Rectangular water trough of suitable size.
3. Jockey weight (movable) with hook.
4. Graduated arc / scale for measuring angle of tilt.
5. Pointer / plumb line attached to the pontoon.
6. Measuring scale and vernier.
7. Weighing balance for the pontoon and jockey weight.

**Procedure:**
1. Note the dimensions of the pontoon (L, B, H) and weigh it accurately to get W.
2. Fill the water trough and float the empty pontoon. Adjust the water level so the pontoon floats freely.
3. Mark the waterline on the pontoon and measure the depth of immersion h using a side scale.
4. Place the jockey weight w at the centre of the pontoon. The pontoon should remain perfectly level (verify using the plumb line or by reading zero on the tilt scale).
5. Slide the jockey weight by a small known distance d (say 2 cm) along the length of the pontoon toward one end.
6. Allow the pontoon to settle and read the angle of tilt θ from the graduated arc.
7. Return the weight to the centre, allow the pontoon to rest, and then shift the weight to the other end by the same distance d. Note the new tilt angle (ideally the same as before in magnitude).
8. Repeat the experiment for different values of d (e.g., 2, 4, 6, 8, 10 cm on each side).
9. Tabulate the observations and compute GM for each reading using the formula $GM = (w \times d) / [(W + w) \times \tan\theta]$.
10. Find the mean GM and then compute the radius of gyration k.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Given:**
- W = 5000 tonnes = 5000 × 1000 × 9.81 = 4.905 × 10⁷ N (weight in newtons)
- ρ_sw = 1.025 × 1000 = 1025 kg/m³
- A = 1200 m²
- I = 6 × 10⁵ m⁴
- BG = 0.4 m

**Step 1 — Submerged Volume:**

By Archimedes' principle, the buoyant force equals the weight:

$$\rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot V_{sub} = W$$

$$V_{sub} = \frac{W}{\rho_{sw} \cdot g} = \frac{4.905 \times 10^{7}}{1025 \times 9.81} = \frac{4.905 \times 10^{7}}{10055.25}$$

$$V_{sub} = 4878.1 \; \text{m}^{3}$$

**Step 2 — Metacentric Radius BM:**

$$BM = \frac{I}{V_{sub}} = \frac{6 \times 10^{5}}{4878.1} = 123.0 \; \text{m}$$

**Step 3 — Metacentric Height GM:**

$$GM = BM - BG = 123.0 - 0.4 = 122.6 \; \text{m}$$

(Note: This is a very large GM, which is typical for a large ship.)

**Step 4 — Radius of Gyration:**

$$k^{2} = \frac{I}{A} = \frac{6 \times 10^{5}}{1200} = 500 \; \text{m}^{2}$$

$$\boxed{k = \sqrt{500} = 22.36 \; \text{m}}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to add W + w in the denominator.** Many students write $GM = w \cdot d / (W \cdot \tan\theta)$ and lose 1–2 marks for not stating the exact form. Use the **exact form** if the problem gives w, then add a comment on the approximation.
>
> 2. **Mixing up BG and GB.** Always state clearly: "BG is the distance from B to G, **positive when G is above B**." A negative BG means G is below B (rare in ships, common in loaded pontoons with ballast).
>
> 3. **Unit mismatch.** GM must be in **metres**. Convert d (cm) and any θ read in degrees to tan θ using a calculator — DO NOT use θ in radians directly. Kaluza and trig tables are obsolete.
>
> 4. **Forgetting to compare experimental k with theoretical k.** Always compute $k_{theory} = B/\sqrt{12}$ and state the **percentage error**. Examiners allocate **1 full mark** for the validity check.
>
> 5. **Sign convention for stability:** Always classify the body as **stable / neutral / unstable** based on the sign of GM. This is a compulsory concluding statement.
>
> 6. **Using sin θ instead of tan θ.** For very small angles, sin θ ≈ tan θ. But for θ > 10°, the difference becomes noticeable. Use tan θ consistently as per the derivation.
>
> 7. **Not including the W of jockey weight** when computing the **submerged volume** in numerical problems. If w is moved off-centre, the average immersion is the same, but the buoyant force equals (W + w).
>
> 8. **In the viva, you must explain** *why the centre of buoyancy shifts sideways on tilting* — the examiner will cut marks if you cannot state "due to the change in the shape of the displaced volume, the centroid of the submerged wedge changes."

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Metacentre (M):** Intersection of the new buoyant force line with the original vertical through the centre of gravity.
- **Metacentric Height (GM):** Distance from G to M — a direct measure of **initial transverse stability**.
- **Stability Equation:** $GM = I / V_{sub} - BG = (k^2 A) / V_{sub} - BG$.
- **Radius of Gyration (k):** $k = \sqrt{I/A}$; for a rectangle tilting about its long axis, $k = B / \sqrt{12}$.
- **Experimental Working Formula (exact):** $GM = w \cdot d / [(W + w) \cdot \tan\theta]$.
- **Experimental Working Formula (approximate, w ≪ W):** $GM \approx w \cdot d / [W \cdot \tan\theta]$.
- **Stability Criteria:**
  - GM > 0 → Stable
  - GM = 0 → Neutral
  - GM < 0 → Unstable (capsizes)
- **Procedure Highlights:** Place jockey at centre → shift by d → read θ → compute GM → repeat for multiple d → average → find k → compare with theoretical.
- **Validity Check:** Experimental k must be close to theoretical k (= B/√12 for a rectangle).
- **Real-World Applications:** Ship design, submarines, pontoon bridges, floating docks, offshore platforms.
- **Key Assumption:** Small angular displacement (sin θ ≈ tan θ ≈ θ in radians).
- **Density of fresh water:** 1000 kg/m³; seawater: 1025 kg/m³ (for numerical problems).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Verification of Bernoulli's Theorem
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCEL308 - FLUID MECHANICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Verification of Bernoulli's Theorem

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Bernoulli's Theorem** is the principal statement of the steady-flow energy balance for an ideal, incompressible, and non-viscous fluid moving along a streamline. It asserts that, in the absence of any external work input or loss, the sum of the **pressure head**, the **velocity head (kinetic head)**, and the **elevation head (potential head)** remains invariant between any two points on the same streamline.

Mathematically, for two points 1 and 2 on a streamline:

$$
\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^{2}}{2g} + z_1 \;=\; \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^{2}}{2g} + z_2
$$

For a real viscous fluid, a head-loss term $\,h_L\,$ is introduced on the right-hand side to account for frictional dissipation, making the equation:

$$
\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^{2}}{2g} + z_1 \;=\; \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^{2}}{2g} + z_2 + h_L
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PCCEL308 / Module 2):**
> The laboratory verification of Bernoulli's theorem is performed on a **converging pipe test section fitted with multiple static pressure tapping points (piezometer / manometer tappings)**. The total energy head is computed at each tapping and plotted against the axial distance to confirm the constancy (or near-constancy) of total head along the flow direction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Assumptions of Bernoulli's Theorem (Examinable):**
> 1. The fluid is **ideal (inviscid) and incompressible** ($\rho = \text{constant}$).
> 2. The flow is **steady** ($\partial / \partial t = 0$).
> 3. The flow is along a **single streamline** (or, in the extended form, irrotional).
> 4. **No shaft work** is done on or by the fluid (no pump or turbine between the points).
> 5. The fluid is **non-conducting** (no heat transfer effects).

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Think of a stream flowing down a mountainside. The water at the top of the mountain (high *elevation head*) is moving slowly, but as it descends the slope, the elevation drops while the speed picks up. Simultaneously, the pressure (the force the water exerts on the rocks and banks) changes as the cross-section narrows and widens. **Energy cannot simply vanish — it is continuously exchanged between three "forms": potential (height), kinetic (speed), and pressure (squeeze).** If you add all three at any cross-section, the total stays the same. That conserved sum is the essence of Bernoulli's theorem.

In the laboratory, the same idea is reproduced at a miniature scale inside a **converging transparent duct**: as the cross-sectional area shrinks, the velocity rises, the static pressure falls, and the sum — measured through piezometer columns — remains essentially unchanged.

## 1.3 Physical Constants / Standard Metrics Used

- Acceleration due to gravity, $g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^{2}$ (standard).
- Density of water at 25 °C, $\rho = 996\ \text{kg/m}^{3}$ (often taken as **1000 kg/m³** for ease).
- Specific weight of water, $\gamma = \rho g = 9810\ \text{N/m}^{3}$.
- Cross-sectional area of the test section at each tapping, $A_i = \frac{\pi}{4} D_i^{2}$ (m²), where $D_i$ is the local diameter.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept — How the Apparatus Verifies Bernoulli's Theorem

The experimental rig is a **closed-loop water circuit** comprising:

1. A **sump / overhead tank** supplying water at a steady head.
2. A **calibrated converging test section** (typically 3 or 4 tappings) machined with a gradually reducing cross-section. Each tapping is connected to a **piezometer tube** or to a **U-tube manometer** filled with mercury (or a lighter manometric fluid for low-pressure sections).
3. A **flow control valve** at the discharge to throttle the discharge and produce a steady, measurable flow rate.
4. A **measuring tank** (collecting tank) and a **stopwatch** to time the collection of a known volume of water, enabling the volumetric flow rate $Q$ to be determined.

The mechanical energy at each tapping is computed as the sum of three measurable heads:

- **Static pressure head**: read directly from the piezometric / manometric column at that section.
- **Velocity head**: obtained from the continuity equation, $V_i = Q / A_i$, where $Q$ is the (constant) volume flow rate and $A_i$ is the known area of the test section at the $i^{\text{th}}$ tapping.
- **Elevation head**: the vertical datum height of the tapping centreline, measured from a chosen reference.

> [!NOTE]
> In the typical KTU rig, all tappings lie on the **same horizontal centreline**, so the elevation head $z$ is identical at every point and therefore cancels when computing the *change* in total head between sections. This makes the verification cleaner: the experiment essentially confirms that $\dfrac{P}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V^{2}}{2g}$ remains constant.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why measure static pressure at multiple tappings?** Because the static pressure varies along a converging duct (it drops as the section narrows), and the piezometer / manometer converts this internal pressure into a visible column height that can be read against a scale.
- **Why compute velocity from $V = Q/A$?** Direct measurement of local velocity inside a closed duct is impractical for undergraduate labs; the volumetric flow rate is far easier to time-collect, and continuity guarantees $Q = A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2 = \cdots$.
- **Why sum the three heads?** Because Bernoulli's theorem is an *energy* statement, and each head is a direct measure of one specific form of mechanical energy per unit weight of the fluid.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity | Symbol | Formula / Definition | SI Unit |
|---|----------|--------|----------------------|---------|
| 1 | Total head at a section | $H$ | $H = \dfrac{P}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V^{2}}{2g} + z$ | m |
| 2 | Static pressure head | $\dfrac{P}{\rho g}$ | Read from piezometer / manometer | m of water |
| 3 | Velocity head | $\dfrac{V^{2}}{2g}$ | $V = \dfrac{Q}{A}$, then $\dfrac{V^{2}}{2g}$ | m |
| 4 | Elevation head | $z$ | Vertical height of section above datum | m |
| 5 | Volume flow rate | $Q$ | $Q = \dfrac{\text{Volume collected}}{\text{Time}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{col}}}{t}$ | m³/s |
| 6 | Cross-sectional area | $A$ | $A = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^{2}$ | m² |
| 7 | Mean velocity | $V$ | $V = \dfrac{Q}{A}$ | m/s |
| 8 | Reynolds number (check regime) | $Re$ | $Re = \dfrac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \dfrac{V D}{\nu}$ | — (dimensionless) |
| 9 | Manometer equation (mercury–water) | $P$ | $P = \rho_w g h_w - \rho_{Hg} g h_{Hg}$ | Pa |
| 10 | Head loss between two sections | $h_L$ | $h_L = H_1 - H_2$ | m |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Unit Consistency Rule:** Always express every head in **metres of water**. When a mercury manometer is used, convert the mercury column to an equivalent water column using $h_w^{\text{eq}} = \left(\dfrac{\rho_{Hg}}{\rho_w} - 1\right) h_{Hg} = 12.6\, h_{Hg}$ before adding to the velocity head.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Verification of Bernoulli's theorem underpins the design of virtually every flow-measuring device used in industry:

- **Venturi meter** (flow rate in pipelines) — relies on the pressure drop across a known area change.
- **Pitot-static tube** (aircraft airspeed indicators) — measures stagnation versus static pressure.
- **Orifice plate, flow nozzle, rotameter** — all are calibrated using the same energy-balance principle.
- **Hydraulic machines** (centrifugal pumps, turbines) — pump head and turbine head are derived from Bernoulli's equation with work / loss terms.

Hence, this humble lab verification is the gateway to modern flow-metering practice.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Rigorous Derivation of Bernoulli's Equation (from Euler's Equation)

Consider a steady, ideal, incompressible flow along a streamline. Take an elemental fluid parcel of cross-section $dA$ and length $ds$ along the streamline. The forces acting on it in the flow direction are:

1. Pressure force on the upstream face: $P\, dA$
2. Pressure force on the downstream face: $-\left(P + \dfrac{\partial P}{\partial s}\, ds\right) dA$
3. Component of body force (gravity) along $ds$: $-\rho g\, dz$

Newton's second law (per unit volume, in the streamline direction) gives Euler's equation of motion:

$$
\frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial P}{\partial s} + g\frac{dz}{ds} + V\frac{dV}{ds} = 0
$$

The derivation that follows rearranges this expression into a perfect differential and integrates along the streamline.

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Step 1: Multiply Euler's equation by } ds. \\
&\frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial P}{\partial s}\, ds \;+\; g\, dz \;+\; V\, dV \;=\; 0 \\[6pt]
&\text{Step 2: Recognise that for an incompressible fluid, } \rho = \text{const} \text{ and that } \frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial P}{\partial s}\, ds = \frac{dP}{\rho}. \\
&\frac{dP}{\rho} \;+\; g\, dz \;+\; V\, dV \;=\; 0 \\[6pt]
&\text{Step 3: Integrate the equation from section 1 to section 2 along the streamline.} \\
&\int_{1}^{2} \frac{dP}{\rho} \;+\; \int_{1}^{2} g\, dz \;+\; \int_{1}^{2} V\, dV \;=\; 0 \\[6pt]
&\text{Step 4: Since } \rho \text{ and } g \text{ are constants, pull them outside the integrals.} \\
&\frac{P_2 - P_1}{\rho} \;+\; g(z_2 - z_1) \;+\; \frac{V_2^{2} - V_1^{2}}{2} \;=\; 0 \\[6pt]
&\text{Step 5: Re-arrange so that all "1" terms sit on the left and "2" terms on the right.} \\
&\frac{P_1}{\rho} + g z_1 + \frac{V_1^{2}}{2} \;=\; \frac{P_2}{\rho} + g z_2 + \frac{V_2^{2}}{2} \\[6pt]
&\text{Step 6: Divide throughout by the specific weight } \gamma = \rho g \text{ to express in head form.} \\
&\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + z_1 + \frac{V_1^{2}}{2g} \;=\; \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + z_2 + \frac{V_2^{2}}{2g}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **classical Bernoulli equation** (energy per unit weight, in metres of fluid).

For a real fluid with friction, a head-loss term $h_L$ appears:

$$
\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + z_1 + \frac{V_1^{2}}{2g} \;=\; \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + z_2 + \frac{V_2^{2}}{2g} + h_L
$$

## 3.2 Derivation of the Manometer Equation (used in the experiment)

Consider a U-tube mercury manometer connected between two tappings. Let the differential manometer reading be $h_{Hg}$ (metres of mercury). Applying the principle of hydrostatics from one tapping, down through the manometric fluid, and up to the other tapping yields:

$$
P_1 - P_2 \;=\; (\rho_{Hg} - \rho_w)\, g\, h_{Hg}
$$

Converting to an equivalent head of water, $h_w = \dfrac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho_w g}$:

$$
h_w \;=\; \left(\frac{\rho_{Hg}}{\rho_w} - 1\right) h_{Hg} \;=\; (13.6 - 1)\, h_{Hg} \;=\; 12.6\, h_{Hg}
$$

This is the conversion used to feed the manometer reading into the "pressure head" column of the experimental table.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Demonstration (Typical KTU Lab)

**Given data (illustrative, fully solved):**

- Constant volume flow rate, $Q = 1.5 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}^{3}\text{/s}$ (150 mL/s, measured by collecting 3 L in 20 s).
- Test section centreline elevation above datum: $z = 0.150\ \text{m}$ (same for all tappings → cancels in total-head differences).
- Diameters of the four tappings: $D_1 = 25\ \text{mm},\ D_2 = 20\ \text{mm},\ D_3 = 16\ \text{mm},\ D_4 = 12.5\ \text{mm}$.
- Piezometer readings (in metres of water) at the four tappings: $h_1 = 0.420\ \text{m},\ h_2 = 0.395\ \text{m},\ h_3 = 0.350\ \text{m},\ h_4 = 0.300\ \text{m}$.

**Step 1 — Compute the area at each tapping.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_1 &= \frac{\pi}{4} (0.025)^{2} = 4.909 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}^{2} \\
A_2 &= \frac{\pi}{4} (0.020)^{2} = 3.142 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}^{2} \\
A_3 &= \frac{\pi}{4} (0.016)^{2} = 2.011 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}^{2} \\
A_4 &= \frac{\pi}{4} (0.0125)^{2} = 1.227 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the mean velocity at each tapping using continuity, $V_i = Q / A_i$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_1 &= \frac{1.5 \times 10^{-4}}{4.909 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.3056\ \text{m/s} \\
V_2 &= \frac{1.5 \times 10^{-4}}{3.142 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.4775\ \text{m/s} \\
V_3 &= \frac{1.5 \times 10^{-4}}{2.011 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.7459\ \text{s} \\
V_4 &= \frac{1.5 \times 10^{-4}}{1.227 \times 10^{-4}} = 1.2225\ \text{m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the velocity head, $V_i^{2}/(2g)$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{V_1^{2}}{2g} &= \frac{(0.3056)^{2}}{2(9.81)} = 0.00476\ \text{m} \\
\frac{V_2^{2}}{2g} &= \frac{(0.4775)^{2}}{2(9.81)} = 0.01162\ \text{m} \\
\frac{V_3^{2}}{2g} &= \frac{(0.7459)^{2}}{2(9.81)} = 0.02836\ \text{m} \\
\frac{V_4^{2}}{2g} &= \frac{(1.2225)^{2}}{2(9.81)} = 0.07618\ \text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the total head at each section, $H_i = z + \dfrac{P_i}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V_i^{2}}{2g}$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_1 &= 0.150 + 0.420 + 0.00476 = 0.5748\ \text{m} \\
H_2 &= 0.150 + 0.395 + 0.01162 = 0.5566\ \text{m} \\
H_3 &= 0.150 + 0.350 + 0.02836 = 0.5284\ \text{m} \\
H_4 &= 0.150 + 0.300 + 0.07618 = 0.5262\ \text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Tabulate and interpret.**

| Tapping | $D$ (m) | $A$ (m²) | $V$ (m/s) | $P/\rho g$ (m) | $V^{2}/2g$ (m) | $z$ (m) | $H_{\text{total}}$ (m) |
|---------|---------|----------|-----------|----------------|----------------|---------|-------------------------|
| 1 | 0.0250 | $4.909 \times 10^{-4}$ | 0.306 | 0.420 | 0.0048 | 0.150 | **0.575** |
| 2 | 0.0200 | $3.142 \times 10^{-4}$ | 0.478 | 0.395 | 0.0116 | 0.150 | **0.557** |
| 3 | 0.0160 | $2.011 \times 10^{-4}$ | 0.746 | 0.350 | 0.0284 | 0.150 | **0.528** |
| 4 | 0.0125 | $1.227 \times 10^{-4}$ | 1.223 | 0.300 | 0.0762 | 0.150 | **0.526** |

**Step 6 — Verification.**

If the fluid were ideal, the total head at all four sections would be identical, producing a perfectly horizontal line in the $H$ vs tapping-position graph. In practice (real water with viscosity and minor losses at each tapping), $H$ decreases *slightly* along the flow direction, as seen above ($\Delta H_{1 \to 4} = 0.575 - 0.526 = 0.049\ \text{m}$). This small drop is the cumulative **head loss** $h_L$, and the near-horizontal trend is the *experimental confirmation* of Bernoulli's theorem.

> [!TIP]
> **How to present the graph in your lab record (KTU 2024 expectation):**
> - X-axis: Tapping number (1, 2, 3, 4) or axial distance from inlet (mm).
> - Y-axis: Total head $H$ (m of water).
> - Plot three curves: static pressure head, velocity head, and total head.
> - The **total-head curve should be (approximately) a horizontal straight line**, validating Bernoulli.
> - Include the equation of the best-fit line ($H = mx + c$) and the slope should be $\approx 0$.

## 3.4 Analytical Reasoning Matrix — Sources of Error and How They Manifest

| Source of deviation | Physical origin | Effect on $H$ vs $x$ plot |
|---------------------|-----------------|-----------------------------|
| Viscous friction along pipe wall | Real fluid → real wall shear | $H$ decreases slightly with $x$ |
| Minor losses at tappings | Flow separation at each probe | $H$ shows a small local drop |
| Manometer reading parallax | Misaligned eye | Random scatter in $H$ |
| Pulsating / unsteady inflow | Pump surge | Wide scatter, non-repeating $H$ |
| Temperature change of water | $\rho_w$ varies with $T$ | Systematic bias in $H$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU lab record is judged on **three mandatory items**: (1) the fully populated observation table, (2) the $H$ vs $x$ graph, and (3) a one-line **inference** stating the principle verified, e.g., *"The total head is observed to be approximately constant along the test section, thus verifying Bernoulli's theorem."*
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

```mermaid
    B2 --> B3[Switch on pump and purge air from manometers]

    B10 --> B11{Inference}

```

> [!NOTE]
