<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*State Bernoulli's theorem for a real fluid in motion. List any two assumptions made in its derivation.*

**Model Answer:**
Bernoulli's theorem states that, for a steady, incompressible flow of a real (viscous) fluid along a streamline between any two sections, the total head is constant except for an irreversible head loss:

$$
\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^{2}}{2g} + z_1 \;=\; \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^{2}}{2g} + z_2 + h_L
$$

Assumptions (any two):
1. The fluid is steady and incompressible with constant $\rho$ and $\mu$.
2. The flow is along a single streamline and no energy is added or extracted by a pump or turbine between the two sections.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*In the verification of Bernoulli's theorem using a converging test section, why does the static pressure head decrease as the section contracts?*

**Model Answer:**
From the continuity equation, $Q = A V = \text{constant}$. As the area $A$ contracts, the velocity $V$ must increase. The kinetic energy per unit weight $\dfrac{V^{2}}{2g}$ therefore rises. To conserve total head (Bernoulli), the pressure head $\dfrac{P}{\rho g}$ must fall by an equivalent amount, hence the static pressure head is observed to decrease at the narrower sections.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]*

**(a)** *With the help of a neat schematic, describe the apparatus and procedure used in the laboratory to verify Bernoulli's theorem.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Apparatus required:**
- Bernoulli's apparatus consisting of a converging duct with 4 (or 5) static pressure tappings.
- Piezometer tubes / U-tube manometer bank fitted to each tapping.
- Centrifugal pump with sump tank.
- Collecting tank of known cross-sectional area and a stopwatch (resolution $\geq 0.1\ \text{s}$).
- Flow control valve at the discharge.

**Procedure:**
1. Fill the sump tank and ensure all piezometer tubes are free of air bubbles.
2. Open the flow control valve to a minimum setting and start the pump.
3. Allow the flow to become steady (wait 2–3 minutes for the manometer levels to stabilise).
4. Set a small discharge and record the manometer / piezometer readings at all tappings.
5. Measure the volume flow rate by collecting water in the measuring tank for a known time $t$; compute $Q = V_{\text{col}}/t$.
6. Repeat steps 4–5 for 4 to 5 different flow rates by progressively opening the valve.

**(b)** *The following data were obtained on a Bernoulli's apparatus with four tappings of diameters 28 mm, 22 mm, 16 mm and 12 mm respectively, all on the same horizontal centreline. The constant volume flow rate was measured as $2.5 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}^{3}\text{/s}$, and the corresponding piezometer heads (in metres of water) were 0.55, 0.49, 0.41 and 0.30. Verify Bernoulli's theorem by computing the total head at each section and commenting on the result.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Areas.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_1 &= \frac{\pi}{4}(0.028)^{2} = 6.158 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}^{2} \\
A_2 &= \frac{\pi}{4}(0.022)^{2} = 3.801 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}^{2} \\
A_3 &= \frac{\pi}{4}(0.016)^{2} = 2.011 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}^{2} \\
A_4 &= \frac{\pi}{4}(0.012)^{2} = 1.131 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Velocities from $V_i = Q/A_i$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_1 &= \frac{2.5 \times 10^{-4}}{6.158 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.406\ \text{m/s} \\
V_2 &= \frac{2.5 \times 10^{-4}}{3.801 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.658\ \text{m/s} \\
V_3 &= \frac{2.5 \times 10^{-4}}{2.011 \times 10^{-4}} = 1.243\ \text{m/s} \\
V_4 &= \frac{2.5 \times 10^{-4}}{1.131 \times 10^{-4}} = 2.211\ \text{m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Velocity heads, $V_i^{2}/(2g)$ with $g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^{2}$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{V_1^{2}}{2g} &= 0.0084\ \text{m}, \quad
\frac{V_2^{2}}{2g} = 0.0221\ \text{m}, \\
\frac{V_3^{2}}{2g} &= 0.0787\ \text{m}, \quad
\frac{V_4^{2}}{2g} = 0.2491\ \text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Total head, $H_i = z + \dfrac{P_i}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V_i^{2}}{2g}$.**

Since all tappings are on the same horizontal centreline, $z$ is the same for every section and therefore cancels in any difference. Setting $z = 0$ as the reference:

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_1 &= 0.55 + 0.0084 = 0.5584\ \text{m} \\
H_2 &= 0.49 + 0.0221 = 0.5121\ \text{m} \\
H_3 &= 0.41 + 0.0787 = 0.4887\ \text{m} \\
H_4 &= 0.30 + 0.2491 = 0.5491\ \text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Tabulation and inference.**

| Tapping | $D$ (m) | $V$ (m/s) | $P/\rho g$ (m) | $V^{2}/2g$ (m) | $H_{\text{total}}$ (m) |
|---------|---------|-----------|----------------|----------------|-------------------------|
| 1 | 0.028 | 0.406 | 0.550 | 0.008 | **0.558** |
| 2 | 0.022 | 0.658 | 0.490 | 0.022 | **0.512** |
| 3 | 0.016 | 1.243 | 0.410 | 0.079 | **0.489** |
| 4 | 0.012 | 2.211 | 0.300 | 0.249 | **0.549** |

**Inference:** The total head varies only between **0.489 m and 0.558 m**, a deviation of about $\pm 6\%$ around a mean of $\approx 0.527\ \text{m}$. This near-constancy confirms Bernoulli's theorem; the small drop in head is attributable to viscous friction and minor losses at the tappings. **Bernoulli's theorem is verified.**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam - May 2023]*

**(a)** *Derive Bernoulli's equation for steady, incompressible, inviscid flow along a streamline, starting from Euler's equation of motion.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**
The full derivation is presented in Section 3.1 of these notes. The expected answer is the *complete* sequence of steps:

1. Write Euler's equation along a streamline:
   $\dfrac{1}{\rho}\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial s} + g \dfrac{dz}{ds} + V \dfrac{dV}{ds} = 0$.
2. Multiply by $ds$ and recognise that for incompressible flow, $\rho = \text{constant}$:
   $\dfrac{dP}{\rho} + g\, dz + V\, dV = 0$.
3. Integrate from section 1 to section 2:
   $\displaystyle \int_{1}^{2} \dfrac{dP}{\rho} + \int_{1}^{2} g\, dz + \int_{1}^{2} V\, dV = 0$.
4. Evaluate each integral:
   $\dfrac{P_2 - P_1}{\rho} + g(z_2 - z_1) + \dfrac{V_2^{2} - V_1^{2}}{2} = 0$.
5. Re-arrange:
   $\dfrac{P_1}{\rho} + g z_1 + \dfrac{V_1^{2}}{2} = \dfrac{P_2}{\rho} + g z_2 + \dfrac{V_2^{2}}{2}$.
6. Divide by $g$ to obtain the head form:
   $\dfrac{P_1}{\rho g} + z_1 + \dfrac{V_1^{2}}{2g} = \dfrac{P_2}{\rho g} + z_2 + \dfrac{V_2^{2}}{2g}$.

**(b)** *In a Bernoulli's apparatus, the diameters at four tappings are 30, 24, 18 and 12 mm and the corresponding manometer readings (in mm of water) are 600, 540, 440 and 250 respectively. The constant flow rate is $3 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}^{3}\text{/s}$. Verify Bernoulli's theorem. All tappings lie on a horizontal centreline. Take $g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^{2}$.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Areas.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_1 &= \frac{\pi}{4}(0.030)^{2} = 7.069 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}^{2} \\
A_2 &= \frac{\pi}{4}(0.024)^{2} = 4.524 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}^{2} \\
A_3 &= \frac{\pi}{4}(0.018)^{2} = 2.545 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}^{2} \\
A_4 &= \frac{\pi}{4}(0.012)^{2} = 1.131 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Velocities, $V_i = Q / A_i$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_1 &= \frac{3 \times 10^{-4}}{7.069 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.424\ \text{m/s} \\
V_2 &= \frac{3 \times 10^{-4}}{4.524 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.663\ \text{m/s} \\
V_3 &= \frac{3 \times 10^{-4}}{2.545 \times 10^{-4}} = 1.179\ \text{m/s} \\
V_4 &= \frac{3 \times 10^{-4}}{1.131 \times 10^{-4}} = 2.653\ \text{m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Velocity heads.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{V_1^{2}}{2g} &= 0.00917\ \text{m},\quad
\frac{V_2^{2}}{2g} = 0.02240\ \text{m} \\
\frac{V_3^{2}}{2g} = 0.07084\ \text{m},\quad
\frac{V_4^{2}}{2g} = 0.35868\ \text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Total head, $H_i = \dfrac{P_i}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V_i^{2}}{2g}$ (elevation head $z$ constant → ignored).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_1 &= 0.600 + 0.00917 = 0.609\ \text{m} \\
H_2 &= 0.540 + 0.02240 = 0.562\ \text{m} \\
H_3 &= 0.440 + 0.07084 = 0.511\ \text{m} \\
H_4 &= 0.250 + 0.35868 = 0.609\ \text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Tabulation and inference.**

| Tapping | $D$ (mm) | $P/\rho g$ (m) | $V^{2}/2g$ (m) | $H_{\text{total}}$ (m) |
|---------|----------|----------------|----------------|-------------------------|
| 1 | 30 | 0.600 | 0.009 | **0.609** |
| 2 | 24 | 0.540 | 0.022 | **0.562** |
| 3 | 18 | 0.440 | 0.071 | **0.511** |
| 4 | 12 | 0.250 | 0.359 | **0.609** |

**Inference:** The total head remains within the narrow band **0.511 m to 0.609 m** (mean $\approx 0.573\ \text{m}$). Considering experimental tolerances (manometer parallax, viscous losses, unsteady inflow), this confirms that the total head is essentially constant, and **Bernoulli's theorem is verified.**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Deduction Traps:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert** the manometer reading to metres (mm → m) before addition. *Always standardise units first.*
> 2. **Using $g = 9.8$ vs $g = 9.81$ inconsistently** in $V^{2}/(2g)$ and the area terms. Pick one value at the top of the answer and use it everywhere.
> 3. **Mixing up diameter and radius**: the formula is $A = \dfrac{\pi}{4}D^{2}$, not $\pi D^{2}$. This single slip costs **0.5–1 mark**.
> 4. **Not including the elevation head**: even when all tappings are on a horizontal line, the *definition* of total head must include $z$; an explicit "z is constant" statement is required.
> 5. **Omitting the unit** of every column in the observation table. KTU evaluators routinely deduct half a mark for missing SI units.
> 6. **Forgetting to plot the graph**: the lab viva often demands the $H$ vs $x$ plot — it is worth a full **2 marks** in the record.
> 7. **Ignoring the head-loss interpretation**: a perfectly horizontal line is unrealistic. A *short note* on the small head loss $h_L$ due to viscosity earns the "inference" mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Bernoulli's equation** in head form:
  $\dfrac{P_1}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V_1^{2}}{2g} + z_1 = \dfrac{P_2}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V_2^{2}}{2g} + z_2 + h_L$.
- **Assumptions**: steady, incompressible, inviscid, along a streamline, no external work.
- **Apparatus in this experiment**: converging test section + piezometer / manometer tappings + sump-pump-return loop + measuring tank + stopwatch.
- **Procedure core**: set a steady flow → read manometer columns → measure $Q$ by collection-and-timing → repeat for 4–5 flow rates.
- **Computation sequence** (memorise this order!):
  1. $A_i = \dfrac{\pi}{4}D_i^{2}$,
  2. $V_i = Q/A_i$ (continuity),
  3. velocity head $= V_i^{2}/(2g)$,
  4. pressure head from manometer (convert mm of Hg → m of water if needed),
  5. total head $H_i = z_i + \text{pressure head} + \text{velocity head}$,
  6. plot $H_i$ vs tapping number / axial distance — expect a near-horizontal line.
- **Mercury manometer conversion**: $h_{w,\text{eq}} = 12.6 \cdot h_{Hg}$.
- **Sign of the head loss $h_L$**: $h_L = H_1 - H_2 \geq 0$ (always positive in the direction of flow).
- **Limitation of the verification**: real water has viscosity → small drop in $H$ is expected and is *not* a contradiction of Bernoulli's theorem; it is the head-loss term.
- **Real-world relevance**: same principle is exploited in Venturi meters, Pitot tubes, orifice plates, and aircraft airspeed indicators.
- **Mandatory sections of the lab record** (per KTU 2024 PCCEL308): aim, apparatus, theory, procedure, observation table, sample calculation, graph ($H$ vs $x$), result, and inference.
- **Quick mental check**: as $A$ contracts, $V$ rises and pressure falls — that single statement captures the *physical* content of Bernoulli's theorem.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hydraulic Coefficients of Orifices and Mouth Pieces under Constant Head Method and Time of Emptying Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCEL308 - FLUID MECHANICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Hydraulic Coefficients of Orifices and Mouth Pieces under Constant Head Method and Time of Emptying Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is an Orifice?

An **orifice** is a small opening (generally circular) made in the wall or base of a tank containing fluid, through which the fluid discharges. The depth of the opening is negligible compared to its other dimensions. When fluid flows through an orifice, the **jet of fluid contracts** immediately after passing through the opening, reaching a minimum cross-sectional area called the **Vena Contracta**, located at a distance of approximately **0.5 times the diameter of the orifice** from the upstream face.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PCCEL308 Module 2):** A circular orifice of diameter $d$ fitted in a tank wall, discharging water into the atmosphere, is the standard test specimen. The diameter $d$ is typically between **10 mm to 25 mm**.

### 1.2 What is a Mouthpiece?

A **mouthpiece** is a short tube (length $L$ usually equal to **2 to 3 times the diameter** $d$) fitted to an opening in a tank wall. It is essentially an orifice fitted with a converging or cylindrical extension. The key difference is that a mouthpiece **eliminates the contraction of the jet** (or significantly reduces it), leading to a higher coefficient of discharge compared to a simple orifice.

| Feature | Orifice | Mouthpiece |
|---|---|---|
| Length | Negligible (thin plate) | 2d to 3d |
| Contraction | Yes (Vena Contracta forms) | Negligible for external cylindrical |
| Typical $C_d$ | 0.60 – 0.65 | 0.80 – 0.85 |

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **The Garden Tap Analogy:**
> Imagine a **thin hole punched in a plastic water bottle** (this is an **orifice**). The water stream coming out will visibly narrow just after the hole — this narrowing is the *vena contracta*. Now imagine **attaching a small cylindrical straw** to the hole and pushing it through (this is a **mouthpiece**). Notice how the stream no longer narrows, and **far more water flows out in the same time**! This is exactly what we measure in the lab: how the geometry affects the three hydraulic coefficients.

### 1.4 The Three Hydraulic Coefficients

**1. Coefficient of Discharge ($C_d$):**
$$C_d = \frac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{theoretical}}$$

It accounts for **all losses** (viscous, turbulence, contraction) in one lumped parameter. Dimensionless.

**2. Coefficient of Velocity ($C_v$):**
$$C_v = \frac{V_{actual}}{V_{theoretical}}$$

It measures how close the actual jet velocity is to the ideal Torricelli velocity $\sqrt{2gH}$. Typical value: **0.95 to 0.99**.

**3. Coefficient of Contraction ($C_c$):**
$$C_c = \frac{A_{vena\ contracta}}{A_{orifice}}$$

It quantifies the **shrinking** of the jet cross-section. Typical value: **0.61 to 0.65** for a sharp-edged orifice.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Golden Relationship (MUST memorize):**
> $$C_d = C_c \times C_v$$
> This single equation links all three coefficients and is asked in nearly every KTU exam.

### 1.5 Experimental Methods at a Glance

- **Constant Head Method:** A constant head tank maintains a fixed water level above the orifice using an overflow arrangement. We collect a known volume $V$ in time $t$.
- **Time of Emptying Method:** The supply is cut off, and the tank empties under gravity from a higher level $H_1$ to a lower level $H_2$ through the orifice. We measure the emptying time $t$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Torricelli's Law

Applying Bernoulli's equation between the free surface (point 1) and the vena contracta (point 2) of the jet:

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2 + h_L$$

With assumptions: both points at atmospheric pressure, $V_1 \approx 0$ (large tank), head loss $h_L$ ignored for the ideal case:

$$V_{theoretical} = \sqrt{2gH}$$

This is the **maximum possible velocity** the fluid could attain. The actual velocity is always less because of viscous and turbulence losses.

### 2.2 Why Three Coefficients Exist — The Logic Chain

- The fluid does **not** flow through the *full* area $a$ of the orifice — the jet shrinks to area $a_c$ at the vena contracta → hence $C_c$.
- The fluid at the vena contracta moves **slower** than the ideal $\sqrt{2gH}$ due to friction → hence $C_v$.
- Both effects compound, so the actual discharge is $Q_{act} = a_c \times V_{actual} = (C_c \cdot a) \times (C_v \cdot \sqrt{2gH}) = C_c C_v \cdot a\sqrt{2gH} = C_d \cdot a\sqrt{2gH}$.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Theoretical Velocity | $V_{th} = \sqrt{2gH}$ | m/s | From Torricelli's Law |
| 2 | Theoretical Discharge | $Q_{th} = a \cdot \sqrt{2gH}$ | m³/s | $a$ = orifice area |
| 3 | Actual Discharge | $Q_{act} = C_d \cdot a \cdot \sqrt{2gH}$ | m³/s | $C_d < 1$ |
| 4 | Actual Velocity | $V_{act} = C_v \cdot \sqrt{2gH}$ | m/s | Measured via trajectory |
| 5 | Contracted Area | $a_c = C_c \cdot a$ | m² | Vena contracta area |
| 6 | Master Relation | $C_d = C_c \times C_v$ | — | Dimensionless |
| 7 | $C_v$ from trajectory | $C_v = \dfrac{x}{\sqrt{4yH}}$ | — | $x$ = horizontal range, $y$ = vertical fall |
| 8 | $C_d$ (Constant Head) | $C_d = \dfrac{V_{collected}}{a \cdot t \cdot \sqrt{2gH}}$ | — | Direct method |
| 9 | $C_d$ (Time of Emptying) | $C_d = \dfrac{2 A_h (\sqrt{H_1} - \sqrt{H_2})}{a \cdot t \cdot \sqrt{2g}}$ | — | $A_h$ = tank cross-section |
| 10 | $C_d$ for Mouthpiece | $C_d = \dfrac{2 A_h (\sqrt{H_1} - \sqrt{H_2})}{a \cdot t \cdot \sqrt{2g}}$ | — | Same as orifice (no contraction) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Vertical Bar Rule:** In the KTU formula sheet, expressions like absolute value are written using `\vert` (e.g., $C_d = \vert \text{ratio} \vert$) to maintain LaTeX compatibility inside markdown tables.

### 2.4 Typical KTU Lab Values to Remember

| Specimen | $C_c$ | $C_v$ | $C_d$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sharp-edged orifice | 0.62 | 0.97 | 0.60 – 0.65 |
| External cylindrical mouthpiece | 1.00 | 0.855 | 0.855 |
| Internal mouthpiece (re-entrant) | ~0.55 | 0.96 | ~0.50 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Engineering Utility:** Hydraulic coefficients are foundational for designing **spillways of dams, fire-fighting nozzles, water supply pipelines, flow meters, and turbines**. The discharge coefficient of nozzles directly impacts the thrust of rockets and the efficiency of Pelton wheel turbines.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of $C_d$ — Constant Head Method

**Setup:** A constant-head tank maintains a steady head $H$ above the orifice of area $a$. Water is collected in a measuring tank of cross-section $A_m$ for a measured time $t$.

**Step 1 — Actual discharge observed:**
Water rises by a height $dh_m$ in the measuring tank during time $t$.
$$Q_{act} = \frac{V_{collected}}{t} = \frac{A_m \cdot dh_m}{t}$$

**Step 2 — Theoretical discharge (Torricelli):**
$$Q_{th} = a \cdot \sqrt{2gH}$$

**Step 3 — Apply definition of $C_d$:**
$$C_d = \frac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} = \frac{A_m \cdot dh_m}{a \cdot t \cdot \sqrt{2gH}}$$

**Step 4 — Final Working Formula for the Lab Manual:**

$$\boxed{C_d = \frac{A_m \cdot dh_m}{a \cdot t \cdot \sqrt{2gH}}}$$

### 3.2 Derivation of $C_d$ — Time of Emptying Method

**Setup:** Supply is closed. The tank of cross-section $A_h$ empties through the orifice from initial head $H_1$ to final head $H_2$ in time $t$.

**Step 1 — Differential volume in time $dt$:**
The head drops by $dh$ (negative) in time $dt$. The volume leaving is:
$$dV = -A_h \cdot dh$$

**Step 2 — Rate of discharge at instantaneous head $h$:**
$$Q = C_d \cdot a \cdot \sqrt{2gh}$$

**Step 3 — Relate $dV$ and $Q$:**
By continuity, $dV = Q \cdot dt$, so:
$$-A_h \cdot dh = C_d \cdot a \cdot \sqrt{2gh} \cdot dt$$

**Step 4 — Separate variables:**
$$dt = -\frac{A_h}{C_d \cdot a \cdot \sqrt{2g}} \cdot \frac{dh}{\sqrt{h}}$$

**Step 5 — Integrate from $H_1$ to $H_2$ over time 0 to $t$:**
$$t = -\frac{A_h}{C_d \cdot a \cdot \sqrt{2g}} \int_{H_1}^{H_2} h^{-1/2} \, dh$$

**Step 6 — Perform the integration:**
$$\int h^{-1/2} \, dh = 2\sqrt{h}$$

Therefore:
$$t = -\frac{A_h}{C_d \cdot a \cdot \sqrt{2g}} \left[ 2\sqrt{h} \right]_{H_1}^{H_2} = -\frac{2A_h}{C_d \cdot a \cdot \sqrt{2g}} \left( \sqrt{H_2} - \sqrt{H_1} \right)$$

**Step 7 — Simplify (negative sign flips the limits):**
$$t = \frac{2A_h}{C_d \cdot a \cdot \sqrt{2g}} \left( \sqrt{H_1} - \sqrt{H_2} \right)$$

**Step 8 — Solve for $C_d$:**

$$\boxed{C_d = \frac{2 A_h \left(\sqrt{H_1} - \sqrt{H_2}\right)}{a \cdot t \cdot \sqrt{2g}}}$$

### 3.3 Derivation of $C_v$ from Trajectory Method

The jet leaves the orifice horizontally and follows projectile motion. Let $H$ = head, $x$ = horizontal distance traveled by jet to fall $y$ (measured with a trajectory apparatus).

**Step 1 — Actual velocity from kinematics (horizontal component):**
$$V_{act} = x \sqrt{\frac{g}{2y}}$$

**Step 2 — Apply definition of $C_v$:**
$$C_v = \frac{V_{act}}{V_{th}} = \frac{x \sqrt{g/(2y)}}{\sqrt{2gH}}$$

**Step 3 — Simplify:**
$$\boxed{C_v = \frac{x}{\sqrt{4yH}}}$$

**Step 4 — Once $C_d$ and $C_v$ are known, recover $C_c$:**
$$C_c = \frac{C_d}{C_v}$$

### 3.4 Sample Numerical — Constant Head Method (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** An orifice of diameter **d = 20 mm** is fitted to a vertical wall of a constant-head tank. The head above the orifice is maintained at **H = 500 mm**. Water is collected in a measuring cylinder of cross-section **$A_m = 0.05\ m^2$**. In **t = 60 s**, the water level in the measuring tank rises by **$dh_m = 0.12\ m$**. Find $C_d$.

**Solution:**

$$a = \frac{\pi}{4} d^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.020)^2 = 3.1416 \times 10^{-4}\ m^2$$

$$C_d = \frac{A_m \cdot dh_m}{a \cdot t \cdot \sqrt{2gH}}$$

$$C_d = \frac{0.05 \times 0.12}{3.1416 \times 10^{-4} \times 60 \times \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.500}}$$

$$\sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.500} = \sqrt{9.81} = 3.132\ m/s$$

$$C_d = \frac{0.006}{3.1416 \times 10^{-4} \times 60 \times 3.132} = \frac{0.006}{0.05904} = 0.1016$$

⚠️ **Note for the student:** A low $C_d$ value like this suggests an unusually small orifice or a measurement inconsistency; real sharp-edged orifices give **0.60 – 0.65**. Recheck your numbers if the result is wildly off — this is a common KTU lab mistake.

### 3.5 Sample Numerical — Time of Emptying Method (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** A cylindrical tank of cross-section **$A_h = 0.50\ m^2$** is fitted with an orifice of diameter **d = 25 mm**. The water level falls from **$H_1 = 1.00\ m$** to **$H_2 = 0.25\ m$** in **t = 240 s**. Determine $C_d$.

**Solution:**

$$a = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.025)^2 = 4.9087 \times 10^{-4}\ m^2$$

$$C_d = \frac{2 A_h (\sqrt{H_1} - \sqrt{H_2})}{a \cdot t \cdot \sqrt{2g}}$$

$$\sqrt{H_1} - \sqrt{H_2} = 1.000 - 0.500 = 0.500$$

$$\sqrt{2g} = \sqrt{19.62} = 4.429\ \sqrt{m/s}$$

$$C_d = \frac{2 \times 0.50 \times 0.500}{4.9087 \times 10^{-4} \times 240 \times 4.429}$$

$$C_d = \frac{0.500}{0.5217} = 0.9584$$

⚠️ **Examination Hint:** A value above 0.7 for a sharp-edged orifice is suspicious. The student should comment on whether the orifice is sharp-edged or has a rounded lip, and discuss the likely cause in the viva.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Experimental Setup

### 4.4 Procedure Summary — Stepwise Operation

| Step | Action | Observation Recorded |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Note orifice diameter $d$ with vernier caliper | $d$ in metres |
| 2 | Set up constant head using overflow | $H$ from pointer gauge |
| 3 | Open inlet valve, let steady flow establish | Confirm steady jet |
| 4 | Place measuring cylinder, start stopwatch | $t$ in seconds |
| 5 | Stop stopwatch, note rise $dh_m$ | $dh_m$ in metres |
| 6 | Compute $C_d$ from formula | Tabulate |
| 7 | Repeat for 3 different heads | Plot $C_d$ vs $H$ |
| 8 | Switch to time-of-emptying mode | Measure $H_1, H_2, t$ |
| 9 | Compute $C_d$ again | Cross-check |
| 10 | Replace orifice with mouthpiece | Repeat all steps |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define the three hydraulic coefficients of an orifice and state the relationship between them.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Coefficient of Discharge ($C_d$):** The ratio of actual discharge to theoretical discharge, $C_d = \dfrac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}}$.
- **Coefficient of Velocity ($C_v$):** The ratio of actual velocity at the vena contracta to the theoretical velocity, $C_v = \dfrac{V_{act}}{\sqrt{2gH}}$.
- **Coefficient of Contraction ($C_c$):** The ratio of the area of the vena contracta to the area of the orifice, $C_c = \dfrac{a_c}{a}$.
- **Relationship:** $C_d = C_c \times C_v$ — *(1 mark for this exact relation)*

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Why is the coefficient of discharge of a mouthpiece greater than that of a sharp-edged orifice?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

In a sharp-edged orifice, the jet contracts and forms a **vena contracta** ($C_c \approx 0.62$). In an external cylindrical mouthpiece, the **length of the tube is 2 to 3 times the diameter**, which prevents the jet from contracting — the fluid fully fills the mouthpiece and exits at full area ($C_c = 1$). Since $C_d = C_c \times C_v$ and the small extra friction loss in the mouthpiece keeps $C_v$ high (~0.855), the **absence of contraction** dominates, giving a higher $C_d$ for the mouthpiece.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks):**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Modified, Module 2 Internal Choice]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the **constant head method** of determining the coefficient of discharge $C_d$ of a small orifice. Derive the expression used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A sharp-edged orifice of **diameter 15 mm** is fitted to a vertical tank wall. Under a constant head of **600 mm**, a measuring cylinder of area **0.04 m²** collects **5.6 litres** of water in **45 seconds**. Calculate $C_d$ and the actual discharge in **litres/second**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **SOLUTION — QUESTION A**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

- **Apparatus description (2 marks):** Constant head tank with overflow arrangement, pointer gauge, sharp-edged orifice, measuring cylinder, stopwatch.
- **Procedure (2 marks):** Maintain constant head $H$ via overflow. Collect water in cylinder, measure rise $dh_m$ in time $t$. Repeat for different heads.
- **Derivation (3 marks):**

$$Q_{act} = \frac{A_m \cdot dh_m}{t}; \quad Q_{th} = a\sqrt{2gH}; \quad C_d = \frac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} = \frac{A_m \cdot dh_m}{a \cdot t \cdot \sqrt{2gH}}$$

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

Given: $d = 0.015\ m$, $H = 0.600\ m$, $A_m = 0.04\ m^2$, $V = 5.6\ L = 5.6 \times 10^{-3}\ m^3$, $t = 45\ s$.

$$a = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.015)^2 = 1.767 \times 10^{-4}\ m^2$$

$$\sqrt{2gH} = \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.600} = \sqrt{11.772} = 3.431\ m/s$$

$$C_d = \frac{5.6 \times 10^{-3}}{1.767 \times 10^{-4} \times 45 \times 3.431} = \frac{5.6 \times 10^{-3}}{2.729 \times 10^{-2}} = 0.2052$$

$$Q_{act} = \frac{V}{t} = \frac{5.6 \times 10^{-3}}{45} = 1.244 \times 10^{-4}\ m^3/s = 0.1244\ L/s$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Question A:** Students frequently **forget to convert litres to m³** and lose 1 mark. Also, do **not round $g$ to 10 m/s²** unless the question explicitly permits; use $g = 9.81$ m/s² for KTU lab calculations. Finally, **the orifice area formula $\frac{\pi}{4}d^2$ must be used in metres** — diameter must be in metres, not mm.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice):**
**[KTU University Exam – June 2023 Modified, Module 2 Internal Choice]**

**(a)** Explain the **time of emptying method** for finding the coefficient of discharge of an orifice. Derive the working formula step by step. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A cylindrical tank of **diameter 0.6 m** has a sharp-edged orifice of **20 mm diameter** at its base. The water level drops from **1.2 m to 0.3 m** in **180 seconds**. Determine $C_d$ and compare it with the standard value. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **SOLUTION — QUESTION B**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

- **Setup (2 marks):** Inlet is closed. Tank discharges through orifice under falling head. Time $t$ to fall from $H_1$ to $H_2$ is measured.
- **Differential equation (3 marks):** $Q = C_d a \sqrt{2gh} = -\dfrac{dV}{dt} = -A_h \dfrac{dh}{dt}$
- **Integration (2 marks):**

$$C_d \cdot a \cdot \sqrt{2g} \int_0^t dt = -A_h \int_{H_1}^{H_2} \frac{dh}{\sqrt{h}}$$

$$C_d = \frac{2A_h(\sqrt{H_1} - \sqrt{H_2})}{a \cdot t \cdot \sqrt{2g}}$$

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

Given: tank diameter $D = 0.6\ m \Rightarrow A_h = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.6)^2 = 0.2827\ m^2$. Orifice $d = 0.020\ m \Rightarrow a = 3.1416 \times 10^{-4}\ m^2$. $H_1 = 1.2\ m$, $H_2 = 0.3\ m$, $t = 180\ s$.

$$\sqrt{H_1} - \sqrt{H_2} = \sqrt{1.2} - \sqrt{0.3} = 1.0954 - 0.5477 = 0.5477\ m^{1/2}$$

$$\sqrt{2g} = 4.4294\ \sqrt{m/s^2}$$

$$C_d = \frac{2 \times 0.2827 \times 0.5477}{3.1416 \times 10^{-4} \times 180 \times 4.4294}$$

$$C_d = \frac{0.3097}{0.2505} = 1.236$$

⚠️ **Examiner's Note:** A value greater than 1 is physically impossible for $C_d$. This indicates an **experimental error** — likely:
- The orifice diameter was measured incorrectly (e.g., 20 mm read as 25 mm).
- The tank cross-section was estimated (not measured).
- Parallax error in reading the head gauge.

The expected value is around **0.61 to 0.65** for a sharp-edged orifice. The student should report the error, not the erroneous value.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Question B:** Many students blindly report values of $C_d > 1$ as the answer. This **immediately costs 2 marks** and signals a lack of conceptual understanding. Always **check for physical sanity** ($0 < C_d \leq 1$) and discuss the deviation. Also, the tank cross-section $A_h$ uses the **tank diameter, not orifice diameter** — a common substitution error worth 1 mark.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🔥 KTU Quick Recall — Print This Before Exam 🔥**

- ✅ **Definition Trio:** $C_d$ (discharge), $C_v$ (velocity), $C_c$ (contraction) — all dimensionless.
- ✅ **Golden Equation:** $C_d = C_c \times C_v$ — must derive it in the derivation question.
- ✅ **Torricelli:** $V_{th} = \sqrt{2gH}$ — this is the **theoretical** velocity, not actual.
- ✅ **Constant Head $C_d$ Formula:** $C_d = \dfrac{A_m \cdot dh_m}{a \cdot t \cdot \sqrt{2gH}}$
- ✅ **Time of Emptying $C_d$ Formula:** $C_d = \dfrac{2A_h(\sqrt{H_1} - \sqrt{H_2})}{a \cdot t \cdot \sqrt{2g}}$
- ✅ **Trajectory $C_v$ Formula:** $C_v = \dfrac{x}{\sqrt{4yH}}$
- ✅ **Standard Values:** Orifice $C_d \approx 0.62$, Mouthpiece $C_d \approx 0.855$.
- ✅ **Why mouthpiece $C_d$ is higher:** No vena contracta forms ($C_c = 1$).
- ✅ **Unit consistency:** All lengths in **metres**, $g = 9.81$ m/s² unless stated.
- ✅ **Validity check:** $C_d$ must lie in $[0, 1]$. If not, find the error.
- ✅ **Viva Question to Prepare:** *"What is the distance of the vena contracta from the orifice?"* — Answer: approximately **0.5 d**.
- ✅ **Apparatus Name:** Trajectory apparatus / Pilot tube arrangement — must know the diagram.
- ✅ **Shape factor:** A round-edged orifice has $C_d$ close to 0.95; a sharp-edged one has $C_d \approx 0.62$.
- ✅ **Two methods, same goal:** Both constant-head and time-of-emptying aim to find $C_d$, just under different boundary conditions (steady vs unsteady flow).

---

### 5.4 Common Mistakes to Avoid (KTU Examiner's Red Pen List)

1. ❌ Writing $\sqrt{2gH}$ with $g = 10$ when the question expects $9.81$.
2. ❌ Mixing up tank cross-section $A_h$ with measuring cylinder area $A_m$.
3. ❌ Reporting $C_d > 1$ without comment.
4. ❌ Forgetting to convert litres → m³ (1 litre = $10^{-3}$ m³).
5. ❌ Using the wrong formula for $C_v$ (writing $\sqrt{2gH}/x$ instead of $x/\sqrt{4yH}$).
6. ❌ Not showing the integration step in the time-of-emptying derivation.
7. ❌ Confusing mouthpiece with orifice coefficients.

> **End of Module Experiment 2 Notes — Hydraulic Coefficients of Orifices and Mouth Pieces**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Calibration of Venturi Meter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCEL308 - FLUID MECHANICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Calibration of Venturi Meter

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

A **Venturi Meter** is a differential pressure type flow measurement device used to determine the discharge (volumetric flow rate) of an incompressible fluid flowing through a closed conduit (pipe). It operates on the principle of **Bernoulli's Equation** combined with the **Continuity Equation**, and consists of three sections: a converging cone (inlet to throat), a cylindrical throat of uniform cross-section, and a diverging cone (throat to outlet). The pressure difference developed between the inlet and the throat is measured using a differential manometer, from which the discharge through the pipe is calculated.

**Calibration** of a Venturi meter refers to the experimental determination of its **Coefficient of Discharge ($C_d$)**, which is the ratio of the *actual discharge* to the *theoretical discharge*. The calibration is performed by passing a known quantity of fluid through the meter, measuring the corresponding manometer deflection, and computing $C_d$ for various flow rates.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Syllabus Highlight:** KTU 2024 Scheme explicitly tests the student's ability to (i) apply Bernoulli's equation across a constriction, (ii) derive the discharge formula, and (iii) experimentally evaluate the coefficient of discharge.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine water flowing through a **wide garden pipe** that suddenly narrows into a thin nozzle. What happens to the water?

1. The water **speeds up** in the narrow section (you can feel the velocity increase at the outlet).
2. As the velocity increases, the **pressure drops** at the narrow section.
3. If you poke a small hole at the wide section and another at the narrow section, the water rises to *different heights* in a U-tube manometer.

This pressure difference is **directly proportional** to the square of the velocity, and therefore to the square of the flow rate. The Venturi meter essentially converts this invisible pressure difference into a measurable deflection, and then into a flow rate.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The diverging cone (recovery cone) is critical:** It gradually decelerates the fluid back to its original velocity, recovering most of the pressure lost in the converging section. This is what makes a Venturi meter the **most efficient** (lowest head loss) flow measurement device compared to an orifice meter.

### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics

- **Density of water at 4°C:** $\rho_w = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- **Specific weight of water:** $\gamma_w = 9810 \text{ N/m}^3$
- **Acceleration due to gravity (KTU standard):** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
- **Manometer fluid for water flow:** Mercury (Hg) with $\rho_{Hg} = 13600 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- **Manometer fluid for air flow:** Water or oil
- **Typical inlet to throat area ratio:** $D_1/D_2 = 2$ to $3$ (in lab scale)

> [!TIP]
> In the KTU lab manual, the Venturi meter used is typically a **horizontal setup** (so no elevation change), and the manometer fluid is **mercury (Hg)**. Always quote $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ unless the problem specifies otherwise.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Concept — Step-by-Step Logic

The Venturi meter functions on two fundamental principles of fluid mechanics:

**Step 1 — Continuity Equation (Conservation of Mass):**
For an incompressible fluid flowing through a pipe of varying cross-section, the mass flow rate remains constant. Hence the velocity is higher where the area is smaller.

**Step 2 — Bernoulli's Equation (Conservation of Energy):**
For an ideal (inviscid, incompressible) fluid, the sum of pressure head, velocity head, and elevation head is constant along a streamline. This means as velocity increases, pressure must decrease.

**Step 3 — Pressure Difference Measurement:**
The pressure difference between the inlet (section 1) and the throat (section 2) is measured using a **U-tube differential manometer**.

**Step 4 — Theoretical Discharge Calculation:**
The measured pressure difference is converted into a theoretical velocity at the throat, and then into a theoretical discharge.

**Step 5 — Actual Discharge & Coefficient of Discharge:**
The actual discharge is measured by collecting the fluid in a measuring tank over a known time interval. The ratio gives $C_d$, which is plotted against Reynolds number (or flow rate) to obtain the **calibration curve**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Coefficient of Discharge ($C_d$)** for a properly designed Venturi meter is typically between **0.95 and 0.99**, which is much higher than an orifice meter ($C_d \approx 0.62$ to $0.65$). This is the principal reason Venturi meters are preferred in applications where **head loss must be minimized**.

### 2.2 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind Each Step

- **Why use a diverging cone?** To prevent flow separation and recover kinetic energy as pressure energy. This minimizes eddy losses and head loss.
- **Why use a manometer instead of a pressure gauge?** A manometer gives the *differential* pressure directly, eliminating the need for two separate gauge readings and improving accuracy.
- **Why calibrate?** Theoretical formulas assume ideal flow (no friction, no vena contracta, no turbulence). In reality, viscous effects and flow separation reduce the actual discharge below the theoretical value. $C_d$ accounts for these losses.
- **How is the calibration curve used in industry?** Once $C_d$ vs Re is plotted, the same Venturi meter can be installed in any pipeline. By measuring the manometer deflection, the operator simply reads off the actual discharge from the calibrated curve.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **#** | **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Description** |
|:-----:|:-------------|:------------|:----------------|
| 1 | Continuity Equation | $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$ | Mass conservation between inlet and throat |
| 2 | Bernoulli's Equation (horizontal) | $\dfrac{P_1}{\gamma} + \dfrac{V_1^2}{2g} = \dfrac{P_2}{\gamma} + \dfrac{V_2^2}{2g}$ | Energy conservation, $z_1 = z_2$ |
| 3 | Pressure Head Difference | $\dfrac{P_1 - P_2}{\gamma} = \dfrac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2g}$ | From Bernoulli's equation |
| 4 | Manometric Head (h) in metres of flowing fluid | $h = x \left[ \dfrac{\rho_m}{\rho} - 1 \right]$ | $x$ = manometer deflection in m |
| 5 | Theoretical Velocity at Throat | $V_2 = \dfrac{A_1}{\sqrt{A_1^2 - A_2^2}} \cdot \sqrt{2gh}$ | Substituting continuity into Bernoulli |
| 6 | Theoretical Discharge | $Q_{th} = A_2 V_2 = \dfrac{A_1 A_2}{\sqrt{A_1^2 - A_2^2}} \cdot \sqrt{2gh}$ | Core formula |
| 7 | Actual Discharge | $Q_{act} = \dfrac{\text{Volume collected}}{\text{Time taken}} = \dfrac{V}{t}$ | Measured experimentally |
| 8 | Coefficient of Discharge | $C_d = \dfrac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}}$ | Dimensionless, < 1 |
| 9 | Reynolds Number at Throat | $Re = \dfrac{\rho V_2 D_2}{\mu}$ | For plotting calibration curve |
| 10 | Calibration Equation | $Q_{act} = C_d \cdot \dfrac{A_1 A_2}{\sqrt{A_1^2 - A_2^2}} \cdot \sqrt{2gh}$ | Final working equation |

> [!NOTE]
> In the formula table above, $\gamma = \rho g$ is the specific weight of the flowing fluid, and $\rho_m$ is the density of the manometric fluid (e.g., mercury = $13600 \text{ kg/m}^3$).

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Venturi meters are extensively used in:

- **Water supply schemes and treatment plants** (measuring raw water intake)
- **Chemical and petrochemical industries** (flow of oils, acids, slurries)
- **Power plants** (feedwater and condensate flow measurement)
- **Aerospace** (aircraft fuel flow measurement, air intake)
- **HVAC systems** (chilled water and condenser water flow)
- **Medical devices** (respiratory flow measurement in ventilators)
- **Agricultural irrigation systems** (fertigation flow control)

> [!TIP]
> In KTU lab viva, you may be asked: *"Why is a Venturi meter preferred over an orifice meter for measuring large flows?"* The standard answer: **Higher $C_d$ (≈ 0.98 vs 0.62), lower head loss, no vena contracta, higher accuracy, and can handle slurries without clogging if designed properly.**
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Theoretical Discharge Equation

**Given:**
- Horizontal Venturi meter (so $z_1 = z_2$, no elevation change)
- Inlet diameter $D_1$, throat diameter $D_2$
- Inlet area $A_1 = \pi D_1^2 / 4$, throat area $A_2 = \pi D_2^2 / 4$
- Inlet pressure $P_1$, throat pressure $P_2$
- Inlet velocity $V_1$, throat velocity $V_2$
- Manometer deflection $x$ in metres, flowing fluid density $\rho$, manometer fluid density $\rho_m$

**Derivation:**

**Step 1:** Apply the **Continuity Equation** between the inlet (Section 1) and the throat (Section 2).

$$
A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2
$$

*Logic:* For an incompressible fluid in a closed pipe, the volumetric flow rate must be the same at every cross-section. Hence the product of area and velocity is conserved.

**Step 2:** Express the inlet velocity $V_1$ in terms of throat velocity $V_2$.

$$
V_1 = \frac{A_2}{A_1} V_2
$$

*Logic:* This is a direct rearrangement of the continuity equation, solving for $V_1$ so we can eliminate it later in Bernoulli's equation.

**Step 3:** Apply **Bernoulli's Equation** between Section 1 and Section 2 (no elevation change, ideal flow).

$$
\frac{P_1}{\gamma} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\gamma} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2
$$

Since $z_1 = z_2$ (horizontal setup), the elevation terms cancel out.

$$
\frac{P_1}{\gamma} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} = \frac{P_2}{\gamma} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g}
$$

*Logic:* Bernoulli's principle states that in steady, incompressible, inviscid flow, the total mechanical energy per unit weight is constant. Equating energy at two points along a streamline gives this equation.

**Step 4:** Subtract and rearrange to obtain the pressure head difference.

$$
\frac{P_1 - P_2}{\gamma} = \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2g}
$$

*Logic:* The right-hand side represents the conversion of pressure head to velocity head. The pressure at the wider section is higher because the velocity is lower.

**Step 5:** Substitute the expression for $V_1$ from Step 2 into Step 4.

$$
\frac{P_1 - P_2}{\gamma} = \frac{V_2^2 - \left(\frac{A_2}{A_1} V_2\right)^2}{2g}
$$

**Step 6:** Factor out $V_2^2$ on the right-hand side.

$$
\frac{P_1 - P_2}{\gamma} = \frac{V_2^2 \left[ 1 - \left(\frac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2 \right]}{2g}
$$

*Logic:* This isolates $V_2^2$, allowing us to solve for the throat velocity in terms of the measurable pressure difference.

**Step 7:** Solve for the throat velocity $V_2$.

$$
V_2 = \sqrt{\frac{2g \cdot \frac{P_1 - P_2}{\gamma}}{1 - \left(\frac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2}}
$$

**Step 8:** Multiply numerator and denominator by $A_1$ to obtain a clean form.

$$
V_2 = \frac{A_1}{\sqrt{A_1^2 - A_2^2}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2g(P_1 - P_2)}{\gamma}}
$$

*Logic:* The grouping $A_1 / \sqrt{A_1^2 - A_2^2}$ emerges naturally from the algebra and is a standard KTU result.

**Step 9:** The theoretical discharge is $Q_{th} = A_2 V_2$.

$$
Q_{th} = A_2 \cdot \frac{A_1}{\sqrt{A_1^2 - A_2^2}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2g(P_1 - P_2)}{\gamma}}
$$

**Step 10:** Introduce the manometric head $h$ (in metres of the flowing fluid) to replace the pressure term.

$$
h = \frac{P_1 - P_2}{\gamma} = x \left[ \frac{\rho_m}{\rho} - 1 \right]
$$

*Logic:* $h$ is the equivalent head of the flowing fluid that produces the same pressure difference as the manometer deflection $x$ of the heavier manometric fluid. The factor $[\rho_m/\rho - 1]$ accounts for the buoyancy of the displaced flowing fluid.

**Step 11:** Substitute $h$ into the discharge equation to obtain the final theoretical formula.

$$
Q_{th} = \frac{A_1 A_2}{\sqrt{A_1^2 - A_2^2}} \cdot \sqrt{2gh}
$$

*Logic:* This is the standard, board-exam-friendly form. It expresses discharge purely in terms of geometry ($A_1$, $A_2$) and a single measurable quantity ($h$).

**Step 12:** Account for real-world losses by introducing the coefficient of discharge $C_d$.

$$
Q_{act} = C_d \cdot \frac{A_1 A_2}{\sqrt{A_1^2 - A_2^2}} \cdot \sqrt{2gh}
$$

*Logic:* $C_d$ is an empirically determined correction factor that lumps together all the deviations from ideal flow (viscous friction, minor turbulence losses, slight asymmetry of flow, etc.).

### 3.2 Derivation of the Manometric Head $h$

Consider a U-tube differential manometer connected between the inlet and throat. Let the manometer fluid be mercury (density $\rho_{Hg}$) and the flowing fluid be water (density $\rho_w$). Let $x$ be the deflection of mercury in the manometer limbs (in metres).

Starting from the inlet, traverse the manometer down through the heavier fluid and back up:

$$
P_1 + \rho_w g (y_1) - \rho_{Hg} g (x) - \rho_w g (y_1 - x) = P_2
$$

where $y_1$ is the height of water column above mercury on the inlet side. Simplifying:

$$
P_1 - P_2 = \rho_{Hg} g x - \rho_w g x
$$

$$
P_1 - P_2 = g x (\rho_{Hg} - \rho_w)
$$

Dividing both sides by $\gamma_w = \rho_w g$:

$$
\frac{P_1 - P_2}{\gamma_w} = x \left( \frac{\rho_{Hg}}{\rho_w} - 1 \right) = h
$$

This is the manometric head expressed in metres of the flowing fluid.

### 3.3 Analytical Summary Matrix

| **Symbol** | **Meaning** | **Typical Unit** | **Origin** |
|:----------:|:------------|:----------------:|:-----------|
| $A_1, A_2$ | Inlet and throat cross-sectional area | $\text{m}^2$ | Geometry of meter |
| $D_1, D_2$ | Inlet and throat diameter | $\text{m}$ | Geometry of meter |
| $V_1, V_2$ | Inlet and throat velocity | $\text{m/s}$ | From continuity |
| $P_1, P_2$ | Inlet and throat pressure | $\text{N/m}^2$ (Pa) | Bernoulli |
| $h$ | Manometric head in metres of fluid | $\text{m}$ | From manometer |
| $x$ | Manometer deflection | $\text{m}$ or $\text{cm}$ | Measured |
| $Q_{th}$ | Theoretical discharge | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ | From formula |
| $Q_{act}$ | Actual discharge | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ | Measured |
| $C_d$ | Coefficient of discharge | Dimensionless | $Q_{act} / Q_{th}$ |
| $Re$ | Reynolds number at throat | Dimensionless | $\rho V_2 D_2 / \mu$ |

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU lab record typically requires a table of at least **5 to 6 readings** of $Q_{act}$ vs $h$ (or vs $Q_{th}$), from which $C_d$ is calculated for each. The mean value of $C_d$ is reported, and a graph of $C_d$ vs $Re$ is plotted.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the diverging cone is longer than the converging cone:** The diverging cone has a smaller angle (≈ 5° to 7°) compared to the converging cone (≈ 19° to 23°). This gradual expansion prevents boundary layer separation, suppresses turbulence, and recovers most of the kinetic energy as pressure energy — yielding the high $C_d$ value characteristic of Venturi meters.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define a Venturi meter. State Bernoulli's theorem as applied to it.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **Venturi meter** is a device used to measure the discharge of a fluid flowing through a closed pipe. It consists of a converging cone, a cylindrical throat, and a diverging recovery cone. The pressure difference between the inlet and the throat is measured using a manometer, and the discharge is calculated from this difference.

**Bernoulli's Theorem as Applied to a Venturi Meter:** For a steady, incompressible, inviscid flow along a horizontal Venturi meter, the sum of pressure head and velocity head is constant between the inlet and the throat.

$$
\frac{P_1}{\gamma} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} = \frac{P_2}{\gamma} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g}
$$

This implies that as the area contracts from inlet to throat, the velocity increases and the pressure drops proportionally. **(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for the statement, 1 mark for the equation with the implication.)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is the coefficient of discharge of a Venturi meter? Why is its value higher than that of an orifice meter?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **coefficient of discharge ($C_d$)** of a Venturi meter is defined as the ratio of the actual discharge to the theoretical discharge, both measured under identical conditions.

$$
C_d = \frac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{theoretical}}
$$

It is a dimensionless number, typically between **0.95 and 0.99** for a well-designed Venturi meter.

The value of $C_d$ for a Venturi meter is **higher than that of an orifice meter (≈ 0.62 to 0.65)** because of the following reasons:

1. There is **no vena contracta** formation in a Venturi meter — the gradual converging cone smoothly accelerates the flow.
2. The **diverging recovery cone** gradually decelerates the flow, minimizing turbulence and eddy losses.
3. The smooth, streamlined geometry of the throat reduces frictional losses significantly.
4. Orifice meters, in contrast, have a sharp-edged plate that causes abrupt flow contraction, flow separation, and significant energy dissipation.

**(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for typical value, 1 mark for comparative reasons.)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the theoretical discharge through a horizontal Venturi meter, starting from Bernoulli's equation and the continuity equation. State clearly the meaning of every term. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A horizontal Venturi meter has an inlet diameter of 20 cm and a throat diameter of 10 cm. Water flows through it at a rate of 30 litres/second. The pressure at the inlet is 1.5 bar (gauge). Calculate: (i) the pressure at the throat, and (ii) the manometer deflection in cm of mercury, if a differential mercury manometer is connected across the meter. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Question A:**

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks):**

**Given:** A horizontal Venturi meter with inlet at Section 1 (diameter $D_1$, area $A_1$, pressure $P_1$, velocity $V_1$) and throat at Section 2 (diameter $D_2$, area $A_2$, pressure $P_2$, velocity $V_2$).

**Step 1 — Continuity Equation:**

$$
A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_1 = \frac{A_2}{A_1} V_2
$$

**Step 2 — Bernoulli's Equation (horizontal, so $z_1 = z_2$):**

$$
\frac{P_1}{\gamma} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} = \frac{P_2}{\gamma} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g}
$$

**Step 3 — Pressure head difference:**

$$
\frac{P_1 - P_2}{\gamma} = \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2g}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $V_1$:**

$$
\frac{P_1 - P_2}{\gamma} = \frac{V_2^2 - \left(\frac{A_2}{A_1} V_2\right)^2}{2g} = \frac{V_2^2}{2g} \left[ 1 - \left(\frac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2 \right]
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $V_2$:**

$$
V_2 = \frac{A_1}{\sqrt{A_1^2 - A_2^2}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2g(P_1 - P_2)}{\gamma}}
$$

**Step 6 — Theoretical discharge $Q_{th} = A_2 V_2$:**

$$
Q_{th} = \frac{A_1 A_2}{\sqrt{A_1^2 - A_2^2}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2g(P_1 - P_2)}{\gamma}}
$$

**Step 7 — In terms of manometric head $h$:**

$$
\boxed{\, Q_{th} = \frac{A_1 A_2}{\sqrt{A_1^2 - A_2^2}} \cdot \sqrt{2gh} \,}
$$

where $h = x \left( \dfrac{\rho_m}{\rho} - 1 \right)$.

**Mark Distribution:** Continuity (1), Bernoulli (1), Substitution (1), Algebraic simplification (1), Final expression in terms of $h$ (1), Term explanation (1), Final boxed answer (1).

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks):**

**Given:**
- $D_1 = 20 \text{ cm} = 0.20 \text{ m}$, $D_2 = 10 \text{ cm} = 0.10 \text{ m}$
- $Q = 30 \text{ L/s} = 30 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 0.030 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$
- $P_1 = 1.5 \text{ bar (gauge)} = 1.5 \times 10^5 \text{ N/m}^2$
- $\rho_w = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\rho_{Hg} = 13600 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

**Step 1 — Compute areas:**

$$
A_1 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.20)^2 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.04) = 0.03142 \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.10)^2 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.01) = 0.007854 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Compute velocities using $Q = AV$:**

$$
V_1 = \frac{Q}{A_1} = \frac{0.030}{0.03142} = 0.9549 \text{ m/s}
$$

$$
V_2 = \frac{Q}{A_2} = \frac{0.030}{0.007854} = 3.820 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Bernoulli's equation to find $P_2$:**

$$
\frac{P_1}{\gamma} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} = \frac{P_2}{\gamma} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g}
$$

$$
\frac{P_2}{\gamma} = \frac{P_1}{\gamma} + \frac{V_1^2 - V_2^2}{2g}
$$

Compute each term:

$$
\frac{P_1}{\gamma} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^5}{9810} = 15.290 \text{ m of water}
$$

$$
\frac{V_1^2}{2g} = \frac{(0.9549)^2}{2 \times 9.81} = \frac{0.9118}{19.62} = 0.0465 \text{ m}
$$

$$
\frac{V_2^2}{2g} = \frac{(3.820)^2}{2 \times 9.81} = \frac{14.592}{19.62} = 0.7437 \text{ m}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\frac{P_2}{\gamma} = 15.290 + 0.0465 - 0.7437 = 14.593 \text{ m of water}
$$

$$
P_2 = 14.593 \times 9810 = 1.4316 \times 10^5 \text{ N/m}^2 = 1.4316 \text{ bar (gauge)}
$$

**Step 4 — Manometric head $h$ (in metres of water):**

$$
h = \frac{P_1 - P_2}{\gamma} = 15.290 - 14.593 = 0.697 \text{ m of water}
$$

**Step 5 — Manometer deflection $x$ in cm of mercury:**

$$
h = x \left( \frac{\rho_{Hg}}{\rho_w} - 1 \right) = x \left( \frac{13600}{1000} - 1 \right) = 12.6 \, x
$$

$$
x = \frac{0.697}{12.6} = 0.0553 \text{ m} = 5.53 \text{ cm of mercury}
$$

**Final Answers:**
- **(i)** Pressure at throat $P_2 \approx 1.432 \text{ bar (gauge)}$
- **(ii)** Manometer deflection $x \approx 5.53 \text{ cm of Hg}$

**Mark Distribution:** Area and velocity calculation (2), Bernoulli application (2), Pressure at throat (1), Manometric head and deflection (1), Units and final answers (1).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the construction and working of a Venturi meter. Explain why its diverging section is made longer than the converging section. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** During a calibration test on a Venturi meter with inlet diameter 15 cm and throat diameter 7.5 cm, the following observations were recorded. For a manometer deflection of 10 cm of mercury, the actual volume of water collected in 60 seconds was 0.18 m³. Calculate the coefficient of discharge $C_d$ of the meter. Take $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Question B:**

**Part (a) — Description (7 Marks):**

A **Venturi meter** consists of the following parts (refer to Section 4 diagram):

1. **Inlet Section:** A straight cylindrical pipe of diameter $D_1$ where the flow is undisturbed. A pressure tap is provided here to measure $P_1$.

2. **Converging Cone:** A frustum that gradually reduces the cross-section from $D_1$ to the throat diameter $D_2$. The half-angle is typically 19° to 23° (or about 21° in standard designs). This section accelerates the fluid and converts pressure energy into kinetic energy.

3. **Throat Section:** A short cylindrical portion of uniform small diameter $D_2$. The flow velocity is maximum here, and pressure is minimum. A second pressure tap is provided at the throat to measure $P_2$.

4. **Diverging Recovery Cone:** A long frustum that gradually expands the cross-section from $D_2$ back to the original $D_1$. The half-angle is small, typically 5° to 7°.

**Why the Diverging Section is Longer:**

The diverging section is made longer (and at a smaller angle) than the converging section for the following critical reasons:

- **Prevention of Flow Separation:** A gradual expansion prevents the boundary layer from separating from the walls, avoiding the formation of eddies and vena contracta.
- **Pressure Recovery:** The kinetic energy gained in the converging section is gradually reconverted into pressure energy, recovering most of the pressure drop.
- **Minimization of Head Loss:** A long, gradual expansion minimizes frictional and turbulence losses, giving a high $C_d$ (≈ 0.95 to 0.99).
- **Energy Efficiency:** For a given flow rate, the overall head loss is much less than an orifice meter, saving pumping energy over the lifetime of the installation.

**Working Principle:** When fluid flows through the meter, the velocity increases and pressure decreases at the throat. The differential manometer connected between the inlet and throat taps shows a deflection $x$ proportional to the square of the flow rate. From this deflection, the discharge is calculated.

**Mark Distribution:** Construction parts identification (2), Sketch description (1), Working explanation (2), Reason for longer diverging section (2).

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks):**

**Given:**
- $D_1 = 15 \text{ cm} = 0.15 \text{ m}$, $D_2 = 7.5 \text{ cm} = 0.075 \text{ m}$
- Manometer deflection $x = 10 \text{ cm} = 0.10 \text{ m}$ of mercury
- Volume collected $V = 0.18 \text{ m}^3$ in time $t = 60 \text{ s}$
- $\rho_w = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\rho_{Hg} = 13600 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

**Step 1 — Compute actual discharge:**

$$
Q_{act} = \frac{V}{t} = \frac{0.18}{60} = 0.003 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 3 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute areas:**

$$
A_1 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.15)^2 = 0.01767 \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.075)^2 = 0.004418 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Compute manometric head $h$ in metres of water:**

$$
h = x \left( \frac{\rho_{Hg}}{\rho_w} - 1 \right) = 0.10 \times (13.6 - 1) = 0.10 \times 12.6 = 1.26 \text{ m of water}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the velocity factor:**

$$
\frac{A_1 A_2}{\sqrt{A_1^2 - A_2^2}} = \frac{0.01767 \times 0.004418}{\sqrt{(0.01767)^2 - (0.004418)^2}}
$$

$$
= \frac{7.808 \times 10^{-5}}{\sqrt{3.122 \times 10^{-4} - 1.952 \times 10^{-5}}}
$$

$$
= \frac{7.808 \times 10^{-5}}{\sqrt{2.927 \times 10^{-4}}} = \frac{7.808 \times 10^{-5}}{0.01711} = 4.564 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 5 — Compute theoretical discharge:**

$$
Q_{th} = \frac{A_1 A_2}{\sqrt{A_1^2 - A_2^2}} \cdot \sqrt{2gh}
$$

$$
\sqrt{2gh} = \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 1.26} = \sqrt{24.7212} = 4.972 \text{ m/s}
$$

$$
Q_{th} = 4.564 \times 10^{-3} \times 4.972 = 2.270 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 0.02270 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute coefficient of discharge:**

$$
\boxed{\, C_d = \frac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} = \frac{0.003}{0.02270} = 0.1321 \,}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **The computed $C_d \approx 0.132$ is unusually low** for a Venturi meter (typical: 0.95 to 0.99). This suggests either an **error in the observation data** (perhaps the manometer deflection or volume was misread) or that the problem is testing whether students can mechanically apply the formula. In a real lab, $C_d < 0.9$ would indicate a defective meter. In KTU exams, the formula application is what is graded, so show all your steps cleanly.

**Mark Distribution:** Actual discharge (1), Area calculations (1), Manometric head (1), Velocity factor (2), Theoretical discharge (1), Final $C_d$ (1).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks in Venturi Meter Problems:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the $\rho_m/\rho - 1$ factor** when converting manometer deflection to head. If the manometer fluid is mercury and the flowing fluid is water, students often write $h = x$ instead of $h = 12.6x$ (in metres). This single error can cost 2 to 3 marks in Part B.
>
> 2. **Unit Inconsistencies:** Mixing cm and m. The area formula requires $D$ in **metres** (not cm) to get $A$ in $\text{m}^2$. Many students substitute cm and forget to convert, producing answers off by a factor of 100 or 10000.
>
> 3. **Confusing Actual and Theoretical Discharge:** Some students compute the *theoretical* discharge and report it as the *actual* discharge, or vice versa. Always remember: $Q_{act} = V/t$ (from experiment) and $Q_{th}$ = from the formula. $C_d = Q_{act} / Q_{th}$.
>
> 4. **Forgetting the $A_1/\sqrt{A_1^2 - A_2^2}$ velocity factor** and writing $Q = A_2 \sqrt{2gh}$ as if it were an orifice formula. This loses the inlet area information entirely and is a classic KTU trap.
>
> 5. **Sign Error in Bernoulli's Equation:** The pressure drop must satisfy $P_1 > P_2$. If the algebra gives a negative manometric head, the student has set up the equation with the wrong sign convention.
>
> 6. **Skipping the Continuity Step:** The derivation marks are split across continuity (1 mark), Bernoulli (1 mark), substitution (1 mark), and final result (1 mark). Jumping directly to the discharge formula without showing the intermediate step loses 2 to 3 marks.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Venturi Meter Calibration**

- **Definition:** A Venturi meter is a differential pressure flow measuring device with three sections: **converging cone → throat → diverging cone**. (✓)
- **Operating Principle:** **Bernoulli's Equation** (energy conservation) + **Continuity Equation** (mass conservation). (✓)
- **Core Discharge Formula:**

$$
Q_{act} = C_d \cdot \frac{A_1 A_2}{\sqrt{A_1^2 - A_2^2}} \cdot \sqrt{2gh}
$$

- **Manometric Head Conversion:** Always use $h = x \left( \dfrac{\rho_m}{\rho} - 1 \right)$ to convert the manometer reading into metres of the flowing fluid. (✓)
- **Typical $C_d$ Range:** **0.95 to 0.99** for a well-designed Venturi meter; much higher than an orifice meter (0.62 to 0.65). (✓)
- **Actual Discharge:** Measured experimentally as $Q_{act} = \dfrac{\text{Volume of water collected}}{\text{Time taken}}$. (✓)
- **Why Diverging Cone is Long and Shallow:** To **prevent flow separation**, **recover pressure energy**, and **minimize head loss**. (✓)
- **Calibration Plot:** A graph of **$C_d$ vs Reynolds number** (or $C_d$ vs $Q_{act}$) is the standard deliverable in the KTU lab record. (✓)
- **Standard Constants:** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$, $\rho_{water} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\rho_{Hg} = 13600 \text{ kg/m}^3$. (✓)
- **Viva Favourites:**
  - "Why is the throat velocity higher than inlet velocity?" → Continuity equation: smaller area means higher velocity.
  - "Why does the pressure drop at the throat?" → Bernoulli: higher velocity means lower pressure.
  - "What happens if air enters the manometer?" → Reading becomes erratic; air must be fully bled out before taking readings.
  - "What is the difference between Venturi meter and orifice meter?" → Venturi: smooth, gradual, high $C_d$, low head loss. Orifice: sharp-edged, abrupt, low $C_d$, high head loss. (✓)
- **Engineering Applications:** Water supply mains, chemical process flow, power plant feedwater, HVAC systems, aircraft fuel systems, medical ventilators. (✓)
- **Unit Conversions to Memorise:** $1 \text{ bar} = 10^5 \text{ N/m}^2$, $1 \text{ L} = 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3$, $1 \text{ cm Hg} = 0.01 \text{ m Hg}$, $\rho_{Hg}/\rho_{water} = 13.6$, so $h/x = 12.6$ for a mercury-water manometer. (✓)

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Tip (KTU 2024 Scheme):** In Part B, the derivation question is almost always worth 7 marks. Memorize the *order* of steps: **Continuity → Bernoulli → Eliminate $V_1$ → Solve for $V_2$ → Multiply by $A_2$ → Substitute $h$**. If you write these six steps clearly, you secure full marks regardless of algebraic memory gaps.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Calibration of Orifice Meter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCEL308 - FLUID MECHANICS LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Calibration of Orifice Meter ## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) An **Orifice Meter** is a differential pressure type flow-measuring device used to measure the rate of fluid flow in a closed conduit (pipe). It consists of a flat, circular concentric **orifice plate** (a thin, sharp-edged disc with a precisely machined hole) inserted between two flanges in a pipeline. The orifice diameter ($d_2$) is always smaller than the pipe diameter ($d_1$). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Calibration** of an orifice meter is the experimental procedure of determining its **Coefficient of Discharge ($C_d$)** by comparing the **actual volumetric flow rate** ($Q_{act}$) collected in a measuring tank over a known time with the **theoretical flow rate** ($Q_{th}$) predicted by Bernoulli's equation. The calibrated $C_d$ is then used as an empirical correction factor for field installations. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Think of a garden hose (the pipe) and your thumb partially covering the nozzle (the orifice). When you press your thumb on the water stream, the water speeds up (velocity increases) and the pressure at the nozzle drops. The orifice meter works on exactly the same principle — fluid is forced through a smaller hole, its velocity increases, and the pressure drop across the plate becomes a measurable signature of the flow rate. **Intuitive Summary of the Three Coefficients:** - **Coefficient of Contraction ($C_c$):** The jet of fluid emerging from the orifice shrinks to a minimum cross-section called the **vena contracta** before re-expanding. $C_c = \dfrac{A_{vc}}{A_o}$ (ratio of jet area at vena contracta to orifice area). - **Coefficient of Velocity ($C_v$):** Due to viscous friction and turbulence, the real velocity is less than the ideal (theoretical) velocity. $C_v = \dfrac{V_{actual}}{V_{theoretical}}$. - **Coefficient of Discharge ($C_d$):** Combines both losses. $C_d = \dfrac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{theoretical}}$. > [!NOTE] > For a sharp-edged concentric orifice, the standard KTU-accepted range is **$C_d \approx 0.60$ to $0.65$**. ### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics - Acceleration due to gravity: $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ (used in theoretical discharge formula). - Density of water at standard lab temperature (25°C): $\rho_w = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$. - Density of mercury (manometric fluid): $\rho_{Hg} = 13600 \text{ kg/m}^3$. - Specific gravity of mercury: $S_{Hg} = 13.6$. ## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ### 2.1 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic 1. **Steady, incompressible, inviscid flow** is assumed to be flowing through a horizontal pipe of cross-section $A_1 = \dfrac{\pi}{4}d_1^2$. 2. A concentric orifice plate of cross-section $A_2 = \dfrac{\pi}{4}d_2^2$ is introduced. By continuity, fluid velocity must increase ($V_2 > V_1$). 3. By **Bernoulli's equation** (conservation of energy), the increase in kinetic energy is compensated by a **drop in pressure head** across the plate. 4. This pressure drop ($\Delta P = P_1 - P_2$) is measured using a **differential U-tube manometer** (usually containing mercury) connected at taps located **one diameter upstream** and **half diameter downstream** of the orifice plate (D and D/2 tapping arrangement as per ISO 5167). 5. The actual volume of water discharged in a fixed time $t$ is collected in a **calibrated collecting/measuring tank**, and $Q_{act}$ is computed. 6. The ratio $\dfrac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}}$ yields the empirical **Coefficient of Discharge, $C_d$**, which calibrates the instrument. ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes | |---|----------|---------|-------|-------| | 1 | Pipe cross-section | $A_1 = \dfrac{\pi}{4}d_1^2$ | m² | Upstream area | | 2 | Orifice cross-section | $A_2 = \dfrac{\pi}{4}d_2^2$ | m² | Orifice area | | 3 | Differential manometer head | $H = \left(\dfrac{\rho_{Hg}}{\rho_w} - 1\right)h = 12.6\,h$ | m of water | For U-tube Hg manometer | | 4 | Theoretical discharge | $Q_{th} = \dfrac{A_1 A_2 \sqrt{2gH}}{\sqrt{A_1^2 - A_2^2}}$ | m³/s | Derived from Bernoulli | | 5 | Simplified form | $Q_{th} = C_d'\,A_2\sqrt{2gH}$ | m³/s | Where $C_d' = \dfrac{C_c\,C_v}{\sqrt{1-C_c^2\,(A_2/A_1)^2}}$ | | 6 | Actual discharge | $Q_{act} = \dfrac{A_{tank} \times R}{t}$ | m³/s | Volume / time | | 7 | Experimental $C_d$ | $C_d = \dfrac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}}$ | dimensionless | The primary calibration output | | 8 | Discharge rate | $V' = \dfrac{A_{tank} \times R}{1000\,t}$ | L/s | Often plotted on Y-axis | | 9 | Vena contracta coefficient | $C_c = \dfrac{A_{vena}}{A_2}$ | dimensionless | For sharp orifice $\approx 0.62$ | | 10 | Velocity coefficient | $C_v = \dfrac{V_{actual}}{V_{ideal}}$ | dimensionless | For sharp orifice $\approx 0.97$ | | 11 | Fundamental relation | $C_d = C_c \times C_v$ | dimensionless | Master identity | | 12 | Reynolds number | $Re = \dfrac{\rho V d_1}{\mu} = \dfrac{V d_1}{\nu}$ | dimensionless | For reporting flow regime | > [!IMPORTANT] > **CRITICAL FORMULA NOTE FOR KTU EXAMS:** When the manometer reads a deflection $h$ (in metres) of mercury and the flowing fluid is water, the equivalent pressure head in metres of water is **$H = 12.6\,h$**, NOT $H = h$. The factor $(\rho_{Hg}/\rho_w - 1) = (13.6 - 1) = 12.6$ is the most-skipped step in board papers. ### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility Orifice meters are the **workhorses of industrial flow measurement** because they are cheap, simple, robust, and easy to install. They are extensively used in: - **Oil & gas pipelines** for custody transfer measurement of crude oil, natural gas, and refined products. - **Water treatment plants** to measure flow in large distribution mains. - **Steam and compressed air** lines in thermal power stations. - **Chemical process industries** for monitoring feed rates of acids, alkalis, and solvents. - **HVAC systems** for chilled water and condenser water circuits. The calibration data ($C_d$ vs. Reynolds number or vs. $V'$) obtained in this lab is then used to size and predict flow in commercial flow meters of identical geometry. ## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Theoretical Discharge Formula ($Q_{th}$) **Starting assumptions** — Steady, incompressible, frictionless flow along a streamline from section 1 (upstream, at the upstream tap) to section 2 (downstream, at the vena contracta). **Step 1: Apply Continuity Equation** (conservation of mass, incompressible flow): $$A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$$ Solving for $V_1$: $$V_1 = \frac{A_2 V_2}{A_1}$$ **Step 2: Apply Bernoulli's Equation** between sections 1 and 2 (horizontal pipe, so $z_1 = z_2$): $$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2$$ Since $z_1 = z_2$ (horizontal pipe), the equation reduces to: $$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g}$$ **Step 3: Rearrange** to express the pressure head difference: $$\frac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho g} = \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2g}$$ Define the **piezometric head difference** in metres of water as $H = \dfrac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho g}$. Then: $$H = \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2g}$$ **Step 4: Substitute** $V_1 = \dfrac{A_2 V_2}{A_1}$ into the equation: $$H = \frac{V_2^2 - \left(\dfrac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2 V_2^2}{2g} = \frac{V_2^2 \left(1 - \dfrac{A_2^2}{A_1^2}\right)}{2g}$$ **Step 5: Solve for $V_2$** (the theoretical velocity at the orifice): $$V_2 = \sqrt{\frac{2gH}{1 - \left(\dfrac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2}}$$ **Step 6: Multiply by orifice area** $A_2$ to get theoretical volumetric discharge: $$Q_{th} = A_2 V_2 = \frac{A_2 \sqrt{2gH}}{\sqrt{1 - \left(\dfrac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2}}$$ **Step 7: Rationalise the denominator** by multiplying numerator and denominator by $A_1$: $$Q_{th} = \frac{A_1 A_2 \sqrt{2gH}}{\sqrt{A_1^2 - A_2^2}}$$ > [!NOTE] > This is the **master theoretical discharge equation** that KTU expects you to derive in Part-B 14-mark questions. Memorize every algebraic step. ### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Manometer Equation Derivation For a U-tube differential manometer with mercury as the manometric fluid and water as the flowing fluid: Let $P_1$ and $P_2$ be the pressures at the two tapping points. Let $h$ be the differential mercury level deflection (in metres). Let $\rho_m$ be the density of mercury and $\rho_w$ be the density of water. Writing the manometer equation by equating pressures at the lower mercury level (left limb): $$P_1 + \rho_w g (y + h) = P_2 + \rho_w g y + \rho_m g h$$ Cancelling $\rho_w g y$ from both sides: $$P_1 + \rho_w g h = P_2 + \rho_m g h$$ Rearranging: $$P_1 - P_2 = \rho_m g h - \rho_w g h = (\rho_m - \rho_w) g h$$ Dividing both sides by $\rho_w g$ to express in **metres of water (head)**: $$\frac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho_w g} = \frac{(\rho_m - \rho_w) g h}{\rho_w g} = \left(\frac{\rho_m}{\rho_w} - 1\right) h$$ Therefore: $$H = \left(\frac{\rho_m}{\rho_w} - 1\right) h = \left(\frac{13.6 \times 1000}{1000} - 1\right) h = 12.6\,h$$ Substituting this $H$ in the master discharge formula gives the working equation: $$Q_{th} = \frac{A_1 A_2 \sqrt{2g \cdot 12.6\,h}}{\sqrt{A_1^2 - A_2^2}}$$ ### 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Board Style) **Given:** Pipe diameter $d_1 = 50 \text{ mm} = 0.05 \text{ m}$, orifice diameter $d_2 = 25 \text{ mm} = 0.025 \text{ m}$, manometer deflection $h = 0.05 \text{ m}$ of Hg, collecting tank area $A_{tank} = 0.5 \text{ m}^2$, rise in level $R = 0.2 \text{ m}$, collection time $t = 30 \text{ s}$. **Step 1: Compute cross-sectional areas** $$A_1 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.05)^2 = 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$ $$A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.025)^2 = 4.9087 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$ **Step 2: Compute equivalent water head** $$H = 12.6 \times 0.05 = 0.63 \text{ m of water}$$ **Step 3: Compute theoretical discharge** $$Q_{th} = \frac{(1.9635 \times 10^{-3})(4.9087 \times 10^{-4})\sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.63}}{\sqrt{(1.9635 \times 10^{-3})^2 - (4.9087 \times 10^{-4})^2}}$$ Numerator computation: $$\sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.63} = \sqrt{12.3606} = 3.5158 \text{ m/s}$$ $$(1.9635 \times 10^{-3})(4.9087 \times 10^{-4}) = 9.638 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^4$$ $$\text{Numerator} = 9.638 \times 10^{-7} \times 3.5158 = 3.388 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^5/\text{s}$$ Denominator computation: $$(1.9635 \times 10^{-3})^2 = 3.8553 \times 10^{-6}$$ $$(4.9087 \times 10^{-4})^2 = 2.4095 \times 10^{-7}$$ $$A_1^2 - A_2^2 = 3.8553 \times 10^{-6} - 2.4095 \times 10^{-7} = 3.6144 \times 10^{-6}$$ $$\sqrt{3.6144 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.9012 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$ Final $Q_{th}$: $$Q_{th} = \frac{3.388 \times 10^{-6}}{1.9012 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.782 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 1.782 \text{ L/s}$$ **Step 4: Compute actual discharge** $$Q_{act} = \frac{A_{tank} \times R}{t} = \frac{0.5 \times 0.2}{30} = 3.333 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 3.333 \text{ L/s}$$ **Step 5: Compute Coefficient of Discharge** $$C_d = \frac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} = \frac{3.333 \times 10^{-3}}{1.782 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.870$$ > [!WARNING] > A $C_d$ value greater than 1 is **physically impossible** for an orifice meter. This indicates an **experimental error** — most likely due to incorrect manometer reading, a leaking pipe, or wrong tank calibration. The expected value for a sharp-edged orifice is **0.60 to 0.65**. In a real exam, the examiner will verify your numbers; always check physical plausibility. ### 3.4 Calibration Graph (Mandatory Viva + Record) Two graphs are typically plotted on the same X-axis ($\sqrt{H}$ in m$^{1/2}$): - **Curve 1:** $Q_{act}$ (L/s) on Y-axis vs. $\sqrt{H}$ on X-axis — gives a **straight line** through origin. - **Curve 2:** $Q_{th}$ (L/s) on Y-axis vs. $\sqrt{H}$ on X-axis — gives a **straight line** with steeper slope. - The **average slope ratio** of the two lines yields the calibrated $C_d$. This linear relationship $Q \propto \sqrt{H}$ is the **fundamental calibration law** of all differential head flow meters. ## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ### 4.1 Functional Block Architecture of the Orifice Meter Test Rig The following Mermaid block diagram represents the **physical setup and signal flow** of the experiment from the supply source to the data logging. ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA["SUMP TANK
Underground Reservoir"] nodeB["CENTRIFUGAL PUMP
1 HP Monoblock"] nodeC["STRAINER
Removes Debris"] nodeD["SUPPLY TANK
Overhead With Overflow"] nodeE["STILLING CHAMBER
Flow Straightener"] nodeF["BY PASS VALVE
Flow Regulation"] nodeG["ORIFICE PLATE
d1 Pipe d2 Orifice"] nodeH["DIFFERENTIAL
U TUBE MANOMETER
Mercury Filled"] nodeI["CONTROL VALVE
Downstream Throttle"] nodeJ["COLLECTING TANK
Calibrated V Notch"] nodeK["STOPWATCH
Time Measurement"] nodeL["DATA TABLE
h R t Computation"] nodeA --> nodeB nodeB --> nodeC nodeC --> nodeD nodeD --> nodeE nodeE --> nodeF nodeF --> nodeG nodeG --> nodeH nodeG --> nodeI nodeI --> nodeJ nodeJ --> nodeK nodeJ --> nodeL nodeH --> nodeL nodeK --> nodeL ``` ### 4.2 Step-by-Step Experimental Procedure Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD stepA["STEP 1: Fill Sump Tank and Prime Pump"] stepB["STEP 2: Open Fully the Bypass Valve and Downstream Valve"] stepC["STEP 3: Start Pump and Allow Steady Flow"] stepD["STEP 4: Slowly Throttle Downstream Valve to Set a Definite Flow"] stepE["STEP 5: Wait for Steady Manometer Reading h mm of Hg"] stepF["STEP 6: Divert Flow to Collecting Tank Using 3 Way Valve"] stepG["STEP 7: Start Stopwatch When Level Crosses Zero Mark"] stepH["STEP 8: Stop Watch at R cm Rise After 20 to 30 Seconds"] stepI["STEP 9: Record h R t in Observation Table"] stepJ["STEP 10: Vary Flow Rate 5 to 6 Times and Repeat Steps 4 to 9"] stepK["STEP 11: Compute Cd for Each Set and Plot Q vs Square Root H Graph"] stepL["STEP 12: Tabulate Mean Cd and Report Result"] stepA --> stepB --> stepC --> stepD --> stepE --> stepF --> stepG --> stepH --> stepI --> stepJ --> stepK --> stepL ``` ### 4.3 Block Diagram of the Coefficient of Discharge Hierarchy ```mermaid flowchart TB master["MASTER COEFFICIENT
Cd equals Qact divided by Qth"] cc["COEFFICIENT OF CONTRACTION
Cc equals Avena divided by Aorifice
Range 0.61 to 0.65"] cv["COEFFICIENT OF VELOCITY
Cv equals Vactual divided by Vtheoretical
Range 0.95 to 0.99"] factors["FACTORS AFFECTING Cd
1. Orifice geometry sharp edged or rounded
2. Diameter ratio d2 by d1
3. Reynolds number of flow
4. Pipe surface roughness
5. Tap location D or D by 2"] master --- cc master --- cv master --- factors ``` ### 4.4 Vena Contracta — Physical Significance (Text Schematic) The **vena contracta** is the point of minimum cross-sectional area of the fluid jet, located approximately **0.5 $d_2$ to 0.7 $d_2$** downstream of the orifice plate for a sharp-edged concentric orifice. At this point, streamlines are parallel, and the jet is at its narrowest. The existence of the vena contracta is the **physical reason** why $C_c < 1$ and consequently $C_d < 1$. ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define Coefficient of Discharge ($C_d$) of an orifice meter. State the fundamental relationship between $C_d$, $C_c$, and $C_v$. **Model Answer:** The **Coefficient of Discharge ($C_d$)** of an orifice meter is defined as the ratio of the **actual volumetric flow rate** ($Q_{act}$) to the **theoretical volumetric flow rate** ($Q_{th}$) calculated using Bernoulli's equation: $$C_d = \frac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}}$$ It is a **dimensionless** empirical constant that accounts for both the contraction of the jet and the frictional losses. For a sharp-edged concentric orifice, $C_d$ typically lies in the range **0.60 to 0.65**.
