The fundamental relationship is: $$C_d = C_c \times C_v$$ where $C_c$ is the coefficient of contraction and $C_v$ is the coefficient of velocity. **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** What is a **vena contracta**? Why does it form? **Model Answer:** The **vena contracta** is the point of minimum cross-sectional area of the fluid jet emerging from an orifice, located at a distance of approximately **0.5 to 0.7 $d_2$** downstream of the orifice plate. **Why it forms:** As fluid approaches the orifice, the streamlines cannot turn sharply through a 90° corner. They continue to converge by virtue of their inertia **even after passing the orifice edge**, until the pressure becomes uniform. This continued contraction reduces the jet cross-section below the orifice area, giving rise to the vena contracta. It is the physical origin of the coefficient of contraction $C_c < 1$. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) #### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **(a)** Derive the expression for the theoretical discharge through a sharp-edged concentric orifice meter in terms of pipe diameter $d_1$, orifice diameter $d_2$, and piezometric head difference $H$. State the assumptions clearly. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Assumptions:** 1. Steady, incompressible flow. 2. Flow is frictionless (ideal fluid). 3. The pipe is horizontal, so datum heads are equal ($z_1 = z_2$). 4. The pressure tapings measure the true static pressure (no velocity head contamination). **Derivation:** Applying **continuity equation** between upstream section (1) and downstream vena contracta (2): $$A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_1 = \frac{A_2 V_2}{A_1}$$ Applying **Bernoulli's equation** between sections 1 and 2 (with $z_1 = z_2$): $$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g}$$ Rearranging and defining $H = \dfrac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho g}$: $$H = \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2g}$$ Substituting $V_1 = \dfrac{A_2 V_2}{A_1}$: $$H = \frac{V_2^2 \left[1 - \left(\dfrac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2\right]}{2g}$$ Solving for $V_2$: $$V_2 = \frac{\sqrt{2gH}}{\sqrt{1 - \left(\dfrac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2}}$$ The theoretical discharge is: $$Q_{th} = A_2 V_2 = \frac{A_2 \sqrt{2gH}}{\sqrt{1 - \left(\dfrac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2}}$$ Rationalising the denominator: $$\boxed{Q_{th} = \frac{A_1 A_2 \sqrt{2gH}}{\sqrt{A_1^2 - A_2^2}}}$$ **(b)** A horizontal pipe of diameter **100 mm** is fitted with a concentric orifice plate of diameter **50 mm**. A differential U-tube mercury manometer connected across the orifice shows a deflection of **150 mm** of Hg. Water flows through the pipe. Determine: **(i)** the theoretical discharge, (ii) the actual discharge if the coefficient of discharge is **0.62**, and (iii) the velocity of approach. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Given data:** - $d_1 = 0.100 \text{ m}$, $d_2 = 0.050 \text{ m}$, $h = 0.150 \text{ m}$ of Hg, $C_d = 0.62$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$. **Step 1: Compute areas** $$A_1 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.1)^2 = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$ $$A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.05)^2 = 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$ **Step 2: Convert manometer reading to water head** $$H = 12.6 \times 0.150 = 1.89 \text{ m of water}$$ **Step 3: Theoretical discharge** $$\sqrt{2gH} = \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 1.89} = \sqrt{37.082} = 6.089 \text{ m/s}$$ $$A_1^2 - A_2^2 = (7.854 \times 10^{-3})^2 - (1.9635 \times 10^{-3})^2 = (61.68 - 3.855) \times 10^{-6} = 57.82 \times 10^{-6}$$ $$\sqrt{A_1^2 - A_2^2} = 7.604 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$ $$A_1 A_2 = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} = 1.5421 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^4$$ $$Q_{th} = \frac{1.5421 \times 10^{-5} \times 6.089}{7.604 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.235 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 12.35 \text{ L/s}$$ **Step 4: Actual discharge** $$Q_{act} = C_d \times Q_{th} = 0.62 \times 12.35 = 7.657 \text{ L/s}$$ **Step 5: Velocity of approach** ($V_1$) $$V_1 = \frac{Q_{act}}{A_1} = \frac{7.657 \times 10^{-3}}{7.854 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.975 \text{ m/s}$$ **Final Answers:** - $Q_{th} = 12.35 \text{ L/s}$ - $Q_{act} = 7.66 \text{ L/s}$ - $V_1 = 0.975 \text{ m/s}$ --- #### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the principle and working of an orifice meter. List any **four** advantages and **two** disadvantages. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Principle and Working:** An orifice meter operates on the principle of **measurement of flow by a pressure drop across a restriction**. A sharp-edged concentric orifice plate is inserted in a pipeline. As fluid flows through the smaller orifice, its velocity increases and pressure drops in accordance with **Bernoulli's equation**. The pressure drop is measured by a **differential U-tube manometer** and is related to the flow rate through the discharge equation: $$Q = C_d \frac{A_1 A_2}{\sqrt{A_1^2 - A_2^2}} \sqrt{2gH}$$ The complete flow line is: **Sump → Pump → Stilling chamber → Orifice assembly → Collecting tank → Sump**. The differential manometer is connected at upstream tap (D) and downstream tap (D/2). **Advantages (any four):** 1. Simple construction, low cost, and easy to install. 2. Compact, occupies very little space. 3. Can be used for a wide range of pipe diameters and fluids (water, oil, gas, steam). 4. Well-established empirical correlations and ISO standards available for accurate flow prediction. 5. Easily replaceable orifice plate; no moving parts, hence low maintenance. **Disadvantages (any two):** 1. Low coefficient of discharge ($C_d \approx 0.62$), so accuracy is less than a venturi meter. 2. High permanent pressure loss (about 50% of the differential head is lost irreversibly). 3. Prone to wear and erosion at the sharp edge over time, which changes the calibration. **(b)** In a calibration test on a 75 mm diameter orifice inserted in a 150 mm diameter pipe, the differential manometer showed a deflection of **200 mm** of Hg. In 60 seconds, the water collected in the tank rose by **250 mm**. The collecting tank is 1 m × 1 m in cross-section. Find the coefficient of discharge. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Given data:** - $d_1 = 0.150 \text{ m}$, $d_2 = 0.075 \text{ m}$, $h = 0.200 \text{ m}$ of Hg, $R = 0.250 \text{ m}$, $t = 60 \text{ s}$, $A_{tank} = 1 \text{ m}^2$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$. **Step 1: Compute areas** $$A_1 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.150)^2 = 1.767 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}^2$$ $$A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.075)^2 = 4.418 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$ **Step 2: Convert manometer reading to head of water** $$H = 12.6 \times 0.200 = 2.52 \text{ m of water}$$ **Step 3: Theoretical discharge** $$\sqrt{2gH} = \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 2.52} = \sqrt{49.4424} = 7.031 \text{ m/s}$$ $$A_1^2 - A_2^2 = (1.767 \times 10^{-2})^2 - (4.418 \times 10^{-3})^2 = (31.22 - 1.952) \times 10^{-4} = 29.27 \times 10^{-4}$$ $$\sqrt{A_1^2 - A_2^2} = 5.410 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}^2$$ $$A_1 A_2 = 1.767 \times 10^{-2} \times 4.418 \times 10^{-3} = 7.807 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^4$$ $$Q_{th} = \frac{7.807 \times 10^{-5} \times 7.031}{5.410 \times 10^{-2}} = 1.014 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$ **Step 4: Actual discharge** $$Q_{act} = \frac{A_{tank} \times R}{t} = \frac{1.0 \times 0.250}{60} = 4.167 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$ **Step 5: Coefficient of Discharge** $$C_d = \frac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} = \frac{4.167 \times 10^{-3}}{1.014 \times 10^{-2}} = 0.411$$ > [!WARNING] > The above $C_d = 0.411$ is **anomalously low** (normal range: 0.60–0.65). This indicates a probable experimental error — likely a **leak in the collecting tank**, **mis-read manometer**, or **timer lag**. In a viva or record, **always report the average $C_d$ from multiple trials** and discuss possible sources of error. **Expected $C_d \approx 0.62$** for a sharp-edged concentric orifice with $d_2/d_1 = 0.5$. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **COMMON MARK-LOSS ZONES IN ORIFICE METER QUESTIONS:** > > 1. **Forgetting the factor 12.6** when converting mercury manometer deflection to water head — costs **2 marks** instantly. Always state: $H = \left(\dfrac{\rho_{Hg}}{\rho_w} - 1\right)h = 12.6\,h$. > 2. **Wrong diameter squared order** in the denominator — must be $A_1^2 - A_2^2$, NOT $A_1 - A_2$. > 3. **Mixing up units** of areas (mm² vs. m²) — leads to $C_d$ off by a factor of 1000. > 4. **Ignoring the vena contracta** explanation in derivations — the examiner expects you to mention why $C_c$ exists. > 5. **Not reporting the average $C_d$** over all trials — always take a minimum of **5 readings** and report the mean ± standard deviation. > 6. **Omitting the calibration graph** from the lab record — KTU mandates a plot of $Q_{act}$ vs. $\sqrt{H}$ and $Q_{th}$ vs. $\sqrt{H}$ (both straight lines through origin). --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - **Orifice meter** = thin plate with a sharp-edged hole inserted in a pipe to measure flow via pressure drop. - **Working principle** = Bernoulli's equation + Continuity equation. - **Theoretical discharge master formula** (must be memorised verbatim): $$Q_{th} = \frac{A_1 A_2 \sqrt{2gH}}{\sqrt{A_1^2 - A_2^2}}$$ - **Manometer conversion (water as flowing fluid, mercury as manometric fluid)**: $H = 12.6\,h$. - **Coefficient of Discharge**: $C_d = \dfrac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}}$, dimensionless, expected value **0.60 to 0.65** for sharp-edged orifice. - **Three coefficients** must be defined separately: $C_d$ (discharge), $C_c$ (contraction), $C_v$ (velocity). Master relation: $C_d = C_c \times C_v$. - **Vena contracta** = point of minimum jet area, located **0.5–0.7 $d_2$** downstream of the plate. - **Standard tapping positions**: Upstream tap at **1$D$** from plate face, downstream tap at **0.5$D$** from plate face (flange taps per ISO 5167). - **Diameter ratio $d_2/d_1$** is the most critical geometric parameter; optimal value is **0.5**. - **Calibration procedure**: 1. Vary flow via downstream control valve. 2. Record manometer deflection $h$ and rise in collecting tank $R$ for time $t$. 3. Compute $Q_{act}$ and $Q_{th}$ for at least **5 different flow rates**. 4. Compute $C_d$ for each set; take the average. 5. Plot $Q_{act}$ vs. $\sqrt{H}$ and $Q_{th}$ vs. $\sqrt{H}$ — both should be **straight lines through origin**. - **Real-world applications**: oil & gas pipelines, water treatment plants, HVAC systems, chemical process industries, power plants. - **Advantages**: simple, cheap, compact, no moving parts, ISO-standardised. - **Disadvantages**: lower $C_d$ than venturi, high permanent head loss (~50% of $\Delta H$), edge erosion with time. - **Sources of experimental error**: air bubbles trapped in manometer, leak in collecting tank, parallax error in level reading, inaccurate stopwatch, unsteady flow during timing, mercury contamination with water. - **KTU Viva High-Yield Questions**: - Why is $C_d$ always less than 1? - What is the difference between orifice meter and venturi meter? - How does an inverted U-tube air manometer differ from a mercury manometer? - What is the effect of Reynolds number on $C_d$? - Why is the downstream tap placed at $D/2$ and not at the vena contracta?

topics.title: Calibration of Water Meter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCEL308 - FLUID MECHANICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Calibration of Water Meter

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Calibration of a Water Meter** is the experimental procedure of determining the relationship between the *actual volume of water* (or *actual discharge*) flowing through the system and the *volume/discharge indicated by the meter*. It establishes a **Calibration Factor (K-factor)** or **Correction Factor** that quantifies the meter's accuracy under varying flow conditions.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> A water meter is a positive displacement or velocity-type measuring device that records the cumulative volume of water passed through it. Calibration is the metrological process of comparing its indicated reading against a known reference standard (e.g., a calibrated collecting tank + stopwatch) to determine the instrument's accuracy, repeatability, and linearity.

The experiment belongs to the broader category of **flow measurement calibration** in fluid mechanics laboratories, where the device under test (DUT) is the water meter and the reference standard is the **volumetric tank method** (also called the *gravimetric* or *volumetric tank-stopwatch method*).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you buy a bathroom weighing scale that always shows 70 kg when you stand on it, regardless of your actual weight. To "calibrate" it, you would place known standard weights (say 50 kg, 60 kg, 70 kg) on it and note the readings. You would then build a correction table or a graph: *Actual Weight vs Indicated Weight*. A water meter calibration works on the **exact same principle**:

- The **weighing scale** = Water Meter (DUT)
- The **known weights** = Calibrated Collecting Tank (Reference Standard)
- The **stopwatch** = The Time Reference
- The **correction graph** = Calibration Curve

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is calibration necessary?**
> In real engineering practice, water meters drift over time due to wear of internal components (gears, pistons, turbine blades), fouling, or air entrainment. A meter that reads "1000 litres" might actually deliver only 980 litres (under-reading) or 1020 litres (over-reading). This is critical for **water billing, process industries, irrigation audits, and ISO 9001 quality compliance**.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following standard values are commonly used in KTU lab calculations:

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Unit | Standard Value / Note |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Acceleration due to gravity | $g$ | m/s² | **9.81 m/s²** |
| Density of water at 25 °C | $\rho$ | kg/m³ | **997 kg/m³** |
| Kinematic viscosity of water at 25 °C | $\nu$ | m²/s | **0.89 × 10⁻⁶ m²/s** |
| Dynamic viscosity of water at 25 °C | $\mu$ | Pa·s | **8.9 × 10⁻⁴ Pa·s** |

> [!TIP]
> Always note the **water temperature** during the experiment because density and viscosity change with temperature, affecting the meter's mechanical friction losses and hence its indicated reading.

## 1.4 Classification of Water Meters

Water meters used in KTU laboratories are typically of the following types:

1. **Positive Displacement (PD) Meters** – Uses a nutating disc or oscillating piston to mechanically isolate fixed volumes. Most accurate at low flow rates.
2. **Velocity Meters** – Includes turbine (multi-jet or single-jet) meters where flow velocity spins a rotor.
3. **Electromagnetic Meters** – Use Faraday's Law (no moving parts, used in industry).
4. **Ultrasonic Meters** – Measure transit-time difference of sound waves.

For the **KTU PCCEL308 lab**, the experiment usually employs a **multi-jet or piston-type domestic water meter** installed in a closed-circuit pipe loop with a sump tank, an overhead tank, and a volumetric measuring tank.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle

The experiment is based on the **principle of volumetric comparison**. A known quantity of water is allowed to pass through the water meter, and the volume recorded by the meter is compared to the **true volume** collected in a pre-calibrated tank over a measured time interval.

### 2.1.1 Step-by-Step Logic of the Calibration Process

1. **Establish steady flow** through the meter by opening the control valve to a fixed setting.
2. **Record the initial meter reading** ($V_1$) in litres or m³.
3. **Simultaneously start the stopwatch** and the water collection process.
4. **Collect water in the calibrated tank** for a measured duration $t$ (typically 30 s to 120 s).
5. **Stop the stopwatch** at the instant the collection ends.
6. **Record the final meter reading** ($V_2$).
7. **Measure the actual volume collected** ($V_{actual}$) using the rise in water level in the calibrated tank.
8. **Repeat the procedure** for at least 5–6 different flow rates (by varying the control valve).
9. **Compute the calibration factor** and **plot the calibration curve**.

## 2.2 Core 'Why' and 'How' Behind Each Step

| Step | Why? | How? |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Steady flow | To eliminate transient errors due to accelerating water column | Allow flow for 1–2 minutes before recording |
| Simultaneous start | To synchronise the time and volume measurement precisely | Use a single observer or electronic trigger |
| Multiple flow rates | To assess the **linearity** of the meter across its operating range | Adjust valve from fully closed to fully open in 5–6 steps |
| Calibrated tank | Provides a traceable reference standard (uncertainty < 0.2%) | Tank cross-section is pre-measured; level rise is read by hook gauge or sight glass |
| Calibration curve | Enables interpolation of meter error at any operating point | Plot $Q_{actual}$ (Y-axis) vs $Q_{indicated}$ (X-axis) |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table consolidates **all key equations** used in the calibration experiment and the subsequent viva voce:

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Volume of water in collecting tank** | $V_{actual} = A_{tank} \times h$ | m³ | $A_{tank}$ = cross-sectional area, $h$ = water level rise |
| 2 | **Volume indicated by meter** | $V_{indicated} = V_2 - V_1$ | m³ | Direct reading difference |
| 3 | **Actual discharge** | $Q_{actual} = \dfrac{V_{actual}}{t}$ | m³/s | True flow rate |
| 4 | **Indicated discharge** | $Q_{indicated} = \dfrac{V_{indicated}}{t}$ | m³/s | Meter's reported flow rate |
| 5 | **Calibration Factor (K)** | $K = \dfrac{V_{actual}}{V_{indicated}}$ | dimensionless | $K > 1 \Rightarrow$ under-reading; $K < 1 \Rightarrow$ over-reading |
| 6 | **Correction Factor (C)** | $C = \dfrac{V_{indicated}}{V_{actual}} = \dfrac{1}{K}$ | dimensionless | Reciprocal of K |
| 7 | **Percentage Error** | $\% E = \dfrac{Q_{indicated} - Q_{actual}}{Q_{actual}} \times 100$ | % | Signed error of the meter |
| 8 | **Absolute Error** | $\vert Q_{indicated} - Q_{actual} \vert$ | m³/s | Magnitude only |
| 9 | **Average Discharge (multiple trials)** | $\bar{Q} = \dfrac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} Q_i$ | m³/s | Statistical mean |
| 10 | **Standard Deviation of K** | $\sigma_K = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{n-1} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (K_i - \bar{K})^2}$ | dimensionless | Repeatability indicator |
| 11 | **Relative Standard Deviation (RSD)** | $\% RSD = \dfrac{\sigma_K}{\bar{K}} \times 100$ | % | Precision measure |
| 12 | **Reynolds Number in pipe** | $Re = \dfrac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \dfrac{4Q}{\pi D \nu}$ | dimensionless | For assessing flow regime |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Distinction for KTU Exams:**
> - The **Calibration Factor K** is defined as the *ratio of actual to indicated* value (K = Actual/Indicated).
> - The **Correction Factor C** is the *reciprocal* of K and is sometimes called the "meter multiplier" — it is the factor you multiply the meter reading by to obtain the true value.
> - Students often confuse the two. Memorise: **"K is Kind to the actual value"** (Actual = K × Indicated).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The calibration of water meters has direct industrial and commercial applications:

- **Municipal Water Supply**: Billing accuracy and revenue protection for water utilities.
- **ISO 4064 / BIS 779 Standards**: Compliance of domestic water meters with international metrology standards.
- **Process Industries**: Pharmaceutical and food industries where precise water consumption is critical for batch records.
- **Irrigation Engineering**: Canal and drip-irrigation flow audits.
- **Hydrostatic Testing**: Verification of cumulative water intake in cooling towers and boilers.
- **Legal Metrology**: Mandatory periodic re-verification (typically every 5 years in India under the Legal Metrology Act, 2009).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Sample Calculation (Worked Numerical)

### 3.1.1 Given Data (Typical KTU Lab Trial)

A piston-type water meter is installed in a horizontal pipe of internal diameter **D = 50 mm**. The collecting tank has a square cross-section of side **a = 1.0 m**. The following readings were obtained for one trial:

- **Time of collection, t** = **60 s**
- **Initial meter reading, V₁** = **12.345 m³**
- **Final meter reading, V₂** = **12.523 m³**
- **Initial water level in tank** = **0.200 m**
- **Final water level in tank** = **0.272 m**

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Evaluation

**Step 1: Compute the volume indicated by the meter**

$$V_{indicated} = V_2 - V_1$$

$$V_{indicated} = 12.523 - 12.345 = 0.178 \text{ m}^3$$

The subtraction directly gives the meter's recorded volume in cubic metres.

**Step 2: Compute the cross-sectional area of the collecting tank**

Since the tank is square with side $a = 1.0$ m:

$$A_{tank} = a \times a = 1.0 \times 1.0 = 1.0 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 3: Compute the actual rise in water level**

$$h = h_{final} - h_{initial} = 0.272 - 0.200 = 0.072 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4: Compute the actual volume of water collected**

$$V_{actual} = A_{tank} \times h$$

$$V_{actual} = 1.0 \times 0.072 = 0.072 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 5: Compute the actual discharge**

$$Q_{actual} = \dfrac{V_{actual}}{t} = \dfrac{0.072}{60} = 1.2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$Q_{actual} = 1.2 \text{ L/s} = 4.32 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr}$$

**Step 6: Compute the indicated discharge**

$$Q_{indicated} = \dfrac{V_{indicated}}{t} = \dfrac{0.178}{60}$$

$$Q_{indicated} = 2.967 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 7: Compute the Calibration Factor K**

$$K = \dfrac{V_{actual}}{V_{indicated}} = \dfrac{0.072}{0.178}$$

$$K = 0.4045$$

**Step 8: Compute the Correction Factor C**

$$C = \dfrac{1}{K} = \dfrac{1}{0.4045} = 2.472$$

**Step 9: Compute the Percentage Error**

$$\% E = \dfrac{Q_{indicated} - Q_{actual}}{Q_{actual}} \times 100$$

$$\% E = \dfrac{(2.967 \times 10^{-3}) - (1.2 \times 10^{-3})}{1.2 \times 10^{-3}} \times 100$$

$$\% E = \dfrac{1.767 \times 10^{-3}}{1.2 \times 10^{-3}} \times 100 = +147.25\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> The above numerical is intentionally exaggerated to demonstrate the **calculation methodology**. In a real KTU lab, a healthy meter should have **% E within ±5%** and **K between 0.95 and 1.05**.

**Step 10: Compute the Reynolds Number (for flow regime identification)**

$$Re = \dfrac{4 Q_{actual}}{\pi D \nu}$$

$$Re = \dfrac{4 \times 1.2 \times 10^{-3}}{\pi \times 0.05 \times 0.89 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$Re = \dfrac{4.8 \times 10^{-3}}{1.398 \times 10^{-7}} = 34{,}335$$

Since $Re > 4000$, the flow is **turbulent**.

## 3.2 Multi-Trial Calibration Matrix (Typical Tabular Format)

The following table represents the **Observation and Calculation Sheet** that students must record during the lab. (Numbers below are illustrative.)

| Trial No. | $V_1$ (m³) | $V_2$ (m³) | $t$ (s) | $h_1$ (m) | $h_2$ (m) | $V_{actual}$ (m³) | $V_{indicated}$ (m³) | $Q_{actual}$ (×10⁻³ m³/s) | $Q_{indicated}$ (×10⁻³ m³/s) | $K$ | % E |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | 0.000 | 0.042 | 60 | 0.10 | 0.142 | 0.042 | 0.042 | 0.700 | 0.700 | 1.000 | 0.00 |
| 2 | 0.042 | 0.115 | 60 | 0.10 | 0.170 | 0.070 | 0.073 | 1.167 | 1.217 | 0.959 | +4.30 |
| 3 | 0.115 | 0.225 | 60 | 0.10 | 0.207 | 0.107 | 0.110 | 1.783 | 1.833 | 0.973 | +2.80 |
| 4 | 0.225 | 0.360 | 60 | 0.10 | 0.250 | 0.150 | 0.135 | 2.500 | 2.250 | 1.111 | -10.00 |
| 5 | 0.360 | 0.520 | 60 | 0.10 | 0.300 | 0.200 | 0.160 | 3.333 | 2.667 | 1.250 | -20.00 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pattern Recognition for KTU Theory Questions:**
> In a well-designed positive displacement meter, the **calibration factor K approaches 1.0 at mid-range flow rates** (Trial 2 and 3 above). At very low flows, mechanical friction causes under-reading ($K < 1$). At very high flows, leakage past the piston causes over-reading ($K > 1$). This non-linearity is plotted as the calibration curve.

## 3.3 Derivation of the Linear Calibration Equation

The calibration curve is often fitted with a straight line using the **Least Squares Method**:

$$Q_{actual} = m \cdot Q_{indicated} + c$$

where the slope $m$ and intercept $c$ are:

$$m = \dfrac{n \sum Q_i I_i - (\sum Q_i)(\sum I_i)}{n \sum I_i^2 - (\sum I_i)^2}$$

$$c = \bar{Q} - m \bar{I}$$

Here, $Q_i = Q_{actual}$ and $I_i = Q_{indicated}$. This is the *standard linear regression* form and is asked in KTU Part B questions for full 14 marks when combined with graphical plotting instructions.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied

- All node IDs are purely **alphanumeric with letter prefix** (e.g., `tank1`, `pumpA`).
- No reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style`) used as standalone IDs.
- All node labels contain **raw uppercase alphanumeric text** only — no markdown formatting.

## 4.2 Block Diagram of the Experimental Setup

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sump1[SUMP TANK] --> pumpA[PUMP]
    pumpA --> valve1[FLOW CONTROL VALVE]
    valve1 --> meter1[WATER METER UNDER TEST]
    meter1 --> outlet1[DELIVERY PIPE]
    outlet1 --> tank1[CALIBRATED COLLECTING TANK]
    tank1 --> gauge1[HOOK GAUGE / SIGHT GLASS]
    tank1 --> drain1[DRAIN VALVE]
    tank1 --> over1[OVERFLOW PIPE]
    sump1 -. return line .-> drain1
```

## 4.3 Step-by-Step Procedure Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([START EXPERIMENT]) --> step1[STEP 1: PRIME THE PUMP AND PURGE AIR FROM PIPES]
    step1 --> step2[STEP 2: SET CONTROL VALVE TO DESIRED POSITION]
    step2 --> step3[STEP 3: NOTE INITIAL METER READING V1]
    step3 --> step4[STEP 4: NOTE INITIAL TANK WATER LEVEL H1]
    step4 --> step5[STEP 5: START STOPWATCH AND OPEN COLLECTION VALVE SIMULTANEOUSLY]
    step5 --> step6[STEP 6: COLLECT WATER FOR FIXED TIME T]
    step6 --> step7[STEP 7: STOP STOPWATCH AND CLOSE COLLECTION VALVE]
    step7 --> step8[STEP 8: RECORD FINAL METER READING V2]
    step8 --> step9[STEP 9: RECORD FINAL TANK WATER LEVEL H2]
    step9 --> step10[STEP 10: COMPUTE Q ACTUAL AND Q INDICATED]
    step10 --> step11[STEP 11: COMPUTE K FACTOR AND PERCENTAGE ERROR]
    step11 --> step12[STEP 12: REPEAT FOR 5 TO 6 FLOW RATES]
    step12 --> step13[STEP 13: PLOT Q ACTUAL VS Q INDICATED CALIBRATION CURVE]
    step13 --> stopA([END EXPERIMENT AND DRAIN TANK])
```

## 4.4 Graphical Plot Specifications (Calibration Curve)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Graph Plotting Standard:**
> - **X-axis**: $Q_{indicated}$ in L/s (or m³/s)
> - **Y-axis**: $Q_{actual}$ in L/s (or m³/s)
> - **Plot**: Scatter points + best-fit straight line passing through origin ideally
> - **Ideal Curve**: A 45° line representing $Q_{actual} = Q_{indicated}$ (i.e., $K = 1.0$)
> - **Error Bands**: ±5% envelope drawn to indicate the acceptable tolerance zone per ISO 4064 Class B

## 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputA[REFERENCE STANDARD: VOLUMETRIC TANK] --> compA[COMPARISON UNIT: K FACTOR COMPUTATION]
    inputB[DUT: WATER METER INDICATION] --> compA
    compA --> outputA[CALIBRATION CURVE AND ERROR TABLE]
    outputA --> outputB[CONFIDENCE INTERVAL AND RSD]
    outputB --> finalA[ACCEPT OR REJECT METER AS PER ISO 4064]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **Define the term "Calibration Factor" for a water meter. What does it indicate if K > 1 or K < 1?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The Calibration Factor (K) of a water meter is the dimensionless ratio of the **actual volume of water** passed through the meter to the **volume indicated by the meter**, i.e., $K = V_{actual} / V_{indicated}$. (1 mark)

- If **K > 1**, it means the meter is **under-reading** (it indicates less volume than what actually passed). The indicated value must be multiplied by K to get the true value. (1 mark)
- If **K < 1**, the meter is **over-reading** (it indicates more volume than what actually passed). (1 mark)

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> **List any three sources of error in the calibration of a water meter using the volumetric tank method.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
1. **Parallax error** while reading the hook gauge or sight glass for water level measurement in the collecting tank. (1 mark)
2. **Time measurement error** due to human reaction time (typically ±0.2 s) when starting/stopping the stopwatch. (1 mark)
3. **Air entrainment** in the delivery pipe, which causes the tank to receive less water than what the meter recorded (since the meter has already accounted for the air-filled volume). (1 mark)
*(Other acceptable answers: leaks in pipe joints, meter wear and tear, water temperature variation affecting density, residual water sticking to tank walls, non-uniform cross-section of tank.)*

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

> **(a)** Describe, with a neat sketch, the experimental setup and procedure to **calibrate a water meter** using the volumetric tank method. State the formulas used. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** During the calibration of a water meter, the following observations were recorded. The collecting tank has a cross-section of **0.75 m × 0.75 m**. Calculate the calibration factor K and percentage error for each trial, and plot the calibration curve. **(7 marks)**

| Trial | $V_1$ (m³) | $V_2$ (m³) | $t$ (s) | $h_1$ (m) | $h_2$ (m) |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | 0.000 | 0.060 | 60 | 0.10 | 0.205 |
| 2 | 0.060 | 0.135 | 60 | 0.10 | 0.225 |
| 3 | 0.135 | 0.230 | 60 | 0.10 | 0.260 |
| 4 | 0.230 | 0.345 | 60 | 0.10 | 0.300 |
| 5 | 0.345 | 0.480 | 60 | 0.10 | 0.350 |

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Setup Description (3 marks):** The setup consists of (i) a sump tank, (ii) a centrifugal pump, (iii) a flow control valve, (iv) the water meter under test installed in a horizontal pipe, (v) a calibrated collecting tank with a hook gauge or sight glass for level measurement, (vi) a stopwatch, and (vii) connecting pipes with a return line to the sump.

**Procedure (3 marks):**
1. Prime the pump, ensure no air locks in the system, and open the flow control valve to a pre-set position.
2. Note the initial meter reading $V_1$ and the initial water level $h_1$ in the collecting tank.
3. Simultaneously start the stopwatch and divert water into the collecting tank.
4. After a fixed time $t$ (≈ 60 s), stop the watch and record $V_2$ and $h_2$.
5. Compute $V_{actual} = A_{tank} \cdot (h_2 - h_1)$ and $V_{indicated} = V_2 - V_1$.
6. Compute $Q_{actual} = V_{actual}/t$, $Q_{indicated} = V_{indicated}/t$, $K = V_{actual}/V_{indicated}$, and $\% E$.
7. Repeat for 5–6 flow rates by varying the valve and plot the calibration curve $Q_{actual}$ vs $Q_{indicated}$.

**Formulas (1 mark):** List $V_{actual} = A \cdot h$, $K = V_{actual}/V_{indicated}$, $\% E = (Q_{indicated} - Q_{actual})/Q_{actual} \times 100$.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:** $A_{tank} = 0.75 \times 0.75 = 0.5625$ m², $t = 60$ s

**Trial 1:**
$$V_{indicated} = 0.060 - 0.000 = 0.060 \text{ m}^3$$
$$h = 0.205 - 0.100 = 0.105 \text{ m}$$
$$V_{actual} = 0.5625 \times 0.105 = 0.0591 \text{ m}^3$$
$$K = 0.0591 / 0.060 = 0.985, \quad \% E = -1.69\%$$

**Trial 2:**
$$V_{indicated} = 0.135 - 0.060 = 0.075 \text{ m}^3$$
$$h = 0.225 - 0.100 = 0.125 \text{ m}$$
$$V_{actual} = 0.5625 \times 0.125 = 0.0703 \text{ m}^3$$
$$K = 0.0703 / 0.075 = 0.937, \quad \% E = -6.72\%$$

**Trial 3:**
$$V_{indicated} = 0.230 - 0.135 = 0.095 \text{ m}^3$$
$$h = 0.260 - 0.100 = 0.160 \text{ m}$$
$$V_{actual} = 0.5625 \times 0.160 = 0.0900 \text{ m}^3$$
$$K = 0.0900 / 0.095 = 0.947, \quad \% E = -5.55\%$$

**Trial 4:**
$$V_{indicated} = 0.345 - 0.230 = 0.115 \text{ m}^3$$
$$h = 0.300 - 0.100 = 0.200 \text{ m}$$
$$V_{actual} = 0.5625 \times 0.200 = 0.1125 \text{ m}^3$$
$$K = 0.1125 / 0.115 = 0.978, \quad \% E = -2.17\%$$

**Trial 5:**
$$V_{indicated} = 0.480 - 0.345 = 0.135 \text{ m}^3$$
$$h = 0.350 - 0.100 = 0.250 \text{ m}$$
$$V_{actual} = 0.5625 \times 0.250 = 0.1406 \text{ m}^3$$
$$K = 0.1406 / 0.135 = 1.042, \quad \% E = +3.97\%$$

**Calibration Curve (final 1 mark for plot):** Plot $Q_{actual}$ on Y-axis vs $Q_{indicated}$ on X-axis. The points should lie close to the ideal 45° line ($K = 1$), confirming the meter is accurate within ISO 4064 Class B limits.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

> **(a)** Explain the **classification of water meters** based on the operating principle. Compare the **positive displacement** and **velocity-type** meters with respect to accuracy, flow range, pressure drop, and typical applications. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A water meter is tested under steady flow. The actual volume collected in 90 s is **0.135 m³** while the meter indicates **0.140 m³**. If the pipe diameter is **80 mm** and water temperature is 25 °C, calculate: (i) the calibration factor, (ii) the percentage error, (iii) the correction factor, and (iv) the Reynolds number. State whether the meter is acceptable as per ISO 4064 Class B (±5% limit). **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Classification (4 marks):**
Water meters are broadly classified into four types:

1. **Positive Displacement (PD) Meters** — Use a nutating disc, oscillating piston, or rotating lobe to mechanically displace fixed volumes of water. They **count the number of displacements** and multiply by the known chamber volume.
2. **Velocity Meters** — Use the kinetic energy of flow to spin a turbine or propeller; rotational speed is proportional to flow velocity.
3. **Electromagnetic Meters** — Use Faraday's Law of electromagnetic induction; suitable for conductive fluids.
4. **Ultrasonic Meters** — Measure the difference in transit time of sound waves travelling with and against the flow.

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Positive Displacement | Velocity Type |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Accuracy | Very High (±0.5% to ±2%) | Moderate (±2% to ±5%) |
| Flow Range | Low to Medium (best at low flows) | Medium to High |
| Pressure Drop | Higher (due to mechanical resistance) | Lower (open flow path) |
| Typical Application | Domestic water billing, small industries | Large pipelines, bulk supply, irrigation |
| Sensitivity to particulates | Low (sealed chamber) | High (turbine damage) |
| Cost | Higher | Lower for large sizes |

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:** $V_{actual} = 0.135$ m³, $V_{indicated} = 0.140$ m³, $t = 90$ s, $D = 0.080$ m, $\nu = 0.89 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s

**(i) Calibration Factor K (2 marks):**

$$K = \dfrac{V_{actual}}{V_{indicated}} = \dfrac{0.135}{0.140} = 0.9643$$

**(ii) Percentage Error (2 marks):**

$$\% E = \dfrac{Q_{indicated} - Q_{actual}}{Q_{actual}} \times 100$$

$$Q_{actual} = \dfrac{0.135}{90} = 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$
$$Q_{indicated} = \dfrac{0.140}{90} = 1.556 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$
$$\% E = \dfrac{(1.556 - 1.5) \times 10^{-3}}{1.5 \times 10^{-3}} \times 100 = +3.70\%$$

**(iii) Correction Factor C (1 mark):**

$$C = \dfrac{1}{K} = \dfrac{1}{0.9643} = 1.0370$$

**(iv) Reynolds Number (1 mark):**

$$Re = \dfrac{4Q_{actual}}{\pi D \nu} = \dfrac{4 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-3}}{\pi \times 0.080 \times 0.89 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$Re = \dfrac{6 \times 10^{-3}}{2.237 \times 10^{-7}} = 26{,}821$$

**(v) Acceptability (1 mark):**
Since $|\% E| = 3.70\% < 5\%$, the meter is **ACCEPTABLE** as per ISO 4064 Class B tolerance limits.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost 2–3 Marks in KTU ESE:**
> 1. **Confusing K and C** — Writing $K = V_{indicated} / V_{actual}$ instead of the correct $K = V_{actual} / V_{indicated}$. Always remember: K = **A**ctual / **I**ndicated.
> 2. **Forgetting units** in the final answer (especially m³/s vs L/s). KTU evaluators deduct 0.5 marks per missing unit.
> 3. **Sign of percentage error** — Students often write $\% E$ as a positive value, losing the distinction between over-reading and under-reading. Always include the **sign** (+ for over-reading, − for under-reading).
> 4. **No sketch in the procedure question** — For 7-mark setup questions, a neat labelled block diagram is **mandatory**. Missing the sketch costs 2 marks minimum.
> 5. **Calibration curve not drawn or drawn without axes labels** — Always label both axes with **names + units** and put a **title** ("Calibration Curve of Water Meter").
> 6. **Skipping the Reynolds number calculation** — Examiners frequently award an "extra" 1 mark for showing flow regime identification, even if not asked.
> 7. **Not mentioning the source of error** — At least 2–3 sources of error must be listed for a complete answer (parallax, reaction time, air entrainment, leaks, etc.).

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Calibration Factor (K)** = Actual Volume / Indicated Volume; **Correction Factor (C)** = 1/K.
- **K > 1** means **under-reading** (meter shows less than actual). **K < 1** means **over-reading**.
- **% Error** = (Indicated − Actual) / Actual × 100; sign is **positive for over-reading**.
- **Volume in tank** $V_{actual} = A_{tank} \times h$ (rise in water level).
- **Discharge** $Q = V / t$ in **m³/s** (or L/s = × 1000).
- The **reference standard** in this experiment is the **calibrated collecting tank + stopwatch** (volumetric method).
- **At least 5–6 flow rates** must be tested to plot a meaningful calibration curve.
- **Calibration curve** is plotted as $Q_{actual}$ (Y-axis) vs $Q_{indicated}$ (X-axis); ideal line is at 45°.
- **ISO 4064 Class B tolerance** = **±5%** for domestic meters; **Class C** = ±2%; **Class D** = ±1% (industrial).
- **Reynolds number** $Re = 4Q / (\pi D \nu)$; for $D = 50$ mm and $Q = 1$ L/s, $Re \approx 28{,}000$ (turbulent).
- **Common errors**: parallax in level reading, stopwatch reaction time (±0.2 s), air entrainment, pipe leaks, meter wear.
- **Acceptance criteria**: meter is accepted if $|\% E| \leq 5\%$ across the entire flow range.
- **Least squares fitting** of calibration curve gives the best linear estimate: $Q_{actual} = m \cdot Q_{indicated} + c$.
- The **correction factor C** is what you apply to every meter reading in the field: $V_{true} = C \times V_{meter}$.
- The collecting tank must be **pre-calibrated** (cross-sectional area known to ±0.1%) for traceability to national standards (NABL, India).
- Always **purge air** from the system before starting the experiment to avoid volumetric errors.
- The **stopwatch** and the **collection valve** must be operated **simultaneously** by the same observer (or use a two-person team with a clear whistle/signal).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Calibration of Rectangular Notch
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCEL308 - FLUID MECHANICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Calibration of Rectangular Notch

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Rectangular Notch:** A rectangular notch is a sharp-edged, rectangular opening cut through the vertical side wall of a tank or channel, used as a **primary flow-measuring device** in open-channel hydraulics. The depth of water flowing over the sill of the notch is termed the **head (H)**, and the discharge passing over it is computed using a derived theoretical equation, which is then related to actual discharge through the **Coefficient of Discharge (Cd)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Calibration:** Calibration is the **experimental process** of establishing the relationship between the **head of water (H)** over the notch and the **actual discharge (Q_act)** flowing through it, by comparing the measured (actual) discharge with the theoretical discharge predicted by the orifice / Torricelli equation. The ratio of these two yields the **Coefficient of Discharge (Cd)**, which is a **dimensionless constant** unique to the geometry of the notch.

### Standard KTU Notation (Used in the 2024 Lab Manual)
| Symbol | Quantity | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|
| L | Length of the notch (sill) | **m** |
| H | Head of water above the sill | **m** |
| g | Acceleration due to gravity | **9.81 m/s²** |
| Q_th | Theoretical discharge | **m³/s** |
| Q_act | Actual (collected) discharge | **m³/s** |
| Cd | Coefficient of discharge | **dimensionless** |
| A | Area of collecting tank | **m²** |
| R | Rise of water in collecting tank | **m** |
| t | Time of collection | **s** |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Bathroom Faucet" Picture:**
> 
> Imagine a rectangular slot cut into a **transparent water tank** on the side. As you pour water in, it spills out through the slot. The **higher** the water level inside the tank, the **faster** the water shoots out (a consequence of Torricelli's theorem, v = √(2gh)). The **wider** the slot, the **more** water passes through per second.
> 
> The *theoretical* amount of water that should flow out is easy to calculate using basic physics (the water at depth h falls with velocity √(2gh), so we integrate across the slot from top to bottom).
> 
> But in **reality**, the water leaving the slot *shrinks* slightly into a band narrower than the slot — this is called the **vena contracta**. Air friction, surface tension, and the viscosity of water also slow the jet down a little. So the **actual water that comes out** is less than what the formula predicts. 
> 
> The **Coefficient of Discharge (Cd)** is simply the **"efficiency factor"** that corrects the theoretical number to match the real one. **Calibration** is the act of *finding* that factor by performing the experiment and weighing the actual water collected.

### Key Physical Constants Used
- Acceleration due to gravity: **g = 9.81 m/s²**
- Standard water density (assumed): **ρ = 1000 kg/m³**
- Typical Cd range for a rectangular notch: **0.61 to 0.66** (with end contractions)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Step-by-Step Logic

The discharge equation for a rectangular notch is derived by applying **Torricelli's Theorem** to an elemental horizontal strip of water flowing through the notch.

> [!NOTE]
> **Torricelli's Theorem (KTU Expected Statement):**
> *"The theoretical velocity of a liquid discharging through a small orifice under a head h is equal to the velocity of a body falling freely under the action of gravity through a vertical distance equal to that head."*
> 
> $$v = \sqrt{2gh}$$

### Structured Derivation Logic (For Your Answer Sheet)
1. **Establish the geometry:** A rectangular notch of width **L** is fitted in the side of a tank, with water at head **H** above the sill.
2. **Slice the nappe into elemental strips:** Consider a thin horizontal strip of water at depth **h** (measured from the free surface) and thickness **dh**.
3. **Compute the elemental area:** dA = L × dh
4. **Compute the elemental velocity:** v = √(2gh) [by Torricelli's theorem]
5. **Compute the elemental discharge:** dQ = v × dA = L × √(2gh) × dh
6. **Integrate across the full head (0 to H):** Q = ∫ dQ to get total theoretical discharge.
7. **Apply a correction factor (Cd):** Since real flow contracts and loses energy, multiply by **Cd**.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q_{th} = \dfrac{2}{3} \cdot L \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot H^{3/2}$ | Theoretical discharge | m³/s |
| 2 | $Q_{act} = \dfrac{A \cdot R}{t}$ | Actual (collected) discharge | m³/s |
| 3 | $C_d = \dfrac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}}$ | Coefficient of discharge | dimensionless |
| 4 | $v = \sqrt{2gh}$ | Torricelli velocity at depth h | m/s |
| 5 | $Q_{act} = C_d \cdot \dfrac{2}{3} \cdot L \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot H^{3/2}$ | Final discharge equation | m³/s |
| 6 | $H = h - h_s$ | Effective head (where hₛ is sill height) | m |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KtU Mark-Winning Tip:** When asked "Why is Cd < 1?", the examiner expects **three explicit reasons**:
> 1. **Vena Contracta:** The nappe contracts after leaving the notch, reducing the effective cross-section.
> 2. **Friction & Viscosity:** Water molecules experience viscous resistance near the notch edges.
> 3. **Surface Tension:** At small heads, surface tension slightly alters the nappe geometry.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Use of Rectangular Notch |
|---|---|
| **Irrigation Channels** | Measures flow in agricultural distributaries and field channels. |
| **Sewage Treatment Plants** | Monitors flow rate in open treatment channels. |
| **Hydraulic Laboratories** | Acts as a secondary standard for calibrating other flow meters. |
| **Industrial Effluent Channels** | Measures flow of treated wastewater before discharge. |
| **Spillways & Weirs** | Suppression weirs are essentially wide rectangular notches. |

> [!TIP]
> **Why "Calibrate"?** Once Cd is determined, the calibrated curve (H vs Q_act) becomes a **permanent measuring tool** for that particular notch. Operators in the field only need to read the head H from a gauge and look up the discharge directly from the chart — no further computation required.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Theoretical Discharge Over a Rectangular Notch

> [!NOTE]
> **Assumptions made in the derivation (write these verbatim in the exam):**
> 1. The velocity of approach of water in the tank is negligible.
> 2. The nappe is fully ventilated (atmospheric pressure below the nappe).
> 3. The notch is sharp-edged and the upstream face is vertical.
> 4. The coefficient of velocity (Cv) is assumed = 1 (ideal flow) for theoretical discharge.

### Step 1 — Set up the geometry

Consider a rectangular notch of length **L** fitted in the vertical side of a tank. Let **H** be the head of water above the sill of the notch.

### Step 2 — Select an elemental strip

Consider a thin horizontal elemental strip of water at a depth **h** below the free surface of the water, and of thickness **dh**.

### Step 3 — Area of the elemental strip

The strip is a rectangle of length **L** and thickness **dh**. Therefore, the area of the strip is:

$$dA = L \cdot dh$$

### Step 4 — Velocity of water through the strip

By **Torricelli's Theorem**, the theoretical velocity of water through the strip is:

$$v = \sqrt{2gh}$$

### Step 5 — Discharge through the elemental strip

The elemental discharge dQ is the product of the velocity and the area of the strip:

$$dQ = v \cdot dA$$

Substituting the expressions from Steps 3 and 4:

$$dQ = L \cdot \sqrt{2gh} \cdot dh$$

### Step 6 — Total theoretical discharge by integration

The total theoretical discharge Q_th is obtained by integrating the elemental discharge dQ from the top of the notch (h = 0) to the bottom (h = H):

$$Q_{th} = \int_{0}^{H} L \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot h^{1/2} \, dh$$

Factor out the constants L and √(2g):

$$Q_{th} = L \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot \int_{0}^{H} h^{1/2} \, dh$$

### Step 7 — Evaluate the integral

Apply the power rule of integration, $\int h^n \, dh = \dfrac{h^{n+1}}{n+1}$:

$$Q_{th} = L \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot \left[ \frac{h^{3/2}}{3/2} \right]_{0}^{H}$$

$$Q_{th} = L \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot \frac{2}{3} \cdot H^{3/2}$$

Rearranging for the standard textbook form:

$$Q_{th} = \frac{2}{3} \cdot L \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot H^{3/2}$$

### Step 8 — Apply the coefficient of discharge

Because the actual flow is less than the theoretical (due to contraction, friction, and surface tension), we introduce the **coefficient of discharge (Cd)**:

$$Q_{act} = C_d \cdot \frac{2}{3} \cdot L \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot H^{3/2}$$

This is the **final, KTU-board-approved discharge equation** for a rectangular notch.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Variable Influence on Discharge

| Variable | Symbol | Effect on Q_act if Increased | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| Length of notch | L | Q increases **linearly** | Wider opening = more cross-section |
| Head over notch | H | Q increases as **H^(3/2)** | Higher pressure head = faster flow (Torricelli) |
| Gravity | g | Q increases as **√g** | Affects velocity of jets |
| Coefficient Cd | Cd | Q increases **linearly** | Higher efficiency of discharge |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield:** The **H^(3/2)** dependence is the reason the calibration curve of H vs Q is *non-linear* (parabolic). Small errors in measuring H lead to **larger errors in Q** (≈ 1.5 × error in H).

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical (For KTU Practice)

**Problem:** A rectangular notch of length **0.5 m** is used to measure the discharge of water. The head over the notch is **100 mm**. In a duration of **30 seconds**, the volume of water collected in the measuring tank is found to be **0.42 m³**. Determine the **coefficient of discharge (Cd)** of the notch.

**Given:**
- L = 0.5 m
- H = 100 mm = 0.1 m
- t = 30 s
- Volume collected, V = 0.42 m³
- g = 9.81 m/s²

**Step 1 — Calculate the actual discharge Q_act:**

$$Q_{act} = \frac{V}{t} = \frac{0.42}{30} = 0.014 \, m^3/s$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the theoretical discharge Q_th:**

$$Q_{th} = \frac{2}{3} \cdot L \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot H^{3/2}$$

Substituting the values:

$$Q_{th} = \frac{2}{3} \cdot 0.5 \cdot \sqrt{2 \times 9.81} \cdot (0.1)^{3/2}$$

Compute each part:

$$\sqrt{2 \times 9.81} = \sqrt{19.62} = 4.4294 \, m^{1/2}/s$$

$$(0.1)^{3/2} = (0.1)^{1.5} = 0.03162 \, m^{3/2}$$

$$Q_{th} = \frac{2}{3} \cdot 0.5 \cdot 4.4294 \cdot 0.03162$$

$$Q_{th} = 0.6667 \times 0.5 \times 4.4294 \times 0.03162$$

$$Q_{th} = 0.04670 \, m^3/s$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the coefficient of discharge:**

$$C_d = \frac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} = \frac{0.014}{0.04670} = 0.2998 \approx 0.30$$

> [!WARNING]
> **This is unrealistically low.** A real rectangular notch has Cd ≈ 0.61–0.66. The low value here is a *deliberate trick* in the problem — but it actually shows the **importance of proper calibration**: if the notch is poorly installed, nappe ventilation is missing, or H is misread, Cd will fall outside the expected range, signalling a faulty setup.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram of the Experimental Setup

> [!NOTE]
> **Apparatus Layout:** The complete KTU bench setup consists of an **overhead tank → pump → supply tank → stilling chamber → notch tank with rectangular notch → collecting/measuring tank → sump**, with a **pointer gauge** to read the head H and a **stopwatch** to time the water collection.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Sump Tank] --> A2[Centrifugal Pump]
    A2 --> A3[Overhead Supply Tank]
    A3 --> A4[Stilling Chamber with Baffle Plates]
    A4 --> A5[Notch Tank with Rectangular Notch of Length L]
    A5 --> A6[Collecting Measuring Tank]
    A5 -.Head H measured at.-> A7[Pointer Gauge or Vernier Gauge]
    A6 -.Volume V collected.-> A8[Measuring Cylinder Scale]
    A6 --> A9[Sump Tank Return]
    A8 -.Time t noted.-> A10[Digital Stopwatch]
    A5 --> A11[Discharge Nappe to Atmosphere]
```

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram of Calibration Logic (H → Q → Cd)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B1[Set up apparatus with rectangular notch] --> B2[Adjust flow control valve for steady flow]
    B2 --> B3[Measure head H using pointer gauge]
    B3 --> B4[Collect water in measuring tank for time t]
    B4 --> B5[Measure rise R of water in measuring tank]
    B5 --> B6[Compute Q_act equals A times R divided by t]
    B3 --> B7[Compute Q_th equals two thirds L sqrt 2g H to the 3 by 2]
    B6 --> B8[Compute Cd equals Q_act divided by Q_th]
    B7 --> B8
    B8 --> B9[Repeat for 5 to 6 different heads]
    B9 --> B10[Plot calibration curve: H on x axis, Q_act on y axis]
    B10 --> B11[Optional: Plot Cd versus H for validation]
```

---

## 4.3 Schematic Cross-Section of the Notch and Nappe

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    C1[Free Water Surface inside Tank] --> C2[Head H above notch sill]
    C2 --> C3[Rectangular Notch Opening of Length L]
    C3 --> C4[Contracted Nappe vena contracta]
    C4 --> C5[Discharged Water to Collecting Tank]
    C1 -.h variable.-> C6[Elemental Strip at depth h thickness dh]
    C6 --> C3
```

---

## 4.4 Calibration Curve — Expected Shape (KTU Mandatory Plot)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Graph Type for KTU Record:** 
> - **X-axis:** Head **H** (m) — typically ranges from **0.02 m to 0.12 m**.
> - **Y-axis:** Actual Discharge **Q_act** (m³/s × 10⁻³, i.e., L/s).
> - **Curve Shape:** A **smooth parabola** passing through the origin (since Q = 0 when H = 0).
> - **Auxiliary Plot:** A second graph of **H vs Cd** should be a **near-horizontal line** (with slight upward or downward trend) showing that Cd is approximately constant for a well-calibrated notch.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    D1[H equal 0.02 m] --> D2[Q_act equal 0.30 L per s]
    D3[H equal 0.04 m] --> D4[Q_act equal 0.85 L per s]
    D5[H equal 0.06 m] --> D6[Q_act equal 1.55 L per s]
    D7[H equal 0.08 m] --> D8[Q_act equal 2.40 L per s]
    D9[H equal 0.10 m] --> D10[Q_act equal 3.35 L per s]
    D11[H equal 0.12 m] --> D12[Q_act equal 4.40 L per s]
    D1 --> D13[Calibration Curve: Smooth parabola through origin]
    D3 --> D13
    D5 --> D13
    D7 --> D13
    D9 --> D13
    D11 --> D13
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** Define a rectangular notch. State the formula for discharge over a rectangular notch. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
A **rectangular notch** is a sharp-edged rectangular opening made on the vertical side wall of a tank, used to measure the discharge of water flowing through it. The length of the notch is denoted by **L**, and the head of water above the sill of the notch is denoted by **H**.

The formula for discharge over a rectangular notch is:

$$Q = C_d \cdot \frac{2}{3} \cdot L \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot H^{3/2}$$

where **g = 9.81 m/s²** and **Cd** is the coefficient of discharge (typically 0.61 to 0.66 for a rectangular notch with end contractions).

---

### **Q2.** What is meant by the calibration of a notch? Why is it necessary? `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
**Calibration** of a notch is the experimental procedure of determining the **coefficient of discharge (Cd)** of the notch by comparing the **actual discharge** (measured by collecting water over a known time) with the **theoretical discharge** (calculated from the formula). 

It is **necessary** because:
1. The theoretical formula assumes ideal flow, but real flow suffers from **vena contracta**, friction, and viscosity losses.
2. The value of Cd cannot be predicted by theory alone — it must be found **experimentally** for the specific notch geometry.
3. The calibration curve (H vs Q_act) obtained is then used as a **permanent reference** for measuring flow in the field by simply reading the head H.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Question (Internal Choice Format)

> **INSTRUCTION:** Answer **ANY ONE** full question. Each question carries **14 marks**, split into two sub-parts of **7 marks each**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for the discharge over a rectangular notch, clearly stating the assumptions made. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe, with a neat sketch, the experimental procedure to determine the **coefficient of discharge (Cd)** of a rectangular notch. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Assumptions (1 mark):**
1. Velocity of approach of water in the tank is negligible.
2. The nappe is fully ventilated (atmospheric pressure acts below the nappe).
3. The notch is sharp-edged and the upstream face is vertical.
4. Coefficient of velocity Cv = 1 for the theoretical derivation.

**Derivation Steps (6 marks — write each step clearly):**

1. Consider a rectangular notch of length **L** fitted on the side of a tank. Let **H** be the head of water above the sill.
2. Consider a thin horizontal elemental strip at depth **h** from the free surface, of thickness **dh**.
3. Area of strip: dA = L × dh.
4. Velocity of water through the strip by Torricelli's theorem: v = √(2gh).
5. Discharge through the strip: dQ = v × dA = L × √(2gh) × dh.
6. Total theoretical discharge: integrate from 0 to H:

$$Q_{th} = L \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot \int_{0}^{H} h^{1/2} \, dh = \frac{2}{3} \cdot L \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot H^{3/2}$$

7. Actual discharge: Q_act = Cd × Q_th.

$$Q_{act} = C_d \cdot \frac{2}{3} \cdot L \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot H^{3/2}$$

#### **Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Apparatus Used (2 marks):** Hydraulic bench, rectangular notch apparatus, collecting tank, pointer gauge, stopwatch, measuring cylinder.

**Procedure (4 marks):**
1. Set up the rectangular notch apparatus on the hydraulic bench and ensure the nappe is **fully ventilated** (air can flow below the nappe).
2. Start the pump and adjust the flow control valve to obtain a **steady head** H over the notch.
3. Measure the head **H** using the pointer gauge, ensuring the tip just touches the water surface.
4. Direct the nappe into the collecting/measuring tank. Simultaneously start the stopwatch and collect water for a known time **t**.
5. Measure the rise **R** of water in the collecting tank using its scale, and note the cross-sectional area **A** of the tank.
6. Calculate the actual discharge: Q_act = (A × R) / t.
7. Calculate the theoretical discharge: Q_th = (2/3) × L × √(2g) × H^(3/2).
8. Calculate Cd = Q_act / Q_th.
9. **Repeat** the experiment for **5–6 different heads** and tabulate the results.
10. Plot the **calibration curve** of H (x-axis) vs Q_act (y-axis) — a smooth parabolic curve.

**Precautions (1 mark):** Steady flow, full nappe ventilation, accurate head measurement, no leakage from collecting tank.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of **calibration of a rectangular notch** with a neat sketch. State the empirical formula obtained. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A rectangular notch of length **0.8 m** is fitted in a tank. When the head of water over the notch is **150 mm**, the volume of water collected in **40 seconds** is **0.85 m³**. Determine: **(i)** the actual discharge, **(ii)** the theoretical discharge, and **(iii)** the coefficient of discharge Cd. Take g = 9.81 m/s². **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Concept (3 marks):**
Calibration is the experimental determination of the relationship between the **head H** of water over the notch and the **actual discharge Q_act** flowing through it. The procedure involves collecting the discharged water over a known time and computing Q_act = (A × R) / t. The theoretical discharge is computed using the formula Q_th = (2/3) × L × √(2g) × H^(3/2). The ratio of the two gives **Cd**, which is plotted against H to obtain a calibration curve.

**Sketch (2 marks):** Show the tank, notch, head H, nappe (vena contracta), and collecting tank.

**Empirical Formula (2 marks):**

$$Q_{act} = C_d \cdot \frac{2}{3} \cdot L \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot H^{3/2}$$

#### **Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Given:** L = 0.8 m, H = 0.15 m, t = 40 s, V = 0.85 m³, g = 9.81 m/s².

**(i) Actual Discharge (2 marks):**

$$Q_{act} = \frac{V}{t} = \frac{0.85}{40} = 0.02125 \, m^3/s = 21.25 \, L/s$$

**(ii) Theoretical Discharge (3 marks):**

$$Q_{th} = \frac{2}{3} \cdot L \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot H^{3/2}$$

$$Q_{th} = \frac{2}{3} \cdot 0.8 \cdot \sqrt{2 \times 9.81} \cdot (0.15)^{3/2}$$

$$\sqrt{2 \times 9.81} = \sqrt{19.62} = 4.4294$$

$$(0.15)^{3/2} = 0.15 \times \sqrt{0.15} = 0.15 \times 0.3873 = 0.05809$$

$$Q_{th} = 0.6667 \times 0.8 \times 4.4294 \times 0.05809$$

$$Q_{th} = 0.1371 \, m^3/s = 137.1 \, L/s$$

**(iii) Coefficient of Discharge (2 marks):**

$$C_d = \frac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} = \frac{0.02125}{0.1371} = 0.1550$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert mm to m** for H (write 0.15 m, not 150 mm) — costs **1–2 marks**.
> 2. **Forgetting g = 9.81 m/s²** in the formula — silent mistake, examiner will mark zero for the whole derivation.
> 3. **Mixing up units of Q:** Stick to **m³/s** in the formula, then convert to **L/s** at the end if required.
> 4. **Forgetting the (2/3) factor:** This is the **most-frequently-skipped step** in derivations — leads to wrong Cd.
> 5. **Forgetting to mention assumptions** in the derivation: this costs at least **1 mark** in Part (a).
> 6. **Cd < 0.5 is suspicious:** Always check your numerical — it usually indicates a unit error or misread head.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **⚡ KTU Last-Minute Revision Flash-Card — Calibration of Rectangular Notch**

- ✅ **Definition:** Rectangular notch = sharp-edged rectangular opening on a tank side, used for flow measurement.
- ✅ **Standard formula:** Q_act = (2/3) × Cd × L × √(2g) × H^(3/2).
- ✅ **Cd range:** **0.61 – 0.66** for a rectangular notch with end contractions.
- ✅ **g = 9.81 m/s²** — always.
- ✅ **Torricelli's theorem:** v = √(2gh) — the foundation of the derivation.
- ✅ **Actual discharge:** Q_act = A × R / t (from collecting tank).
- ✅ **Theoretical discharge:** Q_th = (2/3) × L × √(2g) × H^(3/2).
- ✅ **Cd = Q_act / Q_th** — dimensionless.
- ✅ **Three reasons for Cd < 1:** Vena contracta, viscosity/friction, surface tension.
- ✅ **Calibration curve:** H (x-axis) vs Q_act (y-axis) — **smooth parabola through the origin**.
- ✅ **Auxiliary plot:** H vs Cd — should be **nearly horizontal** for a well-calibrated notch.
- ✅ **Critical precaution:** **Nappe ventilation** is mandatory (else the nappe clings to the notch wall and Cd drops sharply).
- ✅ **Unit conversions:** mm → m (× 10⁻³), cm → m (× 10⁻²), L → m³ (× 10⁻³).
- ✅ **Head H^(3/2) dependence:** H = 0.1 m gives 10× more discharge than H = 0.01 m, not 10× — be careful with the 1.5 power.
- ✅ **Record the experiment** with: Trial No., H, V, t, Q_act, Q_th, Cd — in a **tabular column**.
- ✅ **Typical number of trials:** 5 to 6 different heads for a good calibration curve.
- ✅ **Most-marked topics in exam:** (1) Derivation of Q_th (full 7 marks possible). (2) Numerical with Cd calculation (routine). (3) Sources of error in Cd.
- ✅ **One-line exam answer for "Why calibrate?":** *"To determine the empirical coefficient of discharge (Cd) and establish the H vs Q_act relationship for accurate flow measurement in the field."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Calibration of Triangular Notch
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCEL308 - FLUID MECHANICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Calibration of Triangular Notch

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Triangular Notch** (also called a **V-Notch** or **V-Notch Weir**) is a measuring device cut in the form of an inverted 'V' shape in a thin, flat metallic plate, which is installed vertically across an open channel or tank. When fluid flows over this sharp-edged plate, the geometry of the notch forces the water to converge into a triangular cross-section. By measuring the **head (H)** — the vertical height of the liquid surface above the apex (vertex) of the V — the theoretical discharge can be computed. A **Calibration** experiment determines the empirical **Coefficient of Discharge (Cd)** for that specific notch by comparing the actual (collected) discharge with the theoretical (calculated) discharge.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCCEL308 - Module 2):**
> The calibration of a triangular notch is a compulsory open-channel flow experiment. The student is expected to:
> (i) Record the head 'H' for a given steady discharge.
> (ii) Compute the theoretical discharge $Q_{th}$ using the standard formula.
> (iii) Compute the actual discharge $Q_{act}$ by the volumetric method (collecting water in a tank for a known time 't').
> (iv) Determine the **Coefficient of Discharge $C_d = Q_{act} / Q_{th}$**.
> (v) Plot the **Calibration Curve: $Q_{act}$ vs $H$** (or $C_d$ vs $H$).

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

Imagine a small dam wall made of sheet metal standing in a stream. If you cut a rectangular slot in it, water flows out as a thick curtain. Now, instead of a rectangle, imagine you cut an **inverted triangle (∇)** into that wall. The water is forced through a narrow 'V' opening at the bottom, and the curtain of water tapers to a thin point.

Why would engineers do this? Because a V-notch is **extremely sensitive to small changes in flow**. Think of a kitchen faucet — when you barely turn it, only a tiny trickle comes out, and the tiniest twist changes the flow a lot. A V-notch behaves exactly like that faucet: it magnifies small flow variations into large head changes, making it perfect for measuring **low discharges** (like trickles, drips, or small laboratory flows) where a rectangular notch would give nearly immeasurable head changes.

### 1.3 Standard Symbols, Constants & Units

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard Unit (SI) |
|--------|----------|--------------------|
| Q | Volumetric Discharge | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ |
| H | Head over the notch apex | m |
| $\theta$ | Angle of the notch (apex angle) | degrees / radians |
| $C_d$ | Coefficient of Discharge | dimensionless |
| g | Acceleration due to gravity | **$9.81 \; \text{m/s}^2$** |
| A | Area of the collecting tank | $\text{m}^2$ |
| R | Rise of water in the collecting tank | m |
| t | Time of collection | s |
| v | Velocity of approach | $\text{m/s}$ |
| h | Height of the notch plate | m |
| b | Width of the channel/tank | m |

> [!NOTE]
> **The Universal Notch Angle in KTU Labs:** Most college laboratories use a notch with apex angle $\theta = \mathbf{90^\circ}$, since this simplifies the formula (since $\tan(45^\circ) = 1$). Always verify the angle marked on the plate before the experiment.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation: The Assumed Flow Mechanism

When a liquid flows over a sharp-edged triangular notch, two key physical assumptions are made to simplify the analysis (Borda's classical approach):

- **Assumption 1 — The velocity of approach is negligible.** The channel leading to the notch is considered large compared to the notch opening, so the water approaching the notch is assumed to be at rest (velocity $\approx 0$). Only the head 'H' causes flow.
- **Assumption 2 — The free surface remains horizontal (no draw-down curvature).** The profile of the discharging nappe (the falling sheet of water) is taken as a series of horizontal elementary strips, each acting as a small **orifice** under the head measured to its centroid.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why a Triangular Notch is preferred for small discharges:**
> The discharge varies as $\mathbf{H^{5/2}}$ for a triangular notch, but only as $\mathbf{H^{3/2}}$ for a rectangular notch. This means for a small change in discharge, the head 'H' changes much more noticeably in a V-notch — giving **higher accuracy at low flows**.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic of the Calibration

The theoretical discharge through a triangular notch is obtained by integrating the elemental flow across the entire triangular opening:

1. Consider an elementary horizontal strip of water at a depth 'h' below the free liquid surface, having a thickness 'dh'.
2. The width of this strip 'b' varies linearly with depth (because the notch sides are straight lines converging at the apex). Using similar triangles, $b = 2(H - h)\tan(\theta/2)$.
3. The area of the strip is $dA = b \cdot dh = 2(H - h)\tan(\theta/2) \, dh$.
4. By **Torricelli's Law**, the velocity of the strip is $v = \sqrt{2gh}$ (where 'h' is the head above the strip's centroid, equal to its depth from the surface).
5. The elementary discharge is $dQ = C_d \cdot dA \cdot v = C_d \cdot 2(H - h)\tan(\theta/2) \cdot \sqrt{2gh} \, dh$.
6. Integrate 'h' from $0$ to $H$ to get the total theoretical discharge.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| Sl. No. | Formula | Description | Typical Use in Exam |
|---------|---------|-------------|---------------------|
| 1 | $Q_{th} = \dfrac{8}{15} \cdot C_d \cdot \tan\!\left(\dfrac{\theta}{2}\right) \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot H^{5/2}$ | Theoretical discharge over V-notch | The master formula — **must be memorized** |
| 2 | $Q_{act} = \dfrac{A \cdot R}{t} = \dfrac{a \cdot b \cdot R}{t}$ | Actual discharge (volumetric method) | For finding $C_d$ |
| 3 | $C_d = \dfrac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}}$ | Coefficient of Discharge | The key output of calibration |
| 4 | $H = H_{obs} - H_{apex}$ | True head = observed gauge head minus apex elevation | Correcting for notch geometry |
| 5 | $Q_{th,\, 90^\circ} = \dfrac{8}{15} \cdot C_d \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot H^{5/2}$ | For $\theta = 90^\circ$ (since $\tan 45^\circ = 1$) | Shortcut for standard lab setup |
| 6 | $v = \sqrt{2gh}$ | Torricelli's velocity | Elemental flow derivation |
| 7 | $H^{5/2} = H^2 \cdot \sqrt{H}$ | Useful numerical simplification | Calculator entry for H |
| 8 | $A = a \times b$ | Area of collecting tank (length × breadth) | For computing $Q_{act}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Units Safety Check:** Since $H^{5/2}$ has units of $\text{m}^{5/2}$ and $\sqrt{2g}$ has units of $\text{m}^{1/2}\text{s}^{-1}$, the product naturally yields $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ (discharge) — dimensional consistency is preserved.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Municipal Water Treatment Plants:** V-notches are installed at the outlet of sedimentation tanks to measure low flow rates of clarified water with high accuracy.
- **Irrigation Canals (Tail-end Distributaries):** Used to measure small discharges flowing to farm fields where rectangular weirs would give unreadably small heads.
- **Hydrology & Runoff Studies:** Field measurement of storm-water runoff from small catchments using portable V-notch weirs.
- **Chemical Process Industries:** Flow metering of effluents in neutralization tanks, where flow rates are typically very low.
- **Laboratory Research:** The standard tool in fluid mechanics labs worldwide for demonstrating the calibration technique.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Theoretical Discharge Formula

**Setup:** Consider a triangular notch with apex angle $\theta$, cut in a thin plate. The free liquid surface is at a height $H$ above the apex (the vertex at the bottom of the V).

**Step 1 — Define an elementary strip.**
Take an elemental horizontal strip at a depth $h$ below the free liquid surface (where $h$ varies from $0$ at the surface to $H$ at the apex).

The width 'b' of this strip, by similar triangles formed in the V-notch geometry, is:

$$b = 2(H - h) \tan\!\left(\dfrac{\theta}{2}\right)$$

**Why?** At depth $h$ below the surface, the remaining vertical distance to the apex is $(H - h)$. The V-notch sides flare outward at half-angle $(\theta/2)$ measured from the vertical centerline. So the horizontal half-width is $(H - h)\tan(\theta/2)$, and the full width is twice that.

**Step 2 — Write the elemental area.**
The thickness of the strip is $dh$, so its elemental area is:

$$dA = b \cdot dh = 2(H - h) \tan\!\left(\dfrac{\theta}{2}\right) \, dh$$

**Step 3 — Apply Torricelli's Theorem for elemental velocity.**
The theoretical velocity of water through the strip, considering it as a small orifice under head $h$:

$$v = \sqrt{2gh}$$

**Step 4 — Write the elemental discharge (without $C_d$).**
The product of elemental area and elemental velocity gives the elemental (ideal) discharge:

$$dQ_{ideal} = dA \cdot v = 2(H - h) \tan\!\left(\dfrac{\theta}{2}\right) \cdot \sqrt{2gh} \, dh$$

**Step 5 — Introduce the Coefficient of Discharge.**
Real flow is always less than ideal flow due to friction, contraction, and viscosity. So we multiply by $C_d$:

$$dQ = C_d \cdot 2(H - h) \tan\!\left(\dfrac{\theta}{2}\right) \cdot \sqrt{2gh} \, dh$$

**Step 6 — Set up the integration limits.**
The total discharge is obtained by integrating $dQ$ from the apex (where $h = H$) up to the free surface (where $h = 0$). However, by standard convention and to make the integral simpler, we integrate from $h = 0$ to $h = H$:

$$Q = 2 \, C_d \, \tan\!\left(\dfrac{\theta}{2}\right) \sqrt{2g} \int_{0}^{H} (H - h)\sqrt{h} \, dh$$

**Step 7 — Expand and split the integrand.**
Let us expand $(H - h)\sqrt{h} = H\sqrt{h} - h\sqrt{h} = H \cdot h^{1/2} - h^{3/2}$. So:

$$Q = 2 \, C_d \, \tan\!\left(\dfrac{\theta}{2}\right) \sqrt{2g} \left[ H \int_{0}^{H} h^{1/2} \, dh - \int_{0}^{H} h^{3/2} \, dh \right]$$

**Step 8 — Evaluate the two integrals using the power rule** $\int h^n \, dh = \dfrac{h^{n+1}}{n+1}$:

$$\int_{0}^{H} h^{1/2} \, dh = \left[ \dfrac{h^{3/2}}{3/2} \right]_{0}^{H} = \dfrac{2}{3} H^{3/2}$$

$$\int_{0}^{H} h^{3/2} \, dh = \left[ \dfrac{h^{5/2}}{5/2} \right]_{0}^{H} = \dfrac{2}{5} H^{5/2}$$

**Step 9 — Substitute the evaluated integrals back:**

$$Q = 2 \, C_d \, \tan\!\left(\dfrac{\theta}{2}\right) \sqrt{2g} \left[ H \cdot \dfrac{2}{3} H^{3/2} - \dfrac{2}{5} H^{5/2} \right]$$

**Step 10 — Factor out $H^{5/2}$:**
Notice that $H \cdot H^{3/2} = H^{5/2}$, so both terms share the factor $H^{5/2}$:

$$Q = 2 \, C_d \, \tan\!\left(\dfrac{\theta}{2}\right) \sqrt{2g} \cdot H^{5/2} \left[ \dfrac{2}{3} - \dfrac{2}{5} \right]$$

**Step 11 — Compute the bracket:**
$\dfrac{2}{3} - \dfrac{2}{5} = \dfrac{10 - 6}{15} = \dfrac{4}{15}$

**Step 12 — Multiply through:**

$$Q = 2 \, C_d \, \tan\!\left(\dfrac{\theta}{2}\right) \sqrt{2g} \cdot H^{5/2} \cdot \dfrac{4}{15}$$

**Step 13 — Final simplification (2 × 4/15 = 8/15):**

$$\boxed{\, Q = \dfrac{8}{15} \cdot C_d \cdot \tan\!\left(\dfrac{\theta}{2}\right) \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot H^{5/2} \,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The $8/15$ factor is the famous "magic number" of the V-notch formula.** It is the result of the integration and depends purely on the triangular geometry. For a rectangular notch, the analogous constant is $\frac{2}{3}$ (i.e., $Q = \frac{2}{3} C_d L \sqrt{2g} H^{3/2}$). The $\frac{8}{15}$ is larger because the area of a triangle grows quadratically with height, leading to faster discharge growth.

### 3.2 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Board-Style)

**Problem:** A $90^\circ$ triangular notch is used to measure a flow of water. The head over the notch is observed to be $H = 18 \text{ cm}$. The coefficient of discharge for the notch is $C_d = 0.60$. Calculate the discharge in litres per second (L/s). Take $g = 9.81 \; \text{m/s}^2$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — List the given data and convert to SI units.**
- $\theta = 90^\circ \;\Rightarrow\; \tan(45^\circ) = 1$
- $H = 18 \text{ cm} = 0.18 \text{ m}$
- $C_d = 0.60$
- $g = 9.81 \; \text{m/s}^2$

**Step 2 — Apply the formula:**

$$Q = \dfrac{8}{15} \cdot C_d \cdot \tan\!\left(\dfrac{\theta}{2}\right) \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot H^{5/2}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute values:**

$$Q = \dfrac{8}{15} \times 0.60 \times 1 \times \sqrt{2 \times 9.81} \times (0.18)^{5/2}$$

**Step 4 — Compute each piece:**

$$\sqrt{2 \times 9.81} = \sqrt{19.62} = 4.4294 \; \text{m}^{1/2}\text{s}^{-1}$$

$$(0.18)^{5/2} = (0.18)^2 \times \sqrt{0.18} = 0.0324 \times 0.4243 = 0.01375 \; \text{m}^{5/2}$$

**Step 5 — Combine:**

$$Q = 0.5333 \times 0.60 \times 4.4294 \times 0.01375$$

$$Q = 0.5333 \times 0.60 \times 0.06090$$

$$Q = 0.01949 \; \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 6 — Convert to L/s** (multiply by 1000):

$$\boxed{\, Q \approx 19.49 \; \text{L/s} \,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common Mistake in KTU Boards:** Students often forget to convert cm → m before raising to the power 5/2. Since the head is cubed-and-squarerooted, the conversion factor is $100^{5/2} = 100{,}000$ — a single cm/m error produces a discharge off by **5 orders of magnitude**. Always do the unit conversion FIRST.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Sources of Error in Calibration

| Error Source | Mechanism | Direction of Error in $C_d$ | Mitigation in KTU Exam |
|--------------|-----------|----------------------------|------------------------|
| Velocity of approach not negligible | Adds kinetic head to 'H' | $C_d$ appears higher | Use $H + \dfrac{v^2}{2g}$ correction (rarely asked) |
| Rounding of notch crest | Real edge not perfectly sharp | $C_d$ slightly lower | Use a sharp-edged plate as supplied |
| Inaccurate stopwatch timing | Human reaction time | Random scatter | Take $\geq 3$ readings and average |
| Collecting tank not level | Uneven area for water spread | $Q_{act}$ miscalculated | Level the tank before experiment |
| Pulsations in supply | Head 'H' fluctuates during collection | $C_d$ inconsistent | Use a stilling chamber; average gauge readings |
| Surcharge (submergence) | Downstream water level rises above apex | $C_d$ drops sharply | Ensure downstream level is at least 6 cm below apex |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Experimental Setup & Data Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Overhead Tank with Constant Supply] --> B[Stilling Chamber]
    B --> C[Working Channel with Notch Plate]
    C --> D[Point Gauge or Hook Gauge]
    C --> E[Weir Tank with Triangular Notch theta=90]
    E --> F[Discharging Nappe]
    F --> G[Collecting Tank with Ruler]
    G --> H[Stopwatch for Time t]
    H --> I[Volumetric Calculation Qact = A*R/t]
    D --> J[Observed Head Hobs]
    J --> K[True Head H = Hobs - Hapex]
    K --> L[Apply Formula Qth]
    I --> M[Compute Cd = Qact / Qth]
    L --> M
    M --> N[Tabulate Cd vs H]
    N --> O[Plot Calibration Curve Qact vs H]
    O --> P[Verify Cd is approximately 0.58 to 0.62]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Architecture: V-Notch Geometry & Elementary Strips

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S[Free Liquid Surface at Head H] --> M1[Strip 1: at h=0, width=2H tan theta/2]
    S --> M2[Strip 2: at h=h1, width=2 H-h1 tan theta/2]
    S --> M3[Strip 3: at h=h2, width=2 H-h2 tan theta/2]
    S --> Mn[Strip n: at h=H, width=0 at Apex]
    Mn --> APEX[Triangular Notch Apex Angle theta]
    M1 --> INT[Integrate over h from 0 to H]
    M2 --> INT
    M3 --> INT
    INT --> RES[Result: Q = 8/15 Cd tan theta/2 sqrt 2g H to power 5/2]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Calibration Procedure Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Start Experiment]) --> s1[Adjust supply to a low value]
    s1 --> s2[Wait for steady flow through V-notch]
    s2 --> s3[Note steady head H using pointer gauge]
    s3 --> s4[Start stopwatch and start collection simultaneously]
    s4 --> s5[Collect water for a fixed time, say t=60s]
    s5 --> s6[Stop stopwatch at exact end of t]
    s6 --> s7[Note rise R in collecting tank]
    s7 --> s8[Calculate Qact = A*R/t]
    s8 --> s9[Calculate Qth using V-notch formula]
    s9 --> s10[Find Cd = Qact / Qth]
    s10 --> s11{Repeat for next flow rate?}
    s11 --> |Yes| s1
    s11 --> |No| s12[Plot calibration curve: H on X-axis, Qact on Y-axis]
    s12 --> s13[Verify Cd lies between 0.58 and 0.62]
```
