# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram of the Experimental Setup

The pipe friction apparatus is a closed-loop or open-loop hydraulic test rig. The following Mermaid block diagram captures its functional architecture and signal/flow interactions.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Storage Tank] --> B[Sump Pump]
    B --> C[Constant Head Tank with Overflow]
    C --> D[Inlet Control Valve]
    D --> E[Straightener Section]
    E --> F[Upstream Pressure Tapping P1]
    F --> G[Test Pipe Section of Length L and Diameter D]
    G --> H[Downstream Pressure Tapping P2]
    H --> I[Differential U Tube Mercury Manometer]
    H --> J[Outlet Control Valve]
    J --> K[Collecting Tank with Measuring Cylinder]
    K --> L[Stopwatch for Time Measurement]
    I -.reads.-> M[Manometer Deflection Delta x in m]
    K -.reads.-> N[Volume Collected in m^3]
    L -.reads.-> O[Time t in s]
    M --> P[Calculation of h_f equals 12.6 times Delta x]
    N --> P
    O --> P
    P --> Q[Computation of Re and f_exp]
    Q --> R[Comparison with Moody Chart or Blasius]
    R --> S[Final Result Table and Percentage Deviation]
```

## 4.2 Logical Flow Chart of the Experimental Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start: Ensure tank is filled and pump is primed] --> S2[Open inlet valve fully, set flow to maximum]
    S2 --> S3[Allow flow to stabilize for 2 minutes]
    S3 --> S4[Measure manometer deflection Delta x]
    S4 --> S5[Collect water in measuring cylinder for time t]
    S5 --> S6[Record volume forall and time t]
    S6 --> S7[Reduce flow slightly using outlet valve]
    S7 --> S8{More readings to take?}
    S8 -- Yes --> S3
    S8 -- No --> S9[Repeat for at least 5 to 6 different flow rates]
    S9 --> S10[Compute Q, V, h_f, Re, f_exp for each trial]
    S10 --> S11[Plot f_exp vs Re on log log graph]
    S11 --> S12[Overlay Moody Chart curve for comparison]
    S12 --> S13[Compute percentage deviation and conclude]
    S13 --> S14[Stop: Close valves, switch off pump, drain manometer]
```

## 4.3 Schematic of the Manometer Reading Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Pressure Tapping 1 at Upstream] --> L1[Left Limb Water Column]
    L1 --> MH[Mercury Column Deflection Delta x]
    MH --> L2[Right Limb Water Column]
    L2 --> P2[Pressure Tapping 2 at Downstream]
    P1 --> R1[Higher Pressure Side]
    P2 --> R2[Lower Pressure Side]
    MH -. shows .-> R3[Mercury pushed from high pressure to low pressure side]
```

## 4.4 Typical Apparatus Components (KTU Lab Inventory)

| Component | Material / Specification | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Test pipe | Commercial steel / GI, $D = 20$–$30$ mm, $L \approx 1$–$2$ m | Provides the friction surface |
| Constant head tank | Mild steel with overflow weir | Maintains steady inlet pressure |
| Mercury manometer | Borosilicate glass U-tube, 6 mm ID | Measures differential pressure |
| Collecting tank | Calibration grade, $\pm 1$ mL | Measures volume precisely |
| Stopwatch | Digital, 0.01 s resolution | Measures collection time |
| Control valves | Brass globe valves, $\frac{1}{2}$ inch | Vary the flow rate |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**State the Darcy–Weisbach equation and explain the significance of the friction factor in pipe flow.**

**Model Answer:**
The Darcy–Weisbach equation expresses the head loss $h_f$ due to friction in a pipe as:

$$h_f = \frac{f L V^2}{2 g D}$$

where $L$ is the pipe length, $V$ is the mean velocity, $D$ is the pipe diameter, $g$ is gravitational acceleration, and $f$ is the **Darcy friction factor**.

**Significance of $f$:**
- $f$ is a **dimensionless** quantity that quantifies the resistance offered by the pipe wall to the flowing fluid.
- It accounts for both the **viscous effects** (Reynolds number) and the **wall roughness** ($\varepsilon/D$).
- $f$ is essential for predicting **pump power requirements**, **pipeline sizing**, and **energy losses** in distribution networks.
- In laminar flow, $f = 64/Re$ (independent of roughness); in turbulent flow, $f$ is obtained from the **Moody chart** or empirical correlations.

*(3 marks: 1 for equation, 2 for significance)*

### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Distinguish between the Darcy friction factor and the Fanning friction factor.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Darcy Friction Factor ($f_D$) | Fanning Friction Factor ($f_F$) |
|---|---|---|
| Relation to head loss | $h_f = \dfrac{f_D L V^2}{2 g D}$ | $h_f = \dfrac{4 f_F L V^2}{2 g D}$ |
| Numerical value | **4 times** $f_F$ | $\dfrac{1}{4}$ of $f_D$ |
| Laminar formula | $f_D = \dfrac{64}{Re}$ | $f_F = \dfrac{16}{Re}$ |
| Common usage | Civil, mechanical, chemical engineering (KTU syllabus) | Chemical engineering (older texts) |

**KTU Note:** The 2024 Scheme syllabus **uniformly uses the Darcy friction factor**.

*(3 marks: comparison + relation $f_D = 4 f_F$)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each – Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, split into two sub-parts of 7 marks each. Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the experimental setup used to determine the friction coefficient in a pipe. List the instruments used and the measurements taken. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In a pipe friction experiment, water flows through a horizontal commercial steel pipe of diameter $D = 28$ mm and length $L = 2.0$ m. The differential mercury manometer reads $\Delta x = 60$ mm. The water collected in 25 seconds is 5.5 litres. Water temperature is $25 \,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ($\nu = 0.89 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s). Determine the experimental Darcy friction factor and compare it with the value from the Blasius equation. Comment on the deviation. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

**Apparatus Description:**

1. **Sump tank and centrifugal pump:** Draws water from the reservoir and delivers it to the test section.
2. **Constant head tank (with overflow weir):** Ensures a steady head at the inlet so that flow does not fluctuate during a reading.
3. **Inlet control valve:** Adjusts the flow rate to the desired trial value.
4. **Stilling chamber / honeycomb straightener:** Removes swirl and large-scale turbulence to ensure fully developed flow at the test section.
5. **Test pipe section:** A straight, horizontal pipe of known length $L$ (typically 1.5–2.0 m) and known internal diameter $D$, with two pressure tappings at its ends.
6. **Differential U-tube mercury manometer:** Connected between the two tappings; measures the pressure drop $\Delta p = (\rho_{Hg} - \rho_w) g \Delta x$.
7. **Collecting tank with measuring cylinder:** Receives the outflow for a measured duration to determine the volumetric flow rate.
8. **Stopwatch:** Records the collection time.
9. **Thermometer:** Monitors the water temperature to obtain the correct kinematic viscosity.

**Measurements recorded per trial:**
- Differential manometer reading $\Delta x$ (m)
- Volume of water collected $\forall$ (m³)
- Time of collection $t$ (s)
- Water temperature $T$ (°C)

**Sketch (already shown in Section 4.1).** *(7 marks: 3 for sketch, 2 for component list, 2 for measurements)*

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Step 1 — Volumetric flow rate.**

$$Q = \frac{\forall}{t} = \frac{5.5 \times 10^{-3}}{25} = 2.2 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 2 — Mean velocity.**

$$V = \frac{4Q}{\pi D^2} = \frac{4 \times 2.2 \times 10^{-4}}{\pi \times (0.028)^2} = \frac{8.8 \times 10^{-4}}{2.463 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.3573 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Head loss from the manometer.**

$$h_f = 12.6 \times \Delta x = 12.6 \times 0.060 = 0.756 \text{ m of water}$$

**Step 4 — Experimental friction factor.**

$$f_{exp} = \frac{2 g D h_f}{L V^2} = \frac{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.028 \times 0.756}{2.0 \times (0.3573)^2}$$

$$f_{exp} = \frac{0.4153}{0.2553} = 0.01627$$

**Step 5 — Reynolds number.**

$$Re = \frac{4Q}{\pi D \nu} = \frac{4 \times 2.2 \times 10^{-4}}{\pi \times 0.028 \times 0.89 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{8.8 \times 10^{-4}}{7.827 \times 10^{-8}} = 11{,}243$$

**Step 6 — Theoretical $f$ from Blasius equation.**

$$f_{Blasius} = \frac{0.316}{Re^{0.25}} = \frac{0.316}{(11243)^{0.25}} = \frac{0.316}{10.30} = 0.03068$$

**Step 7 — Percentage deviation.**

$$\% \text{ Deviation} = \frac{\vert 0.01627 - 0.03068 \vert}{0.03068} \times 100 = 46.97\%$$

**Comment:** Such a high deviation indicates that the experimental pipe is **not smooth**; the Blasius equation is invalid for rough pipes. A more appropriate theoretical value should be obtained from the **Colebrook–White equation** or directly from the **Moody chart** at the corresponding $\varepsilon/D$. Possible sources of experimental error include air bubble entrapment in the manometer limbs, inaccurate volume/time measurement, and non-uniform flow development at the test section.

*(7 marks: 1 for Q, 1 for V, 1 for h_f, 1 for f_exp, 1 for Re, 1 for f_Blasius, 1 for deviation and comment)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the use of the Moody chart in determining the friction factor. How is the chart structured? What information is needed to read it? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A pipe of diameter $D = 50$ mm carries oil of specific gravity 0.85 and kinematic viscosity $4 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s at a mean velocity of $2.5$ m/s. The head loss over a 10 m length is measured as 1.2 m of oil. Compute: (i) the experimental friction factor, (ii) the Reynolds number, and (iii) the theoretical friction factor using $f = 64/Re$. Comment on the flow regime. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

The **Moody chart** (proposed by Lewis F. Moody in 1944) is the universal graphical tool for determining the Darcy friction factor $f$ for any pipe flow scenario.

**Chart Structure:**
- **X-axis:** Reynolds number $Re$ on a **logarithmic scale**, ranging from $10^0$ to $10^8$.
- **Y-axis:** Darcy friction factor $f$ on a **logarithmic scale**, ranging from $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-1}$.
- **Curves:** A family of curves, each corresponding to a fixed value of **relative roughness** $\varepsilon/D$ (e.g., 0.00001, 0.00005, 0.0001, 0.0002, …, 0.05).
- **Laminar region ($Re < 2000$):** A single straight line $f = 64/Re$, independent of roughness.
- **Transitional region ($2000 < Re < 4000$):** Dashed/unstable region, $f$ cannot be reliably read.
- **Turbulent region ($Re > 4000$):** Curves fan out and bend — higher $\varepsilon/D$ gives higher $f$.

**Information Needed:**
1. Reynolds number $Re$ (to locate the x-coordinate).
2. Relative roughness $\varepsilon/D$ (to select the correct curve).
3. The pipe material's roughness $\varepsilon$ is obtained from standard tables.

**Use:** Locate $Re$ on the x-axis, move vertically to intersect the appropriate roughness curve, then read the $f$ value on the y-axis.

*(7 marks: 3 for structure, 2 for information, 2 for usage procedure)*

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Given:**
- $D = 50 \text{ mm} = 0.050$ m
- Specific gravity of oil, $S = 0.85$, so $\rho = 850$ kg/m³
- Kinematic viscosity, $\nu = 4 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s
- Velocity, $V = 2.5$ m/s
- Length, $L = 10$ m
- Head loss, $h_f = 1.2$ m of oil

**(i) Experimental friction factor:**

$$f_{exp} = \frac{2 g D h_f}{L V^2} = \frac{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.050 \times 1.2}{10 \times (2.5)^2} = \frac{1.1772}{62.5} = 0.01884$$

**(ii) Reynolds number:**

$$Re = \frac{V D}{\nu} = \frac{2.5 \times 0.050}{4 \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{0.125}{4 \times 10^{-5}} = 3125$$

**(iii) Theoretical $f$ using $f = 64/Re$:**

$$f_{thy} = \frac{64}{3125} = 0.02048$$

**Comment on flow regime:**
$Re = 3125$ falls in the **transitional regime** ($2000 < Re < 4000$). The formula $f = 64/Re$ is strictly valid only for **laminar** flow. Its use here is an approximation, and the small deviation ($\approx 8.7\%$) between $f_{exp}$ and $f_{thy}$ reflects the onset of turbulence. The Moody chart cannot be reliably read in the transition zone.

*(7 marks: 2 for f_exp, 2 for Re, 2 for f_Blasius, 1 for comment)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Lose Marks (and how to avoid it):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the factor 12.6** when computing $h_f$ from $\Delta x$ — leads to $f_{exp}$ off by an order of magnitude. Always write: $h_f = (\rho_{Hg}/\rho_w - 1) \cdot \Delta x = 12.6 \, \Delta x$.
> 2. **Confusing Darcy and Fanning** — the lab always uses Darcy. Don't write $f = 16/Re$ unless the question explicitly says Fanning.
> 3. **Using wrong velocity formula** — the velocity is mean velocity $V = Q/A = 4Q/(\pi D^2)$, NOT the centreline velocity.
> 4. **Applying Blasius outside its range** — Blasius is valid only for $Re < 10^5$ on smooth pipes. Beyond that, use Colebrook.
> 5. **Forgetting units in Reynolds number** — $\nu$ must be in m²/s, NOT centistokes. (1 cSt = $10^{-6}$ m²/s)
> 6. **No mention of sources of error** — KTU expects at least 2 sources (e.g., air bubble in manometer, fluctuation of head, parallax in manometer reading).
> 7. **Not commenting on flow regime** — always state whether $Re < 2000$ (laminar), $2000 < Re < 4000$ (transitional), or $Re > 4000$ (turbulent) before applying a formula.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep)**

- **Darcy–Weisbach equation:** $h_f = \dfrac{f L V^2}{2 g D}$ — the bedrock formula of this experiment.
- **Darcy $f$ = 4 × Fanning $f_F$** — universally use Darcy in KTU 2024.
- **Laminar:** $f = 64/Re$ (analytical, exact).
- **Turbulent smooth:** $f = 0.316 / Re^{0.25}$ (Blasius, valid for $Re < 10^5$).
- **General turbulent:** Colebrook–White (implicit, requires iteration or Moody chart).
- **Manometer head loss:** $h_f = 12.6 \, \Delta x$ for mercury–water manometer.
- **Reynolds number:** $Re = V D / \nu = 4Q / (\pi D \nu)$.
- **Mean velocity:** $V = 4Q / (\pi D^2)$.
- **Working formula for $f_{exp}$:** $f = \dfrac{2 g D h_f}{L V^2}$ — derived from Darcy–Weisbach.
- **Standard physical constants:** $g = 9.81$ m/s², $\rho_{Hg} = 13600$ kg/m³, $\nu_w(25°\mathrm{C}) = 0.89 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s.
- **Flow regime boundaries:** Laminar $Re < 2000$; Transitional $2000 \le Re \le 4000$; Turbulent $Re > 4000$.
- **Moody chart:** log–log plot of $f$ vs $Re$, parameterised by $\varepsilon/D$.
- **At least 5–6 trials** at different flow rates are required; plot $f$ vs $Re$ and overlay the Moody chart.
- **Typical error sources:** air bubble in manometer, fluctuating head tank, parallax in $\Delta x$, inaccurate volume/time, non-uniform pipe roughness.
- **Always state the flow regime** before selecting the theoretical formula.
- **Always convert all dimensions to SI** (mm → m, mL → m³, min → s) before substituting.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of Loss Coefficient for Pipe Fittings
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCEL308 - FLUID MECHANICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of Loss Coefficient for Pipe Fittings

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Loss Coefficient (K)** for a pipe fitting is a dimensionless quantity that characterizes the irreversible head loss of mechanical energy that occurs when fluid flows through a fitting (such as an elbow, bend, tee, valve, or sudden contraction/expansion). It is formally defined as the ratio of the head loss across the fitting to the **velocity head** of the flowing fluid.

The governing mathematical statement (Darcy–Weisbach based form) is:

$$h_L = K \cdot \frac{V^{2}}{2g}$$

where:
- $h_L$ = head loss across the fitting (in **meters of fluid**)
- $K$ = dimensionless loss coefficient
- $V$ = mean velocity of flow (in **m/s**)
- $g$ = acceleration due to gravity = **9.81 m/s²**

The loss coefficient is also sometimes called the **resistance coefficient (K)** or **minor loss coefficient**, because the losses contributed by fittings are usually much smaller than the **major (frictional) losses** along the straight pipe length. However, in a short pipe with multiple fittings, these so-called "minor" losses can collectively exceed the major loss.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a highway toll booth on a fast expressway.**

Imagine cars (fluid) cruising at a constant high speed (V) on a smooth highway. When they approach a toll plaza (the pipe fitting), they must slow down, queue up, change lanes, and accelerate back. This sudden disturbance causes:
1. **Energy dissipation** as heat (engine braking, friction between cars)
2. **Local turbulence** at the merge/divergence points
3. A net **loss of kinetic energy** that cannot be recovered downstream

Similarly, when water rushes through a 90° elbow, the fluid particles are forced to change direction violently. The outer particles travel a longer path, the inner particles bunch up, eddies form, and a portion of the mechanical (pressure) energy is permanently converted into heat. The **loss coefficient (K)** is simply a number that quantifies *how much* of the kinetic energy is wasted at that particular fitting.

## 1.3 Standard KTU Experimental Context

In the PCCEL308 lab, the test rig consists of:
- A **horizontal pipe test section** with a chosen fitting (e.g., 90° elbow, 45° bend, sudden expansion, sudden contraction, gate valve, or globe valve)
- **Pressure tappings** (piezometer nipples) placed at section 1 (upstream, distance ≥ 10D from the fitting) and section 2 (downstream, distance ≥ 5D from the fitting) to ensure fully developed flow
- A **U-tube differential manometer** (typically mercury–water or CCl₄–water) connected across the two tappings
- A **measuring tank / rotameter** for discharge measurement
- A **constant-head overhead tank** supplying steady, gravity-driven flow

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why measure pressure at 10D upstream and 5D downstream?**
> Because the flow disturbance caused by a fitting propagates a finite distance upstream (vena contracta) and downstream (wake). Only at these minimum distances does the flow re-attach and become fully developed, so the **Bernoulli equation** can be validly applied between sections 1 and 2.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Reference Values

| Parameter | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|
| Acceleration due to gravity (g) | **9.81 m/s²** |
| Density of water (ρ_w) at 25 °C | **997 kg/m³** |
| Specific gravity of mercury (S_Hg) | **13.6** |
| Specific gravity of CCl₄ | **1.58** |
| Atmospheric pressure (Kerala lab) | ≈ **1.013 × 10⁵ Pa** |
| Loss coefficient — 90° standard elbow | K ≈ **0.9** (threaded) / **0.3** (flanged, long radius) |
| Loss coefficient — gate valve (fully open) | K ≈ **0.15 – 0.19** |
| Loss coefficient — globe valve (fully open) | K ≈ **6.0 – 10.0** |
| Loss coefficient — sudden expansion (1:2) | K ≈ **0.5625** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation: Why Does a Fitting Cause Energy Loss?

When fluid traverses a fitting, the following physical phenomena occur in sequence:

1. **Flow separation**: The streamlines cannot follow the sharp geometric change; they detach from the wall, forming a free shear layer.
2. **Vena contracta formation**: The flow area momentarily shrinks to a minimum cross-section smaller than the geometric opening.
3. **Turbulent mixing zone**: The high-velocity core jet mixes violently with the slower recirculating fluid in the separated region, generating intense small-scale turbulence.
4. **Viscous dissipation**: Turbulent eddies cascade down to the Kolmogorov micro-scale, where viscosity converts kinetic energy irreversibly into heat (entropy increase).
5. **Flow reattachment**: Downstream of the fitting, the boundary layer gradually re-attaches to the wall, and the velocity profile returns to a fully developed shape (after ≥ 5D distance).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key KTU Takeaway**: Loss coefficient (K) is a *property of the geometry and flow regime*, not of the fluid. For a given fitting shape, K is approximately constant over a wide range of Reynolds numbers, provided the flow is fully turbulent.

## 2.2 Energy Equation Between Two Sections

Applying the **steady-flow energy equation (SFEE)** between upstream section (1) and downstream section (2), both on the pipe centerline (so z₁ = z₂ for a horizontal test section):

$$\frac{p_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^{2}}{2g} = \frac{p_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^{2}}{2g} + h_L$$

For a constant-diameter pipe test section: $V_1 = V_2 = V$, so the velocity-head terms cancel:

$$h_L = \frac{p_1 - p_2}{\rho g} = \frac{\Delta p}{\rho g}$$

The differential manometer directly reads $\Delta p$ (or its equivalent in terms of fluid column heights). Hence:

$$h_L = \frac{\Delta p}{\rho g} = \left(\frac{\rho_m}{\rho_f} - 1\right) \cdot h \quad \text{(for a U-tube manometer)}$$

where:
- $\rho_m$ = density of manometric fluid (mercury or CCl₄)
- $\rho_f$ = density of flowing fluid (water)
- $h$ = manometer deflection (difference in levels of manometric fluid)

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $h_L = K \dfrac{V^{2}}{2g}$ | Definition of loss coefficient | m |
| 2 | $K = \dfrac{2g \, h_L}{V^{2}}$ | Direct K from measured head loss & velocity | — |
| 3 | $V = \dfrac{Q}{A} = \dfrac{4Q}{\pi D^{2}}$ | Mean velocity from discharge & pipe diameter | m/s |
| 4 | $Q = \dfrac{V}{t} \times \text{Tank area}$ | Volumetric discharge from collecting tank | m³/s |
| 5 | $Q = C_d \, A_t \, \sqrt{2g h}$ | Discharge through a V-notch / orifice (if used) | m³/s |
| 6 | $h_L = \left( \dfrac{S_m}{S_f} - 1 \right) h$ | Head loss from U-tube manometer (water–mercury) | m of water |
| 7 | $h_L = \left( \dfrac{\rho_m}{\rho_f} - 1 \right) h$ | General manometer formula | m of water |
| 8 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \dfrac{VD}{\nu}$ | Reynolds number (flow regime check) | — |
| 9 | $h_{L,\text{total}} = \sum K_i \dfrac{V_i^{2}}{2g}$ | Cumulative minor losses for a system | m |
| 10 | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\text{Actual K}}{\text{Ideal K}} \times 100$ | Efficiency of fitting (%) | % |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Always use $S_m / S_f$ (specific gravity ratio) in the manometer formula, NOT densities directly.** This avoids confusion at examination halls and matches the standard KTU valuation key.

## 2.4 Loss Coefficient for Common Fitting Categories (Reference)

> [!NOTE]
> These are the values examiners expect you to quote when asked *"What is the theoretical K of a 90° elbow?"*. Always quote the **standard K value** before your experimental K, then comment on the % deviation.

| Fitting Type | K (Threaded) | K (Flanged) | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 90° Elbow (standard) | 0.9 | 0.3 | Long radius reduces K |
| 45° Elbow | 0.4 | 0.2 | — |
| Tee (line flow) | 0.9 | 0.2 | — |
| Tee (branch flow) | 2.0 | 1.0 | Higher turbulence |
| Gate valve (open) | 0.19 | 0.15 | Best for fully open service |
| Globe valve (open) | 10.0 | 6.0 | Highest loss in valves |
| Sudden expansion (1:2) | 0.5625 | — | Borda–Carnot: $K = (1-A_1/A_2)^2$ |
| Sudden contraction | 0.5 (1:2) | — | See standard tables |

## 2.5 Engineering Utility

Loss coefficients are used by practicing engineers to:

1. **Design piping networks** in water-supply schemes, HVAC, refineries, and chemical plants. The total pumping head must overcome $\sum h_L + h_f$, so accurate K values are critical to specifying pump pressure.
2. **Predict energy consumption** of a hydraulic system over its operating life.
3. **Compare fittings** from different manufacturers or materials (e.g., long-radius vs. short-radius elbow).
4. **Calibrate CFD (Computational Fluid Dynamics) simulations** of complex piping systems.
5. **Troubleshoot excessive pressure drop** in field-installed systems (e.g., a partially closed valve has a much higher K than its nameplate value).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Loss Coefficient from Manometer Reading

### Step 1 — Identify the system

A horizontal pipe of internal diameter **D** carries water at a steady discharge **Q**. A fitting (e.g., 90° elbow) is installed between two pressure tappings located at:
- Section 1: 10D upstream of the fitting
- Section 2: 5D downstream of the fitting

A U-tube manometer containing mercury is connected across these tappings.

### Step 2 — Write the manometric equation

Let $h$ be the difference in mercury levels (deflection). Applying hydrostatic balance from point A (tapping 1) down through mercury, up through water to point B (tapping 2):

$$p_1 + \rho_w g \, y_1 - \rho_{Hg} g \, h - \rho_w g (y_2 + h) = p_2$$

Rearranging (and noting $y_1 - y_2$ cancels for a horizontal pipe):

$$p_1 - p_2 = (\rho_{Hg} - \rho_w) \, g \, h$$

### Step 3 — Convert to head of flowing fluid (water)

The head loss in *meters of water* is:

$$h_L = \frac{p_1 - p_2}{\rho_w g} = \frac{(\rho_{Hg} - \rho_w) \, g \, h}{\rho_w g}$$

$$\boxed{h_L = \left(\frac{\rho_{Hg}}{\rho_w} - 1\right) h = (S_{Hg} - 1) \, h}$$

For mercury ($S_{Hg} = 13.6$):

$$h_L = 12.6 \, h$$

### Step 4 — Compute the mean velocity

$$V = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{Q}{\dfrac{\pi D^{2}}{4}} = \frac{4Q}{\pi D^{2}}$$

### Step 5 — Apply the loss-coefficient definition

From $h_L = K \dfrac{V^{2}}{2g}$, solve for K:

$$\boxed{K = \frac{2g \, h_L}{V^{2}}}$$

### Step 6 — Substitute the experimental quantities

$$K = \frac{2g \, (S_{Hg} - 1) \, h}{\left(\dfrac{4Q}{\pi D^{2}}\right)^{2}}$$

$$K = \frac{2g \, (S_{Hg} - 1) \, h \, \pi^{2} D^{4}}{16 \, Q^{2}}$$

$$\boxed{K = \frac{g \, (S_{Hg} - 1) \, h \, \pi^{2} D^{4}}{8 \, Q^{2}}}$$

This is the **master formula** the student must commit to memory for the KTU lab examination.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Full KTU-Style Solution)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sample Problem (Dec 2023 KTU Pattern)**
> In a pipe-friction apparatus, water flows through a 90° elbow fitted in a horizontal pipe of internal diameter **25 mm**. The discharge collected in a measuring tank of area **0.05 m²** in **20 seconds** is **24 litres**. The deflection in a mercury U-tube manometer connected across pressure tappings placed 10D upstream and 5D downstream of the elbow is **120 mm**. Determine the loss coefficient of the elbow.

### Solution — Step by Step

**Given Data:**

$$D = 25 \text{ mm} = 0.025 \text{ m}$$
$$A_{\text{tank}} = 0.05 \text{ m}^{2}, \quad t = 20 \text{ s}, \quad V_{\text{collected}} = 24 \text{ L} = 0.024 \text{ m}^{3}$$
$$h = 120 \text{ mm} = 0.120 \text{ m}, \quad S_{Hg} = 13.6, \quad g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^{2}$$

**Step 1 — Volumetric discharge**

$$Q = \frac{V_{\text{collected}}}{t} = \frac{0.024}{20} = 1.2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s}$$

**Step 2 — Cross-sectional area of pipe**

$$A = \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.025)^{2}}{4} = 4.9087 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Mean velocity of flow**

$$V = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{1.2 \times 10^{-3}}{4.9087 \times 10^{-4}} = 2.4447 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 4 — Head loss from manometer reading**

$$h_L = (S_{Hg} - 1) \, h = (13.6 - 1) \times 0.120 = 12.6 \times 0.120 = 1.512 \text{ m of water}$$

**Step 5 — Loss coefficient**

$$K = \frac{2g \, h_L}{V^{2}} = \frac{2 \times 9.81 \times 1.512}{(2.4447)^{2}}$$

$$K = \frac{29.6643}{5.9766} = 4.963$$

**Step 6 — Compare with standard value**

Standard K for a 90° threaded elbow ≈ **0.9** (textbook value).
% Deviation:

$$\% \text{ deviation} = \frac{K_{\text{exp}} - K_{\text{std}}}{K_{\text{std}}} \times 100$$

Wait — in practice a 90° elbow in a lab rig is closer to K = 0.9. The high K in this example indicates a *short-radius* elbow, or that the upstream/downstream tapings were not at the prescribed distances (so the flow was not fully developed). This is a *typical examiner trick* — your answer must discuss probable causes of deviation.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examination Pitfall**: Students often confuse $\rho_{Hg}/\rho_w = 13.6$ with $(13.6 - 1) = 12.6$ in the head-loss step. Forgetting to subtract 1 gives K values inflated by ~8%, which costs 1 mark.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Reading Errors & Sensitivity

| Source of Error | Direction of K | Magnitude (Typical) |
|---|---|---|
| Manometer inverted / read wrong limb | Wrong sign | Whole K negative |
| Tappings < 10D upstream (in flow disturbance zone) | K overstated | Up to +30% |
| Tappings < 5D downstream (wake zone) | K overstated | Up to +20% |
| Pipe not horizontal (z₁ ≠ z₂) | Random | Must include $\Delta z$ in energy equation |
| Collecting tank calibration off | K scaled by 1/Q² | +5% per cm³ |
| Stopwatch start/stop error ±0.5 s | K scaled by 1/Q² | ±2.5% |
| Temperature change → ρ_w changes | K changes by ~0.3% per °C | Negligible |

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation Note**: For all minor losses, K is theoretically independent of Reynolds number once the flow is **fully turbulent (Re > 4000)**. Plotting K vs. Re should yield a near-horizontal line. A falling trend indicates the flow is in the *laminar-to-turbulent transition regime*, which examiners will deduct marks for not commenting upon.

## 3.4 Graphical Plot Specification (For Record Submission)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU lab record must include the following graphs:
> 1. **$h_L$ vs. $V^{2}$** → straight line through origin; slope = $K/2g$
> 2. **K vs. Re** → nearly horizontal line for fully turbulent flow
> 3. **K vs. V** → constant line for fully turbulent flow (optional alternate)

The best-fit slope of $h_L$ vs. $V^{2}$ is determined by the **least-squares method**:

$$\text{slope} = \frac{n \sum V^{2} h_L - \sum V^{2} \sum h_L}{n \sum (V^{2})^{2} - \left(\sum V^{2}\right)^{2}}$$

$$\Rightarrow \quad K = 2g \times \text{slope}$$

This slope-based K is *more accurate* than a single-trial K because it averages out random reading errors.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Test Rig

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[OHT\nOverhead Tank] --> A2[Stabilizing\nBaffle]
    A2 --> A3[Inlet\nStraight Pipe 10D]
    A3 --> A4[Test Fitting\nElbow / Bend / Valve]
    A4 --> A5[Outlet\nStraight Pipe 5D]
    A5 --> A6[Control\nValve]
    A6 --> A7[Measuring\nTank]
    A7 --> A8[Stopwatch\nand Scale]

    M1[Pressure Tapping 1\n10D Upstream] --> N1[U-Tube Manometer\nLeft Limb]
    M2[Pressure Tapping 2\n5D Downstream] --> N2[U-Tube Manometer\nRight Limb]
    N1 --> N3[Differential\nManometer Reading h]
    N2 --> N3
    N3 --> N4[Compute\nHead Loss hL]
    A8 --> N5[Compute\nDischarge Q]
    A8 --> N6[Compute\nVelocity V]
    N4 --> N7[Compute\nK Value]
    N6 --> N7
    N7 --> N8[Compare with\nStandard K]
    N8 --> N9[Plot hL vs V squared\nand K vs Re]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Procedure Flowchart for the Experiment

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Step 1: Inspect rig and identify fitting] --> P2[Step 2: Ensure manometer is filled and zeroed]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3: Open inlet valve slowly to fill the pipe]
    P3 --> P4[Step 4: Open control valve to set desired flow]
    P4 --> P5[Step 5: Wait for steady manometer reading]
    P5 --> P6[Step 6: Record manometer deflection h]
    P6 --> P7[Step 7: Start stopwatch and collect water in tank]
    P7 --> P8[Step 8: After fixed time t stop stopwatch and close inlet]
    P8 --> P9[Step 9: Measure rise in water level in measuring tank]
    P9 --> P10[Step 10: Compute Q, V, hL, K]
    P10 --> P11[Step 11: Repeat for at least 5 different flow rates]
    P11 --> P12[Step 12: Plot graphs and report mean K]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block — Energy-Grade Line Diagram (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Section 1\nUpstream\np1 rho g high] -->|No loss\nin straight pipe| S2[Fitting Entry\nLocal separation\nbegins]
    S2 -->|Sudden drop\nin EGL| S3[Vena Contracta\nand turbulence]
    S3 -->|Energy dissipated\nas heat| S4[Fitting Exit\nReattachment zone]
    S4 -->|Small additional\nloss in wake| S5[Section 2\nDownstream\np2 rho g low]
    S1 --- EGL1[EGL Line 1]
    S5 --- EGL2[EGL Line 2 Lower]
    EGL1 -.->|Vertical drop = hL| EGL2
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **Energy Grade Line (EGL)** drops vertically by $h_L$ across the fitting. The **Hydraulic Grade Line (HGL)** lies exactly $V^{2}/2g$ below the EGL everywhere in a constant-diameter pipe. The vertical separation between EGL-upstream and EGL-downstream IS the loss coefficient multiplied by velocity head.

## 4.4 ASCII Schematic — U-Tube Manometer

```
                              |
   __________                 |
  |          |                |  Water in pipe
  | SECTION 1|                |  (p1)
  |__________|                |
        \                     |
         \_______________    _|_
              \             \_/ \________
               \             |  Hg  |     \
                \____________|______|______\________
                              \  |  /        \
                               \_|_/          \
                                 |             |
   __________                    |             |
  |          |                   |             |
  | SECTION 2|                   |             |
  |__________|                   |             |
        |                        |             |
                              (h = manometer
                                  deflection)
```

| Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|
| p1 | Pressure at upstream tapping |
| p2 | Pressure at downstream tapping |
| h | Mercury level difference (mm) |
| $\rho_{Hg}$ | Density of mercury = 13600 kg/m³ |
| $\rho_w$ | Density of water = 1000 kg/m³ |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**Define loss coefficient of a pipe fitting. What are the factors on which it depends?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 marks):** The loss coefficient K of a pipe fitting is defined as the dimensionless number that, when multiplied by the velocity head $V^{2}/2g$, gives the head loss $h_L$ across the fitting, i.e., $h_L = K \cdot V^{2}/2g$.
- **Factors (1.5 marks):** K depends on (i) **fitting geometry** — shape, radius, angle; (ii) **surface roughness**; (iii) **flow regime** — Reynolds number (in transition zone only); (iv) **area ratio** for sudden expansion/contraction; (v) **degree of opening** in the case of valves.

### Question A2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Distinguish between major losses and minor losses in a pipe flow.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Major Loss | Minor Loss |
|---|---|
| Due to friction along straight pipe length | Due to fittings, bends, valves, sudden changes |
| Distributed uniformly over the length | Localized at discrete points |
| Computed by Darcy–Weisbach: $h_f = f \dfrac{L}{D} \dfrac{V^{2}}{2g}$ | Computed by $h_L = K \dfrac{V^{2}}{2g}$ |
| Depends on pipe roughness & Re | Depends on fitting geometry & Re |
| Dominant in long pipelines | Significant in short pipe sections with many fittings |

> **Mnemonic**: "Major = Many meters of pipe", "Minor = Many accessories in short runs"

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A. (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**(a)** Derive an expression for the loss coefficient K of a pipe fitting in terms of the manometer reading, discharge, and pipe diameter, when a differential U-tube manometer is used across the fitting. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In a pipe fitting test rig, water flows at 1.5 L/s through a 30 mm diameter horizontal pipe fitted with a sudden expansion from 30 mm to 60 mm. A differential mercury manometer connected across the expansion shows a deflection of 50 mm. Calculate: (i) the head loss, (ii) the loss coefficient, and (iii) the percentage deviation from the theoretical Borda–Carnot value. Take $S_{Hg} = 13.6$. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Apply steady-flow energy equation between sections 1 and 2 of the constant-diameter pipe (upstream and downstream of the fitting):

$$\frac{p_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^{2}}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{p_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^{2}}{2g} + z_2 + h_L$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** For a horizontal pipe, $z_1 = z_2$. For a constant diameter, $V_1 = V_2 = V$. The equation reduces to:

$$h_L = \frac{p_1 - p_2}{\rho g}$$

**Step 3 (1.5 marks):** For a U-tube manometer containing mercury, apply hydrostatic balance from the upstream tapping through the manometer fluid to the downstream tapping:

$$p_1 - p_2 = (\rho_{Hg} - \rho_w) \, g \, h$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Substitute and simplify to obtain the head loss in metres of water:

$$h_L = \frac{(\rho_{Hg} - \rho_w) \, g \, h}{\rho_w \, g} = (S_{Hg} - 1) \, h$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** The mean velocity from the discharge Q and pipe diameter D is:

$$V = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{4Q}{\pi D^{2}}$$

**Step 6 (1.5 marks):** From the definition of loss coefficient, $K = \dfrac{2g \, h_L}{V^{2}}$. Substituting the expressions for $h_L$ and $V$:

$$\boxed{K = \frac{g \, (S_{Hg} - 1) \, h \, \pi^{2} \, D^{4}}{8 \, Q^{2}}}$$

This is the required working formula.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given Data:**

$$Q = 1.5 \text{ L/s} = 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s}, \quad D_1 = 0.030 \text{ m}$$
$$h = 50 \text{ mm} = 0.050 \text{ m}, \quad S_{Hg} = 13.6, \quad g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^{2}$$

**Sub-part (i) — Head Loss (2 marks):**

$$h_L = (S_{Hg} - 1) \, h = (13.6 - 1) \times 0.050 = 12.6 \times 0.050 = 0.630 \text{ m of water}$$

**Sub-part (ii) — Loss Coefficient (3 marks):**

Velocity in the smaller (upstream) section, $D_1 = 30 \text{ mm}$:

$$A_1 = \frac{\pi (0.030)^{2}}{4} = 7.0686 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^{2}$$

$$V_1 = \frac{Q}{A_1} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^{-3}}{7.0686 \times 10^{-4}} = 2.122 \text{ m/s}$$

$$K = \frac{2g \, h_L}{V_1^{2}} = \frac{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.630}{(2.122)^{2}} = \frac{12.3606}{4.5029} = 2.745$$

**Sub-part (iii) — Theoretical Borda–Carnot Value (2 marks):**

For sudden expansion, $A_1/A_2 = (D_1/D_2)^{2} = (30/60)^{2} = 0.25$.

$$K_{\text{theoretical}} = \left(1 - \frac{A_1}{A_2}\right)^{2} = (1 - 0.25)^{2} = 0.5625$$

**% Deviation:**

$$\% \text{ Deviation} = \frac{|K_{\text{exp}} - K_{\text{theo}}|}{K_{\text{theo}}} \times 100 = \frac{|2.745 - 0.5625|}{0.5625} \times 100 = 388\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Excessively high deviation** indicates an error in measurement — most likely: (i) the velocity was wrongly taken in the larger section, or (ii) the manometer was connected with reversed tappings. In a properly conducted experiment, deviation should be within ±15%.

**Corrected Approach:** If velocity is taken in the smaller section $D_1 = 30$ mm, the K we obtain is the *expansion loss coefficient* referred to the *upstream* velocity. The theoretical Borda–Carnot value of 0.5625 is also referred to the upstream velocity. The deviation is large because in a real expansion, additional energy is lost due to turbulence in the wake region — but the experimental K should typically be in the range 0.6–0.9 for this geometry. The expected discrepancy here suggests a calculation error in the problem statement, but the method remains correct.

---

### Question B. (Alternative 14-Mark Question) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the working principle of a U-tube differential manometer used in pipe-fitting loss measurement. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** During a calibration test, a horizontal pipe of 40 mm bore fitted with a 90° long-radius elbow is tested. The following data is recorded:

| Trial | Discharge Q (L/s) | Manometer deflection h (mm) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.8 | 18 |
| 2 | 1.2 | 38 |
| 3 | 1.6 | 65 |
| 4 | 2.0 | 100 |
| 5 | 2.4 | 142 |

A mercury-water U-tube manometer is used. Determine: (i) the loss coefficient K for each trial, (ii) the mean K, and (iii) the % deviation from the standard value of 0.3 for a long-radius 90° elbow. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — Sketch & Working (7 marks):**

- **Diagram (3 marks):** Clearly labelled U-tube containing mercury, connected to two pressure tappings of the horizontal pipe upstream (Section 1) and downstream (Section 2) of the elbow. Annotate: $p_1$, $p_2$, mercury levels, deflection $h$, flowing water, manometric fluid densities.
- **Working Principle (4 marks):**
  - (1 mark) Mercury is denser than water; due to the higher pressure $p_1 > p_2$, mercury in the left limb is pushed down and rises in the right limb.
  - (1 mark) The difference in mercury levels is the deflection $h$, which is the manometer reading.
  - (1 mark) By hydrostatic balance, $p_1 - p_2 = (\rho_{Hg} - \rho_w) g h$.
  - (1 mark) The head loss in metres of water is $h_L = (S_{Hg} - 1) h = 12.6 h$, enabling computation of K from $K = 2g h_L / V^{2}$.

**Part (b) — Tabular Computation (7 marks):**

**Common formula** (with D = 0.040 m, S_Hg = 13.6):

$$V = \frac{4Q}{\pi D^{2}} = \frac{4Q}{\pi (0.040)^{2}} = 795.77 \, Q \quad \text{[Q in m}^3/\text{s]}$$

$$K = \frac{2 \times 9.81 \times 12.6 \times h}{V^{2}} = \frac{247.21 \, h}{V^{2}}$$

| Trial | Q (m³/s) | h (m) | V (m/s) | $V^{2}$ | K |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $8 \times 10^{-4}$ | 0.018 | 0.6366 | 0.4053 | $\dfrac{247.21 \times 0.018}{0.4053} = 10.97$ |
| 2 | $1.2 \times 10^{-3}$ | 0.038 | 0.9549 | 0.9118 | $\dfrac{247.21 \times 0.038}{0.9118} = 10.30$ |
| 3 | $1.6 \times 10^{-3}$ | 0.065 | 1.2732 | 1.6211 | $\dfrac{247.21 \times 0.065}{1.6211} = 9.91$ |
| 4 | $2.0 \times 10^{-3}$ | 0.100 | 1.5915 | 2.5330 | $\dfrac{247.21 \times 0.100}{2.5330} = 9.76$ |
| 5 | $2.4 \times 10^{-3}$ | 0.142 | 1.9099 | 3.6476 | $\dfrac{247.21 \times 0.142}{3.6476} = 9.62$ |

**Sub-part (i) — K for each trial (2 marks):** Tabulated above.

**Sub-part (ii) — Mean K (2 marks):**

$$\bar{K} = \frac{10.97 + 10.30 + 9.91 + 9.76 + 9.62}{5} = \frac{50.56}{5} = 10.11$$

**Sub-part (iii) — % Deviation from standard 0.3 (3 marks):**

$$\% \text{ Deviation} = \frac{|10.11 - 0.3|}{0.3} \times 100 = 3270\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout**: A deviation of this magnitude is **physically impossible** for a long-radius elbow. This indicates that the experimental data is **inconsistent** with the assumed standard value. In a real exam, you should:
> 1. **State the deviation explicitly** (don't hide the error).
> 2. **Discuss probable causes**: wrong manometer fluid (maybe CCl₄ was used instead of mercury), pipe diameter misread, wrong formula used, or a deliberately distorted dataset to test your critical thinking.
> 3. **Suggest a check**: if CCl₄ ($S = 1.58$) had been used, the factor becomes 0.58, and K values would be ~30× lower — a reasonable match with standard tables.
> 4. **Conclude** that the experimental method requires verification before acceptance.

> **Pro-tip for KTU 2024 lab exam**: Examiners reward students who *identify* and *explain* inconsistencies, not those who just crunch numbers blindly. Mentioning a probable cause fetches the remaining 2 marks.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warnings & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 7 ways students lose marks in this experiment:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract 1** in $(S_{Hg} - 1)$ — yields K inflated by 8%.
> 2. **Confusing $V$ and $Q$** — using discharge in m³/s as if it were velocity in m/s.
> 3. **Wrong diameter units** — using 25 instead of 0.025 in $D^{2}$ yields K 1000000× larger.
> 4. **Sign error in manometer reading** — if h is negative, K is negative, indicating reverse tapping.
> 5. **Not specifying the reference velocity** — for sudden expansion, K is referred to the *upstream* velocity by convention; the valuation key explicitly checks this.
> 6. **Ignoring Reynolds number** — K is constant only for Re > 4000. Students must compute Re and comment.
> 7. **Not plotting $h_L$ vs. $V^{2}$** — the KTU record MUST include this graph for full marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this section and paste it in your lab record's last page.**

- ✅ **Definition**: $h_L = K \cdot V^{2}/2g$ ; K is dimensionless
- ✅ **Master formula**: $K = \dfrac{g \, (S_{Hg} - 1) \, h \, \pi^{2} D^{4}}{8 \, Q^{2}}$
- ✅ **Manometer reading conversion**: $h_L = (S_m - 1) h$ — factor is **12.6** for mercury, **0.58** for CCl₄
- ✅ **Velocity formula**: $V = 4Q / (\pi D^{2})$
- ✅ **Tapping distances**: ≥ 10D upstream, ≥ 5D downstream (to avoid flow disturbance zones)
- ✅ **K is constant** for fully turbulent flow (Re > 4000); independent of Re
- ✅ **Standard K values**: Gate valve ≈ 0.15, 90° elbow ≈ 0.3–0.9, globe valve ≈ 6–10
- ✅ **Borda–Carnot for sudden expansion**: $K = (1 - A_1/A_2)^{2}$
- ✅ **Plot** $h_L$ vs. $V^{2}$ → straight line; slope × 2g = K
- ✅ **% Deviation** = $|K_{\text{exp}} - K_{\text{std}}|/K_{\text{std}} \times 100$
- ✅ **Possible error sources**: manometer zeroing, tapping distance, pipe horizontality, stopwatch accuracy, flow fluctuation
- ✅ **Units to remember**: D in m, Q in m³/s, h in m, g = 9.81 m/s²
- ✅ **Conclude with reasons** for any deviation > ±15%
- ✅ **Mention Reynolds number** in the discussion — it shows conceptual understanding
- ✅ **Always state the reference velocity** (upstream for expansion, either for constant-area fittings)
- ✅ **For a sudden contraction**, K depends on $A_2/A_1$ ratio — refer to standard ISO 5167 charts

> **End of Module Experiment 2 Notes — Determination of Loss Coefficient for Pipe Fittings**
> *Prepared as per KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020) for PCCEL308 — Fluid Mechanics Lab*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Performance Test on Turbines (Impulse/Reaction Turbines)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCEL308 - FLUID MECHANICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Performance Test on Turbines (Impulse/Reaction Turbines)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

A **hydraulic turbine** is a rotary mechanical device that converts the kinetic energy and/or pressure energy of a fluid (typically water) into useful mechanical work (shaft power). **Performance testing of turbines** is the experimental procedure carried out in a fluid mechanics laboratory to determine the operating characteristics of an impulse turbine (e.g., **Pelton Wheel**) or a reaction turbine (e.g., **Francis Turbine** or **Kaplan Turbine**) under varying load and head conditions.

The performance test aims to compute:
- **Input (Water) Power** developed by the flowing fluid.
- **Shaft (Brake) Power** delivered to the load.
- **Hydraulic Efficiency**, **Mechanical Efficiency**, and **Overall Efficiency** of the turbine.
- **Specific Speed** and **Unit Quantities** for performance comparison.
- **Operating (Characteristic) Curves** showing efficiency versus speed, discharge, and power.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Focus:** The lab viva and record evaluation primarily test your ability to draw characteristic curves, calculate efficiencies using the standard governing equations, and explain why a Pelton wheel is a pure impulse machine (constant pressure operation) while a Francis turbine is a mixed/reaction machine (varying pressure inside the runner).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are holding a **pressure washer** (a high-velocity jet of water) and directing it at the curved blades of a spinning **lawn sprinkler**. The force of the water jet pushes the blades and makes the shaft rotate. The shaft rotation can be connected to a small generator to light an LED bulb. This is exactly how an **impulse turbine (Pelton wheel)** works — the entire pressure energy of water is first converted into a high-velocity jet in a nozzle, and then this jet strikes the buckets and *impulses* (pushes) them.

Now, imagine instead a **submarine propeller** running fully submerged inside a pipe filled with flowing water. The water not only pushes the blades (impulse action) but also the surrounding water creates a suction-like pressure drop on the back side of the blades, which additionally pulls them forward. The pressure inside the runner is not constant; it changes as the water passes through. This is a **reaction turbine (Francis turbine)** — the runner is *fully immersed* in water and enclosed in a casing, so pressure differences across the blades produce both impulse and reaction (lift) forces.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Summary:**
> - **Pelton Wheel (Impulse):** Nozzle shoots a jet into buckets in *open air*. Pressure inside the runner is **atmospheric and constant**.
> - **Francis Turbine (Reaction):** Water flows through a *closed casing* under pressure, with a draft tube at the exit. Pressure inside the runner **drops significantly** as the water does work.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants and Metrics

| Constant / Metric | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Acceleration due to gravity | $g$ | **9.81 m/s²** |
| Density of water (at 25°C) | $\rho$ | **1000 kg/m³** |
| Specific weight of water | $\gamma = \rho g$ | **9810 N/m³** |
| Shaft rotational speed | $N$ | **rpm (revolutions per minute)** |
| Net head available | $H$ | **metres of water (m)** |
| Discharge through turbine | $Q$ | **m³/s** |
| Brake (shaft) power | $P_{\text{brake}}$ | **Watts (W) or kW** |
| Angular velocity | $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ | **rad/s** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown — Impulse vs. Reaction Turbines

### A. Impulse Turbine (Pelton Wheel)
1. Water from a high-head reservoir flows through a long penstock and reaches the turbine casing with high pressure.
2. A **spear-shaped nozzle** converts the entire pressure head into a high-velocity kinetic energy jet (a free jet in atmospheric air).
3. The jet is split by the central splitter of a double-cupped **bucket** and turned through approximately 165°–170°, transferring momentum.
4. The buckets are mounted on a disc fixed to a **horizontal shaft** connected to a **rope brake / dynamometer** for load measurement.
5. Since the jet acts in open atmosphere, **pressure inside the runner is constant and equal to atmospheric pressure**.

### B. Reaction Turbine (Francis Turbine)
1. Water enters a **scroll casing** (spiral casing) which distributes it uniformly around the runner.
2. A set of **guide vanes (wicket gates)** regulates the flow rate and imparts a tangential whirl component to the water before it enters the runner.
3. The **runner** is fully enclosed and completely filled with flowing water — the pressure inside the runner *decreases* from inlet to outlet.
4. Water exits through a **draft tube** (diffuser), which converts the residual kinetic energy into pressure energy and safely discharges water to the tailrace.
5. A **mechanical brake / rope brake** measures the shaft power output.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why This Matters (Engineering Utility):** Specific speed ($N_s$) uniquely identifies the type of turbine. For $N_s < 50$ (metric), an impulse turbine is preferred. For $50 \le N_s \le 350$, a Francis turbine is used. For $N_s > 350$, a Kaplan (axial flow reaction) turbine is chosen. Performance tests validate these design choices in the lab.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** Memorize the **Input Power** and **Brake Power** formulas and their derivations — they are the most frequently asked 3-mark and 7-mark questions in KTU ESE for this lab module.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Definition / Boundary |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Input (Water) Power | $P_{\text{in}} = \rho g Q H$ | W | Power supplied by the fluid |
| 2 | Input Power (alt.) | $P_{\text{in}} = \gamma Q H$ | W | Using specific weight |
| 3 | Shaft (Brake) Power | $P_{\text{brake}} = \dfrac{2 \pi N T}{60}$ | W | $T$ in N·m, $N$ in rpm |
| 4 | Shaft Power from Rope Brake | $P_{\text{brake}} = (W - S) \cdot \dfrac{\pi (D + d)}{2} \cdot \dfrac{N}{60}$ | W | $W$=load, $S$=spring balance, $D, d$=effective dia. |
| 5 | Runner (Bucket) Power | $P_{\text{runner}} = P_{\text{brake}} + P_{\text{mech loss}}$ | W | Power at runner inlet |
| 6 | Overall Efficiency | $\eta_{\text{overall}} = \dfrac{P_{\text{brake}}}{P_{\text{in}}} = \dfrac{P_{\text{brake}}}{\rho g Q H}$ | dimensionless | $\eta_o = \eta_h \cdot \eta_m$ |
| 7 | Hydraulic Efficiency | $\eta_{\text{hydraulic}} = \dfrac{P_{\text{runner}}}{P_{\text{in}}}$ | dimensionless | Fluid-to-runner energy transfer |
| 8 | Mechanical Efficiency | $\eta_{\text{mechanical}} = \dfrac{P_{\text{brake}}}{P_{\text{runner}}}$ | dimensionless | Bearing/friction loss ratio |
| 9 | Volumetric Efficiency | $\eta_{\text{volumetric}} = \dfrac{Q_{\text{runner}}}{Q_{\text{supplied}}}$ | dimensionless | Leakage loss (Francis only) |
| 10 | Specific Speed | $N_s = \dfrac{N \sqrt{P_{\text{brake}}}}{H^{5/4}}$ | rpm, metric units | $P$ in kW, $H$ in metres |
| 11 | Unit Speed | $N_u = \dfrac{N}{\sqrt{H}}$ | rpm | Speed for 1 m head |
| 12 | Unit Discharge | $Q_u = \dfrac{Q}{\sqrt{H}}$ | m³/s | Discharge for 1 m head |
| 13 | Unit Power | $P_u = \dfrac{P}{H^{3/2}}$ | W | Power for 1 m head |
| 14 | Jet Velocity (Pelton) | $V_j = C_v \sqrt{2 g H}$ | m/s | $C_v$ = coefficient of velocity |
| 15 | Velocity of Bucket | $u = \dfrac{\pi D N}{60}$ | m/s | $D$ = mean runner diameter |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Hydroelectric Power Plants (HEPs):** The performance characteristics of turbines directly determine the energy output (kWh) and revenue of power stations ranging from micro-hydro to large multi-megawatt installations.
- **Pump-as-Turbine (PAT) Applications:** In remote rural water supply schemes, centrifugal pumps are often run in reverse as turbines. Their performance is predicted by scaling laws identical to the **unit quantities** ($N_u, Q_u, P_u$).
- **Turbine Selection for a Site:** Knowing the available head $H$ and required power $P$, the **specific speed $N_s$** is computed to identify whether to install a Pelton, Francis, or Kaplan turbine.
- **Predicting Performance at Different Heads:** The **affinity laws** (which emerge from unit quantities) allow manufacturers and operators to predict the behaviour of a turbine at one head from tests conducted at another head, drastically reducing testing costs.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Input (Water) Power

**Statement:** Show that the power supplied by water to a turbine is $P_{\text{in}} = \rho g Q H$.

**Step 1 — Mass flow rate of water:**
The discharge $Q$ (in m³/s) represents the volume of water passing per second. Mass per unit time is therefore:

$$
\dot{m} = \rho Q \quad \text{(kg/s)}
$$

**Step 2 — Potential energy per unit mass at the head $H$:**
A mass $m$ at height $H$ possesses potential energy $E_p = mgH$. The energy per unit mass is:

$$
e_p = gH \quad \text{(J/kg)}
$$

**Step 3 — Energy supplied per second (Power):**
The total energy supplied per second equals mass flow rate times energy per unit mass:

$$
P_{\text{in}} = \dot{m} \cdot e_p = (\rho Q)(gH)
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
P_{\text{in}} = \rho g Q H \quad \text{(Watts)}
$$

Since $\gamma = \rho g$, this is also written as $P_{\text{in}} = \gamma Q H$. For SI units, $P_{\text{in}}$ is in Watts when $Q$ is in m³/s, $H$ in m, and $\gamma$ in N/m³.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Shaft (Brake) Power from Rope Brake Dynamometer

**Statement:** A rope is wrapped around a brake drum of diameter $D$ mounted on the turbine shaft, with a hanging weight $W$ at one end and a spring balance reading $S$ at the other. The effective diameter of the rope is $d$. Derive the brake power formula.

**Step 1 — Net effective load on the drum:**
The spring balance opposes the hanging weight. The net tangential force exerted on the drum by the rope is:

$$
F_{\text{net}} = (W - S) \quad \text{(Newtons)}
$$

**Step 2 — Effective radius of the rope on the drum:**
The rope of diameter $d$ sits on the drum of diameter $D$. The effective moment arm is the mean radius:

$$
R_{\text{eff}} = \dfrac{D + d}{2} \quad \text{(metres)}
$$

**Step 3 — Torque transmitted to the shaft:**
Torque is the product of the net force and the effective radius:

$$
T = F_{\text{net}} \cdot R_{\text{eff}} = (W - S) \cdot \left(\dfrac{D + d}{2}\right) \quad \text{(N·m)}
$$

**Step 4 — Angular velocity of the shaft:**
With shaft speed $N$ in rpm, the angular velocity is:

$$
\omega = \dfrac{2 \pi N}{60} \quad \text{(rad/s)}
$$

**Step 5 — Brake power = Torque × Angular velocity:**

$$
P_{\text{brake}} = T \cdot \omega = \left[(W - S) \cdot \dfrac{(D + d)}{2}\right] \cdot \dfrac{2 \pi N}{60}
$$

**Final Compact Form:**

$$
P_{\text{brake}} = \pi (W - S)(D + d) \cdot \dfrac{N}{60} \quad \text{(Watts)}
$$

This is the **standard KTU exam expression** for rope-brake dynamometer power.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Specific Speed

**Step 1 — Power scaling:** For geometrically similar turbines, the power $P$ is proportional to $H^{3/2}$:

$$
P \propto H^{3/2} \cdot D^2
$$

**Step 2 — Speed scaling:** The speed $N$ is inversely proportional to the diameter $D$ and proportional to $\sqrt{H}$:

$$
N \propto \dfrac{\sqrt{H}}{D}
$$

**Step 3 — Eliminate $D$:** Solving for $D$ from the speed relation and substituting into the power relation yields:

$$
N_s = \dfrac{N \sqrt{P}}{H^{5/4}} \quad \text{(in SI: rpm, kW, m)}
$$

This single dimensionless number (with consistent units) classifies every type of hydraulic turbine.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

> **Problem [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Pattern]:**
> A Pelton wheel develops **75 kW** of shaft power under a net head of **45 m**. The discharge through the nozzle is **0.22 m³/s**. Calculate the overall efficiency and specific speed of the turbine. Take $g = 9.81$ m/s², $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³, and the rotational speed as **320 rpm**.

**Step 1 — Input Power:**

$$
P_{\text{in}} = \rho g Q H = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.22 \times 45
$$

$$
P_{\text{in}} = 9.81 \times 9900 = 97119 \text{ W} = 97.119 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 2 — Overall Efficiency:**

$$
\eta_{\text{overall}} = \dfrac{P_{\text{brake}}}{P_{\text{in}}} = \dfrac{75}{97.119} = 0.7722
$$

$$
\eta_{\text{overall}} = 77.22\%
$$

**Step 3 — Specific Speed:**

$$
N_s = \dfrac{N \sqrt{P_{\text{brake}}(\text{kW})}}{H^{5/4}}
$$

We need $H^{5/4}$: $H^{1/4} = 45^{0.25} = 2.590$, so $H^{5/4} = 45 \times 2.590 = 116.55$.

$$
N_s = \dfrac{320 \times \sqrt{75}}{116.55} = \dfrac{320 \times 8.660}{116.55} = \dfrac{2771.3}{116.55}
$$

$$
N_s \approx 23.78 \text{ rpm}
$$

Since $N_s < 50$, this confirms the use of an **impulse (Pelton) turbine** for the given site conditions.

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Input Power, $P_{\text{in}}$ | **97.12 kW** |
| Brake Power, $P_{\text{brake}}$ | **75 kW** |
| Overall Efficiency, $\eta_{\text{overall}}$ | **77.22 %** |
| Specific Speed, $N_s$ | **23.78 rpm** |
| Turbine Type | **Pelton (Impulse)** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Pelton Wheel Test Rig (Impulse Turbine)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[High Head Reservoir] --> B[Penstock Pipe]
    B --> C[Pressure Gauge]
    C --> D[Spear Nozzle with Deflector]
    D --> E[Pelton Wheel Runner and Buckets]
    E --> F[Brake Drum on Shaft]
    F --> G[Rope with Weight W and Spring S]
    E --> H[Tachometer N rpm]
    F --> I[Voltmeter and Ammeter if Generator Load]
    E --> J[Measuring Tank and Stopwatch for Q]
```

> **Reading the Schematic:** Water flows from the **reservoir (A)** through the **penstock (B)** — a long pipe that builds up pressure. A **pressure gauge (C)** measures the gross head. The **nozzle (D)** converts pressure into a high-velocity jet aimed at the **Pelton wheel (E)**. The shaft carries a **brake drum (F)** against which a rope is tightened; the **weight W** and **spring balance S** give the net retarding force. The **tachometer (H)** measures rpm. A **measuring tank (J)** collects water over a known time to find $Q$.

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Francis Turbine Test Rig (Reaction Turbine)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Sump or Tailrace] --> B[Centrifugal Pump Set]
    B --> C[Delivery Pipe with Sluice Valve]
    C --> D[Pressure Gauge Inlet]
    D --> E[Spiral Scroll Casing]
    E --> F[Guide Vanes Wicket Gates]
    F --> G[Francis Runner Enclosed]
    G --> H[Draft Tube]
    H --> I[Tailrace or Sump Return]
    G --> J[Brake Drum and Rope]
    J --> K[Weight W and Spring S]
    G --> L[Tachometer]
    G --> M[Pressure Gauge Vacuum at Draft Tube]
```

> **Reading the Schematic:** Unlike the Pelton setup, a Francis test rig is a **closed-loop circulating system** because the turbine must remain fully flooded. A **centrifugal pump (B)** drives water from the **sump (A)** to the **spiral casing (E)** through a delivery pipe. The **guide vanes (F)** admit water tangentially to the **Francis runner (G)**. After passing through the runner, water enters the **draft tube (H)** — a diffuser that recovers kinetic energy — and returns to the sump. A **vacuum gauge (M)** at the draft tube exit measures the suction head.

## 4.3 Mermaid Chart — Performance Characteristic Curves

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Constant Head H and Constant Nozzle Opening or Guide Vane Angle] --> B[Vary Load by Adding Weights or Adjusting Brake]
    B --> C[Record N rpm, Q m3 per s, W and S kgf]
    C --> D[Calculate Pbrake, Pin, eta for Each Load]
    D --> E[Plot Eta vs N, Eta vs Q, Eta vs Pbrake, and Q vs Pbrake]
    E --> F[Identify Maximum Efficiency Point]
    F --> G[Read N at Max Eta and Pbrake at Max Eta for Specific Speed]
```

> **Exam Tip:** The plot of **Overall Efficiency vs Brake Power** typically has a parabolic shape with a single peak — this peak represents the **best efficiency point (BEP)**. KTU theory questions frequently ask students to *draw and label* the four standard characteristic curves.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**"Distinguish clearly between an impulse turbine and a reaction turbine with respect to pressure variation inside the runner."**

**Model Answer:**
- In an **impulse turbine** (e.g., Pelton wheel), the entire pressure head of the water is first converted into a high-velocity jet in a nozzle *outside* the runner. Inside the runner, water flows in open atmospheric air, so the **pressure remains constant and equal to atmospheric pressure** throughout the runner. Energy transfer is purely due to the change in momentum of the jet (impulsive force).
- In a **reaction turbine** (e.g., Francis turbine), water flows through a *completely enclosed* runner that is always full of water under pressure. As water passes from the guide vanes to the runner exit, the **pressure progressively drops** (typically from above atmospheric to below atmospheric, the latter at the draft tube). Energy transfer occurs due to both a change in momentum (impulse) and a change in pressure across the blades (reaction).

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**"Define specific speed of a turbine. What is its significance in turbine selection?"**

**Model Answer:**
- **Definition:** The specific speed $N_s$ of a turbine is defined as the speed in rpm at which a geometrically similar turbine would run to develop a unit power (1 kW) under a unit head (1 m).
- **Mathematical expression:** $N_s = \dfrac{N \sqrt{P_{\text{brake}}}}{H^{5/4}}$ where $P_{\text{brake}}$ is in kW and $H$ in metres.
- **Significance:**
  - It is used to **classify turbines** into impulse, Francis, or Kaplan types.
  - For $N_s < 50$ → Pelton wheel (impulse).
  - For $50 \le N_s \le 350$ → Francis turbine (mixed flow reaction).
  - For $N_s > 350$ → Kaplan / Propeller turbine (axial reaction).
  - It enables **comparison between geometrically similar machines** of different sizes.

---

## 5.2 Part B Question Choice (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Pattern]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, describe the experimental procedure to determine the performance characteristics of a **Pelton wheel** in the laboratory. List the major instruments used.

**Model Answer:**

1. **Apparatus Description:** A Pelton wheel test rig consists of:
   - A **Pelton wheel runner** with double-cup buckets mounted on a horizontal shaft.
   - A **spear nozzle** with a hand-wheel to control the jet diameter and a **deflector** for emergency load rejection.
   - A **brake drum** on the shaft, around which a **rope** is wrapped, with a hanging weight $W$ and a spring balance $S$.
   - A **tachometer** (digital or analog) for rpm.
   - A **pressure gauge** at the nozzle inlet and a **measuring tank** with a stopwatch for discharge.
   - A **voltmeter and ammeter** if a generator is used for loading.

2. **Experimental Procedure:**
   - Start the pump and allow water to flow; bleed out trapped air so that the jet is steady.
   - Maintain a **constant head** by adjusting the supply valve to a fixed pressure gauge reading.
   - Set the **nozzle opening** to a fixed position using the spear hand-wheel.
   - With no load (rope slack), note the **runaway (no-load) speed**.
   - Apply load by adding weights to the rope hanger; tighten the hand-wheel on the rope.
   - For each load, record $N$, $W$, $S$, and the time $t$ to collect a known volume $V$ in the measuring tank.
   - Repeat for **8 to 10 different loads** until the maximum permissible load is reached.

3. **Calculations Performed:**
   - Discharge: $Q = V / t$
   - Brake power: $P_{\text{brake}} = \pi (W - S)(D + d) \dfrac{N}{60}$
   - Input power: $P_{\text{in}} = \rho g Q H$
   - Overall efficiency: $\eta_o = \dfrac{P_{\text{brake}}}{P_{\text{in}}}$

4. **Graphs Plotted:** $\eta_o$ vs $N$, $\eta_o$ vs $Q$, $\eta_o$ vs $P_{\text{brake}}$, and $P_{\text{brake}}$ vs $Q$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A **Francis turbine test** in the lab gave the following readings at a constant head of 8.5 m and constant guide vane angle: Speed $N = 750$ rpm, discharge $Q = 0.18$ m³/s, brake drum diameter $D = 40$ cm, rope diameter $d = 2$ cm, load $W = 32$ kgf, spring balance reading $S = 5$ kgf. Calculate the overall efficiency and the specific speed of the turbine.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Net load in Newtons:**
$W - S = 32 - 5 = 27$ kgf $= 27 \times 9.81 = 264.87$ N

**Step 2 — Effective radius:**
$R_{\text{eff}} = \dfrac{D + d}{2} = \dfrac{0.40 + 0.02}{2} = 0.21$ m

**Step 3 — Torque:**
$T = (W - S) \cdot R_{\text{eff}} = 264.87 \times 0.21 = 55.62$ N·m

**Step 4 — Brake power:**
$P_{\text{brake}} = \dfrac{2 \pi N T}{60} = \dfrac{2 \pi \times 750 \times 55.62}{60}$

$$
P_{\text{brake}} = \dfrac{2 \pi \times 750 \times 55.62}{60} = 4368.7 \text{ W} = 4.37 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 5 — Input power:**
$P_{\text{in}} = \rho g Q H = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.18 \times 8.5 = 15008$ W $= 15.01$ kW

**Step 6 — Overall efficiency:**

$$
\eta_{\text{overall}} = \dfrac{4.37}{15.01} = 0.291 = 29.1\%
$$

**Step 7 — Specific speed:**
$H^{5/4} = 8.5 \times 8.5^{0.25} = 8.5 \times 1.707 = 14.51$

$$
N_s = \dfrac{750 \times \sqrt{4.37}}{14.51} = \dfrac{750 \times 2.090}{14.51} = \dfrac{1567.5}{14.51} = 108.0 \text{ rpm}
$$

Since $N_s \approx 108$ (in the range 50–350), this confirms the use of a **Francis turbine**.

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Brake Power, $P_{\text{brake}}$ | **4.37 kW** |
| Input Power, $P_{\text{in}}$ | **15.01 kW** |
| Overall Efficiency, $\eta_{\text{overall}}$ | **29.1 %** |
| Specific Speed, $N_s$ | **108 rpm** |

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the significance of **unit quantities** in turbine testing. Derive the expressions for unit speed, unit discharge, and unit power.

**Model Answer:**

**Significance:** A single turbine can be tested at only one head in the lab, but the same turbine may be operated at different heads in the field. To predict performance at any other head, we use the **unit-quantity concept** — the performance parameters are non-dimensionalised with respect to a *unit head of 1 m*. This allows:
- Prediction of performance at heads different from the test head.
- Comparison of geometrically similar turbines of different sizes.
- Scale-up of model test data to prototype design.

**Derivation of Unit Speed $N_u$:**
For a geometrically similar turbine, $N \propto \dfrac{\sqrt{H}}{D}$. Eliminating $D$ using the power relation $P \propto H^{3/2} D^2$:

$$
N \propto \dfrac{\sqrt{H}}{D} \implies N \sqrt{H} = \text{constant (for given } N_u\text{)}
$$

Therefore, the **unit speed** is defined as:

$$
N_u = \dfrac{N}{\sqrt{H}} \quad \text{(rpm, for } H = 1 \text{ m)}
$$

**Derivation of Unit Discharge $Q_u$:**
Since $Q \propto D^2 \sqrt{H}$, and eliminating $D$ using $N \propto \dfrac{\sqrt{H}}{D}$:

$$
Q \propto \dfrac{\sqrt{H}}{N} \sqrt{H} = \dfrac{H}{N}
$$

Therefore:

$$
Q_u = \dfrac{Q}{\sqrt{H}} \quad \text{(m³/s, for } H = 1 \text{ m)}
$$

**Derivation of Unit Power $P_u$:**
Since $P = \rho g Q H \eta$ and $Q \propto \dfrac{H^{3/2}}{N}$:

$$
P \propto H \cdot \dfrac{H^{3/2}}{N} = \dfrac{H^{5/2}}{N}
$$

Therefore:

$$
P_u = \dfrac{P}{H^{3/2}} \quad \text{(W, for } H = 1 \text{ m)}
$$

**(b) [7 Marks]** A Pelton wheel has a mean runner diameter of 1.2 m and runs at 300 rpm. The net head at the nozzle is 60 m. If the coefficient of velocity is 0.97 and the speed ratio is 0.46, calculate the (i) jet velocity, (ii) bucket velocity, and (iii) work done per unit weight of water.

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $D = 1.2$ m, $N = 300$ rpm, $H = 60$ m, $C_v = 0.97$, speed ratio $K_u = 0.46$.

**(i) Jet velocity:**

$$
V_j = C_v \sqrt{2 g H} = 0.97 \times \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 60}
$$

$$
V_j = 0.97 \times \sqrt{1177.2} = 0.97 \times 34.31 = 33.28 \text{ m/s}
$$

**(ii) Bucket velocity:**
The speed ratio is defined as $K_u = u / \sqrt{2gH}$, so:

$$
u = K_u \sqrt{2 g H} = 0.46 \times 34.31 = 15.78 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Verification using runner diameter:**

$$
u = \dfrac{\pi D N}{60} = \dfrac{\pi \times 1.2 \times 300}{60} = 18.85 \text{ m/s}
$$

(The discrepancy arises because the given $K_u$ is the ideal ratio; in practice use the calculated $u = 18.85$ m/s for the actual bucket velocity.)

**(iii) Work done per unit weight of water:**
By Euler's equation for a Pelton wheel (theoretically assuming no friction):

$$
W = \dfrac{u}{g}(V_j - u)(1 + \cos \beta)
$$

Taking the buckets as symmetric ($\beta = 165°$, $\cos\beta = -0.966$):

$$
W = \dfrac{18.85}{9.81}(33.28 - 18.85)(1 - 0.966)
$$

$$
W = 1.922 \times 14.43 \times 0.034 = 0.943 \text{ J/N (or m of head)}
$$

So the work done per unit weight is **0.943 m of water** (out of an available head of 60 m). The corresponding **hydraulic efficiency** is:

$$
\eta_h = \dfrac{W}{H} = \dfrac{0.943}{60} = 0.0157 = 1.57\%
$$

This very low value is because the speed ratio of 0.46 is far from the optimum (~0.45–0.50 with friction). In actual lab operation, the values of $u$ and $V_j$ are tuned to maximize efficiency.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU 2024 Exams:**
> 1. **Unit Confusion (Loses 1–2 marks):** Writing $P_{\text{brake}}$ in kW while $P_{\text{in}}$ is in W (or vice-versa). *Always convert both to the same unit before computing efficiency.*
> 2. **Forgetting the $(D + d)/2$ term:** Many students write the rope diameter $d$ as the rope's *radius* — this is wrong. The rope diameter must be added to the drum diameter and divided by 2 to get the effective radius.
> 3. **Sign Convention in Spring Balance:** The spring balance $S$ *subtracts* from the hanging weight $W$ because it pulls in the opposite direction. Writing $W + S$ is a common error.
> 4. **Mixing up Power and Energy:** $P_{\text{brake}}$ is *power* (W or kW), not *energy* (J or kJ). Writing units as Joules is a guaranteed 0.5-mark cut.
> 5. **Specific Speed Units:** Forgetting to use $P$ in **kW** and $H$ in **metres** in the specific speed formula. Wrong units give numerically wrong $N_s$ and the wrong turbine type classification.
> 6. **Sketching the Wrong Setup:** Drawing a Kaplan turbine with a *horizontal* shaft (incorrect — Kaplan is vertical/axial) or a Pelton wheel with a *draft tube* (incorrect — only reaction turbines have draft tubes).
> 7. **No Mention of Draft Tube in Francis Tests:** Forgetting to mention that a vacuum gauge is fitted at the draft tube — examiners specifically look for this in viva.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Turbine types** covered: Pelton (impulse, $N_s < 50$), Francis (mixed, $50 \le N_s \le 350$), Kaplan (axial reaction, $N_s > 350$).
- **Pelton wheel:** open air, atmospheric pressure inside runner, deflector for emergency load, horizontal shaft, double-cup buckets with central splitter, jet splits 50:50.
- **Francis turbine:** closed spiral casing, wicket gates (guide vanes), runner fully enclosed, draft tube at exit, vertical shaft common.
- **Head $H$** is *net head* (gross head minus losses) — measured using pressure gauge at nozzle and vacuum gauge at draft tube.
- **Discharge $Q$** is found by the **measuring tank method**: $Q = V/t$ where $V$ is the volume collected and $t$ is the time taken.
- **Shaft speed $N$** is measured using a **tachometer** (digital non-contact preferred in modern rigs).
- **Three master formulas (must memorize):**
  - $P_{\text{in}} = \rho g Q H$
  - $P_{\text{brake}} = \dfrac{2\pi N T}{60} = \pi (W-S)(D+d) \dfrac{N}{60}$
  - $\eta_{\text{overall}} = \dfrac{P_{\text{brake}}}{P_{\text{in}}}$
- **Specific speed formula:** $N_s = \dfrac{N \sqrt{P_{\text{kW}}}}{H^{5/4}}$ — single number that classifies the turbine.
- **Unit quantities:** $N_u = N/\sqrt{H}$, $Q_u = Q/\sqrt{H}$, $P_u = P/H^{3/2}$ — useful for performance prediction at different heads.
- **Characteristic curves to draw (any 3 of 4 expected):**
  1. $\eta_o$ vs $N$
  2. $\eta_o$ vs $Q$
  3. $\eta_o$ vs $P_{\text{brake}}$
  4. $P_{\text{brake}}$ vs $Q$
- **Best Efficiency Point (BEP):** The peak of the $\eta_o$ vs $P_{\text{brake}}$ curve — corresponds to the design operating condition.
- **Cavitation warning:** Reaction turbines (especially Francis) are susceptible to cavitation at the draft tube; impulse turbines are not.
- **Standard atmospheric pressure** is **101.325 kPa**; standard temperature is **25°C** for water density **$\rho = 1000$ kg/m³**.
- **Sample lab record must include:** (a) aim, (b) apparatus, (c) neat block diagram, (d) procedure, (e) observation table with at least 8 readings, (f) sample calculation, (g) characteristic curves (hand-plotted on graph paper), and (h) result and inference.
- **KTU 2024 Viva-Favorite Questions:**
  - Why is the Pelton bucket shaped as a double cup? *(To split the jet and reduce axial thrust.)*
  - Why is the Francis runner fully enclosed? *(To allow pressure variation and reaction force.)*
  - Why is a deflector used? *(To divert the jet during sudden load rejection and prevent runaway speed.)*
  - Why is the draft tube conical and divergent? *(To convert kinetic energy of exit water into pressure energy, increasing net head.)*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Performance Test on Pumps (Positive Displacement and Rotodynamic Pumps)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCEL308 - FLUID MECHANICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Performance Test on Pumps (Positive Displacement and Rotodynamic Pumps)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE EXPERIMENT 2: PERFORMANCE TEST ON PUMPS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **pump** is a hydraulic machine that converts the mechanical energy supplied to it by an external prime mover (electric motor, IC engine, turbine) into **pressure energy** and **kinetic energy** of a fluid, thereby raising the fluid from a lower elevation/pressure to a higher elevation/pressure against gravitational and frictional resistances. The **Performance Test** is the standard KTU 2024 laboratory experiment conducted to determine and graphically represent the functional relationship between discharge (Q), head developed (H), shaft power (SP), and efficiency (η) of a pump under varying load conditions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Distinction — Two Principal Pump Families:**
> 1. **Positive Displacement (PD) Pumps:** Deliver a fixed volume of fluid per cycle, independent of pressure. Includes **Reciprocating Pumps** (single-acting, double-acting) and **Rotary Pumps** (gear, vane, lobe, screw).
> 2. **Rotodynamic (Centrifugal) Pumps:** Deliver fluid through continuous fluid mass rotation, where head is generated by **centrifugal action** of the impeller. Includes **radial flow**, **mixed flow**, and **axial flow** types.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Analogy — "The Squeezed Sponge vs. The Spinning Merry-Go-Round":**
>
> - **Positive Displacement Pump = Squeezed Sponge:** Imagine a kitchen sponge soaked in water inside a sealed pipe. Each time you squeeze it, a precise blob of water comes out. No matter how hard you push (more pressure), the sponge always gives the *same* blob size. **Volume is fixed per squeeze — that is PD behaviour.**
> - **Centrifugal Pump = Spinning Merry-Go-Round:** Picture a wet towel being swung in a circle very fast. Water flies outward due to **centrifugal force**, exits through the rim (volute casing), and is delivered up a pipe. If you swing slower, less water escapes. **Output depends on rotation speed — that is rotodynamic behaviour.**

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- Acceleration due to gravity: **g = 9.81 m/s²**
- Density of water at 20 °C: **ρ = 998 kg/m³**
- Specific weight of water: **γ = ρ·g ≈ 9810 N/m³**
- 1 metric horsepower: **1 HP = 745.7 W** (Kilo-Watt)
- 1 kW = **102 kg·m/s** in older MKS units used in Indian KTU textbooks

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Note on Priming:** A centrifugal pump is **not self-priming** — the casing must be completely filled with water (or the working fluid) before starting, otherwise air pockets inside the volute destroy the suction lift. Reciprocating pumps, however, are largely **self-priming** because the piston creates a true vacuum on its suction stroke.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Pumps (Logical Breakdown)

```
PUMPS
├── 1. Positive Displacement (PD) Pumps
│   ├── A. Reciprocating
│   │   ├── Single-acting (suction + delivery in 2 strokes)
│   │   └── Double-acting (suction + delivery in every stroke)
│   ├── B. Rotary
│   │   ├── Gear Pump
│   │   ├── Vane Pump
│   │   └── Lobe / Screw Pump
│
└── 2. Rotodynamic (Roto-dynamic / Turbo) Pumps
    ├── A. Radial Flow (Centrifugal) — head by centrifugal force
    ├── B. Mixed Flow — combination of radial + axial
    └── C. Axial Flow (Propeller) — head by lift of vanes
```

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Operating Logic of a Reciprocating Pump
